When sentencing for sedition offences committed after the National Security Law came into force and while the defendant was on bail, the court must impose deterrent sentences that reflect the defendant's culpability and serve the public interest, while also considering the totality principle to avoid an unduly crushing punishment.

relationships.description: The present judgment notes that the Judge's ruling on jurisdiction for sedition offences differs from the later District Court judgment in Chan Tai Sum, which held the District Court had jurisdiction.

relationships.target_case_name: Fei Yi Ming & Another v the Crown
relationships.description: The judgment cites Fei Yi Ming regarding the historical procedure for prosecuting sedition and the rejection of the argument that incitement to violence was not a necessary element.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. OUSAINOU TOURAY
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and M Poon JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court may quash a conviction based on a 'lurking doubt' about the safety of the verdict, but this is a high threshold applicable only in very rare cases where the evidence against the defendant is weak.

When directing a jury on the mental element (mens rea) for a crime of specific intent, the judge should generally avoid elaborating on or paraphrasing the meaning of intent, and instead leave it to the jury's common sense to decide, unless further explanation is strictly necessary to prevent misunderstanding.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HO HIU LONG
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Hostile intent is only an element of the offence of assault, not an element of the offence of battery.
When considering whether a defendant held an honest but mistaken belief, the reasonableness of that belief is a relevant factor, but it is not a strict requirement that the belief must be reasonable.
The mental element for battery requires the defendant to have intentionally or recklessly inflicted unlawful force or violence on another person.

principles.principle_label: Hostility as a factor, not an element
principles.paraphrase_en: Hostility is not a necessary element of the offences of assault or battery, but it is a factor the court may consider.
A defendant acts recklessly for the purpose of battery if they anticipate that their act would cause harm (such as pain) to another person but nevertheless take the risk of proceeding.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows Ho Po Chun in recognizing that hostile intent applies only to assault, not battery.
The judgment cites Chan Tak Cheung as the source of the principle on hostile intent discussed in Ho Po Chun.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Ng Man Yuen Avery
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that reasonableness is a factor in assessing an honest but mistaken belief.
The judgment cites this case for the principle that hostility is a factor, not an element, of assault or battery.
The judgment applies the legal principle for proving recklessness as illustrated in this case.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN CHEUK HEI
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Woodcock in Court

principles.principle_label: Definition of battery and unlawful force
principles.paraphrase_en: Any physical contact with another person, no matter how minor, can constitute a battery, as an individual's body is inviolate and protected from interference by others.

principles.principle_label: Recklessness and mental condition in assault
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's mental condition, such as ADHD, may be relevant in determining whether they acted recklessly in inflicting unlawful personal violence.

principles.principle_label: Consistency in factual findings for recklessness
principles.paraphrase_en: A court's factual findings regarding a defendant's recklessness must be logically consistent across different instances of alleged unlawful touching within the same incident.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Collins v Wilcock for the principle that even slight touching can amount to a battery, though it notes that reasonable contact to attract attention may not be unlawful.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SHAHID MOHAMMAD SULEMAN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An application to adjourn an appeal hearing to arrange private legal representation may be refused where the hearing is only to determine whether reasonably arguable grounds of appeal exist, not the substantive appeal itself, and where the applicant retains the ability to arrange a lawyer for any subsequent appeal.

The absence of a defendant's DNA from a complainant's fingernail scrapings may have little or no probative value on the issue of consent where sexual intercourse itself is not disputed, as finding one's own DNA in one's fingernails is considered normal.

A judge's explanatory remarks to a jury about the estimated timing for concluding a summing-up and delivering a verdict, made to provide context for procedural scheduling, do not necessarily constitute improper pressure or influence on the jury's deliberations.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YUEN WAI KIT
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Casewell

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWONG CHI SING
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Casewell

principles.principle_label: Arson - recklessness as to endangerment of life
principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits the offence of arson when they intentionally or recklessly destroy or damage property by fire, and are reckless as to whether the life of another would be endangered.
A defendant who offers a guilty plea at an early stage is entitled to a full one-third discount from the sentencing starting point.
In sentencing for arson, the court may obtain psychiatric reports, as the offence often arises from psychiatric or psychological problems, to assess the defendant's mental condition and risk.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YIP KA YU
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A judicial direction regarding the defendant's honest but mistaken belief in the complainant's consent is not required in every rape case. It is only necessary when the evidence, considered as a whole, leaves room for the possibility that the defendant held such a belief.

principles.principle_label: Prosecution burden for belief in consent
principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution bears the burden of proving, beyond reasonable doubt, that the defendant did not genuinely believe the complainant was consenting. The defendant does not need to prove they held such a belief.
For a conviction of rape, the prosecution must prove the defendant either knew the complainant was not consenting to sexual intercourse, or was reckless as to whether she was consenting.
The element of knowledge that the complainant was not consenting can be proven by inference from the facts established by the prosecution, without requiring a direct admission from the defendant.
A defendant is reckless as to consent if they realized there was a risk the complainant was not consenting and proceeded with sexual intercourse regardless.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Morgan as the origin of the honest belief direction in sexual offences.
The judgment applies the principle from Adkins that an honest belief direction is only necessary where evidence leaves room for that possibility.
The judgment applies the same principle from Haughian regarding the conditional necessity of an honest belief direction.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Lee Sze Lung and Anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for its examination of authorities on the honest belief defence.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheung Moon Tong and Another v The Queen
relationships.description: The judgment applies the holding that a Morgan direction is not necessary in every case but only when the evidence supports the possibility of a belief in consent.
The judgment applies the principle that the necessity of a genuine belief direction depends on the circumstances and evidence of the specific case.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOI KWAN KIN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge M Chow

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for non-domestic burglary
principles.paraphrase_en: The established sentencing starting point for non-domestic burglary is two and a half years' imprisonment.

The conventional sentencing starting point for burglary can be reduced where the offender is an opportunistic burglar, similar to a sneak thief who enters an open premises to steal readily available items.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Chow Chak Man and Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case in the context of mitigation, noting it involved a sentence substitution to a Community Service Order for a defendant who played a minor role.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that the conventional sentencing starting point for burglary can be lowered for an opportunistic burglar.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v CHENG CHUN WAI
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Amy Chan

principles.paraphrase_en: For arson, recklessness is established when the defendant, being an adult of normal intelligence, is aware of a risk of property damage and, in the circumstances known to them, it is unreasonable to take that risk.

A defendant's ability to operate a business of substantial scale can be evidence of their normal intelligence and capacity to understand the difference between right and wrong and the consequences of their actions.
A conviction requires the prosecution to prove every element of the charge beyond reasonable doubt.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WRIGHT JAMES MICHAEL
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Casewell

principles.paraphrase_en: A conviction for wounding can be based on recklessness rather than requiring proof of specific intent to cause the wound.
When the evidence is largely agreed, the court's role may be confined to determining specific legal issues, such as the mental state of recklessness.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YIP KA YU
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and Anthea Pang JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Direction on honest belief in consent
principles.paraphrase_en: A jury direction regarding an honest but mistaken belief in consent is only required when the evidence provides a foundation for the possibility of such a genuine belief, regardless of whether the evidence comes from the prosecution or the defence.

The mental element for rape requires proof that the accused either knew the complainant did not consent or was reckless as to whether she consented. Recklessness exists if the accused foresaw the risk of non-consent but proceeded regardless.

A genuine, albeit mistaken or unreasonable, belief that the complainant was consenting is a complete defence to rape. The prosecution bears the burden of disproving this belief beyond reasonable doubt; the defendant does not need to prove it.

A trial judge must put a defence before the jury if there is a proper evidential basis for it, even if it is inconsistent with the defendant's primary case. This applies to the defence of mistaken belief in consent in rape cases.

An accused's evidence asserting consent constitutes evidence of a belief in consent. If the accused does not testify, a genuine belief may still be raised by evidence from the prosecution case. If raised, the jury must be directed to consider whether the prosecution has disproved it.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Morgan for the principle that a mistaken belief in consent, if genuine, is a defence to rape.
The judgment cites this case for the required direction to the jury on the state of mind in rape cases involving consent.
The judgment cites this Scottish case as being of assistance on the issue of a defendant who does not give evidence.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that putting a genuine but mistaken belief before the jury is a common practice where there is an evidential basis.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YU CHI KIT
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN SZE CHUN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP in Court

principles.principle_label: Direction on mistaken belief in consent
principles.paraphrase_en: A judge must give a direction to the jury on the defendant's genuine but mistaken belief in the complainant's consent to sexual intercourse when that issue is raised by the evidence as a whole and is a live issue in the case.

principles.principle_label: Role of jury in assessing evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: It is for the jury, as judges of the facts, to decide what facts to accept, which witnesses are credible, and to determine the issues based on the evidence.

An appellate court will find it difficult to sustain an allegation that counsel failed to explain admitted facts to the defendant when the standard practice of reading them in open court in the defendant's presence was followed and counsel signed them on the defendant's behalf.

A judge's direction to the jury is not necessarily deficient when referring to matters not put to a witness, provided the jury is also reminded that the defendant had instructed his lawyers about those matters.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from this case regarding when a direction on mistaken belief in consent is required.
The judgment applies this case, which further explained the principles from HKSAR v Ma Kin Yiu regarding directions on mistaken belief.
The judgment notes a submission that the present case resembles the facts of this cited authority.
The judgment cites this case again in relation to the potential evidential value of a complainant's diary entries.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MEREDITH GERALD SYDNEY
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: First-instance decisions from unrelated cases are generally not helpful for sentencing comparisons on appeal, especially for money-laundering offenses where facts vary widely and the amount involved is not the sole determinant of culpability.
A sentencing judge must identify the relevant considerations and ensure the starting point is consistent with established case law.
The application of the totality principle in sentencing is a matter for the judge's discretion and will not be faulted on appeal absent error.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites Hsu Yu Yi as providing relevant case law for determining an appropriate sentencing starting point for money-laundering offenses.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HOSSAIN AEJAZ
judges: Before: Hon D’Almada Remedios J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The act of intentionally applying unlawful force to another person, or causing them to apprehend immediate unlawful force, constitutes common assault.
A location is considered a public place for the purposes of mask-wearing regulations if it is accessible to any member of the public, such as a prayer hall open for worship.
A record of interview containing admissions made by a defendant is admissible as prosecution evidence to prove the facts admitted.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAU KAI MAN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Amy Chan

principles.principle_label: Sentencing guidelines applicability across drug types
principles.paraphrase_en: Sentencing guidelines established for trafficking in one type of dangerous drug (heroin) can also be applied to trafficking in another type of dangerous drug (cocaine).
When an offender commits separate and distinct types of offences, the sentences for those offences should generally be ordered to be served consecutively.

Even when consecutive sentences are appropriate for distinct offences, the overall combined sentence must be adjusted if necessary to ensure the total sentence is not excessive, applying the principle of totality.

relationships.target_case_name: Attorney General v Pedro Nel Rojas
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the holding that sentencing guidelines for heroin trafficking apply to cocaine trafficking.
The judgment cites the sentencing guidelines established in this case for trafficking in dangerous drugs.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TANG TAI WAI
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Ivy Chui

principles.principle_label: Sentencing approach for dangerous driving causing death
principles.paraphrase_en: The established sentencing approach for the offence of dangerous driving causing death requires a deterrent sentence due to the serious consequences of the offence, while recognizing that the defendant's culpability is the primary factor in determining the sentence.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites Liu Kwok Chun for its reference to the established sentencing approach from Poon Wing Kay and Cooksley.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Poon Wing Kay
relationships.description: The judgment applies the sentencing approach adopted in Poon Wing Kay, which itself followed the principles from Cooksley for dangerous driving causing death.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAU KIT TONG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge KH Cheang in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing factors for dangerous driving causing grievous bodily harm
principles.paraphrase_en: The primary consideration when sentencing for the offense of causing grievous bodily harm by dangerous driving is the defendant's culpability, which requires evaluating both the objective dangerousness of the driving and the defendant's moral blameworthiness.

principles.principle_label: Harm as sentencing factor in dangerous driving cases
principles.paraphrase_en: The harm and impact inflicted on the victim(s) is a factor closely connected to culpability and must be assessed in sentencing for dangerous driving causing grievous bodily harm.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Chu Wing Yin Christine
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing framework established by the Court of Appeal in that case for offenses of causing grievous bodily harm by dangerous driving.

judges: Before: HH Judge K Lo

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WHITE NATHAN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge David Cheung in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits burglary under section 11(1)(b) of the Theft Ordinance when they enter a building as a trespasser with intent to steal therein.
Burglary of commercial premises such as a restaurant is classified as non-domestic burglary, which is a relevant factor for sentencing.
Entering a building without permission after breaking a secured lock constitutes trespassory entry for burglary.
Multiple defendants can be jointly charged with burglary when they participate together in the unlawful entry and theft.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSE MING HIN
judges: Before: HH Judge A. J. Woodcock

principles.principle_label: Latent risk assessment in drug sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for drug offenses, the court must assess whether there is a latent risk of the offender re-offending or posing a danger to society; if no such latent risk is found, the sentence should not be increased to reflect this factor, and quantity becomes the primary determinant of the starting point.
A defendant who enters a guilty plea is entitled to a reduction in sentence, with the judgment applying a one-third discount from the starting point for the plea.

When imposing sentences for multiple offenses, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the combined sentence is just and proportionate to the overall criminality, which may result in concurrent sentences.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YIU CHI KUEN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge K Lo

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for traffic offenses, the court's primary focus should be on the culpability of the defendant's driving behavior, rather than giving significant weight to the defendant's clear record or general good character.

principles.principle_label: Dominant sentencing principles for traffic offenses
principles.paraphrase_en: The dominant sentencing principles for traffic offenses are general deterrence, retribution, and denunciation.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing principles established in HKSAR v Lee Yau Wing regarding the focus on culpability and the limited weight of good character in traffic offense cases.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YEUNG MING KIT
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A sentencing court has discretion to impose a reduced sentence when an offense is committed due to severe financial pressure or compelling circumstances.
When imposing consecutive sentences for multiple offenses, the court must consider the overall total sentence and may order parts to run concurrently to avoid a crushing aggregate term.
When offenses occur at different locations, on different dates, and involve different victims, consecutive sentences should generally be imposed as the starting point.

relationships.target_case_name: The Queen v Yau Kwok Tung
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to establish that a 4-year sentence is at the top end of the tariff for robberies where weapons were not displayed.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that sentencing courts may reduce sentences for offenses committed under grave financial hardship or compelling circumstances.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TONG PAK-FAI, SAMMY
judges: Before: HH Judge A. J. Woodcock

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MAN CHI KWONG
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.principle_label: Sentencing principles for dangerous driving causing harm
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for offenses of dangerous driving causing grievous bodily harm or death, the court must accommodate the principles of retribution, deterrence, and denunciation to maintain public confidence in the sentencing system.

In dangerous driving causing grievous bodily harm cases, the defendant's culpability should be the dominant component in sentencing, with the outcome of the offense being an important but secondary consideration.
When the focus is on the culpability of the defendant's driving in dangerous driving offenses, a clear record and general good character carry little weight in sentencing.
The dominant sentencing principle for dangerous driving causing harm offenses is general deterrence, with principles of retribution and denunciation also being relevant.
The court must consider what constitutes a just and fair final sentence in light of the totality principle, even when multiple offenses arise from the same incident.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing principles of retribution, deterrence, and denunciation established in Lee Yau Wing for dangerous driving causing harm offenses.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Chan Chi Chiu Louis
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Chan Chi Chiu Louis that culpability is the dominant sentencing component in dangerous driving causing grievous bodily harm cases.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG PUI FUN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge M Chow in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits an offence under s.18(1)(b) of the Import and Export Ordinance (Cap. 60) when they attempt to export goods that are not accurately declared in the export manifest.

A person commits an offence under s.7(1) and (3) of the Protection of Endangered Species of Animals and Plants Ordinance (Cap. 586) when they attempt to export specimens of Appendix I species without the required CITES documentation.
Section 159G of the Crimes Ordinance (Cap. 200) provides the legal basis for charging a person with an attempt to commit an offence under other ordinances.
The export of specimens of Appendix I species under the Protection of Endangered Species of Animals and Plants Ordinance requires valid documentation issued under the CITES convention.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. PUN KAM HUNG
judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing guideline for s17I IO employment offenses
principles.paraphrase_en: For a basic offense under section 17I of the Immigration Ordinance involving a single employee, where the employer had full knowledge or was reckless about the illegal status, with no aggravated features and employment on a casual basis, an immediate three-month sentence after trial is appropriate.

principles.principle_label: Aggravating factor: employment for cheap labor
principles.paraphrase_en: Employing a person who cannot be lawfully employed in order to obtain cheap labor constitutes an aggravating feature of the offense, even if the primary motive is financial gain rather than direct exploitation of the worker.
When assessing whether a sentence enhancement is excessive, the percentage increase must be considered in the context of the overall totality of the sentence and the starting point, not in isolation.
A more severe sentence is justified for an employer who employs several people who cannot be lawfully employed, compared to the sentence for employing a single such person.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HO KA PO
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.principle_label: Sentencing framework for dangerous driving causing death/GBH
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for the offense of dangerous driving causing death or grievous bodily harm, the court must conduct a two-stage assessment: first, evaluating the objective dangerousness of the driving, and second, evaluating the moral culpability of the driver.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Law Siu Kin
relationships.description: The present judgment adopts and applies the two-stage sentencing framework established by the Court of Appeal in this case.

candidates.case_name: NATURAL DAIRY (NZ) HOLDINGS LTD (IN PROVISIONAL LIQUIDATION) v. CHEN KEEN (alias JACK CHEN) AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: Allegations of fraud or conspiracy must be pleaded with the highest degree of specificity and particularity, and cannot be left to be inferred from the general facts.

principles.principle_label: Henderson v Henderson abuse of process in interlocutory hearings
principles.paraphrase_en: The principles preventing the re-litigation of issues (Henderson v Henderson) apply to interlocutory hearings as well as final trials. A party should generally advance all reasonably available arguments at the first opportunity in interlocutory applications concerning similar relief, unless there is a significant change in circumstances.

A 'rolled-up plea' that alleges a person 'knew or ought reasonably to have known' is treated as a single allegation of constructive knowledge, not as alternative allegations of actual and constructive knowledge. Where dishonesty requires a plea of actual knowledge, such a rolled-up plea may be defective.

principles.principle_label: Striking out dishonest assistance based on pleading wording
principles.paraphrase_en: The inclusion of the wording 'ought reasonably to have known' in a plea of dishonest assistance is not, by itself, a sufficient basis to strike out the entire claim. The court may instead order the amendment or particularization of the pleading to clarify whether 'blind-eye' knowledge is alleged.

The intention requirement for unlawful means conspiracy is not satisfied by mere carelessness ('should have known'), but can be satisfied by recklessness, where a defendant knows his acts would likely injure the plaintiff and consciously disregards that outcome.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this authority for the proposition that reckless imprudence can call into question a person's honesty for the purpose of dishonest assistance.

relationships.target_case_name: Top Point Ltd v K&L Gates (A Firm)
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that a 'rolled-up plea' of knowledge is treated as a single allegation of constructive knowledge.
The judgment cites this case, read with Top Point Ltd, to establish that carelessness is insufficient for the intention element in unlawful means conspiracy.

relationships.target_case_name: E D & F Man Capital Markets v Come Harvest Holdings Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this English authority to support the view that recklessness can satisfy the intention requirement for unlawful means conspiracy if harm was a known necessary consequence.
The judgment refers to this case in the context of discussing the benefit or effect of a prior injunction decision.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WRIGHT JAMES MICHAEL
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Casewell

principles.paraphrase_en: An action is unreasonable and may constitute an offense if it is taken in circumstances where a dangerous outcome is foreseeable, particularly when the action is directed towards a target without adequate safety measures to prevent harm to areas frequented by people.
A conviction requires that the charge be proved beyond a reasonable doubt.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. FUNG KA WAI ROGER
judges: Before: Her Honour Judge A N Tse Ching in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court may revise a sentence if it finds the original sentence to be manifestly inadequate and wrong in principle.
Where multiple defendants are involved in the same incident, their culpability may be assessed as identical, warranting equal sentencing treatment.
The period of driving disqualification imposed should reflect both the seriousness of the offence and the offender's previous driving record.
Driving while avoidably distracted, such as by using a mobile phone (especially hand-held), constitutes an aggravating factor in sentencing for driving offences.
The commission of other offences at the same time, such as driving while disqualified or without insurance, is an aggravating factor in sentencing.

relationships.description: The judgment notes that the principles from this case were considered in a subsequent Hong Kong appeal.

relationships.description: The judgment references this case as an application of the Cooksley principles to a case involving dangerous driving and racing.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Lau Sin Ting

judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TONG KIN WAI
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge K K Leung

principles.principle_label: Sentencing principles for dangerous driving causing death
principles.paraphrase_en: For the offence of dangerous driving causing death, the primary sentencing objectives are general deterrence, retribution, and denunciation, due to the serious consequences of the offense. The defendant's culpability is the central factor in determining the sentence.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Poon Wing Kay and Another
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the sentencing principles established in this case, particularly regarding the approach to sentencing for dangerous driving causing death.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Liu Kwok Chun
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority supporting the sentencing principles for dangerous driving causing death.
The judgment references this case as containing relevant holdings on the matter.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Lam Siu Tong
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Court of Appeal case for its holdings on sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KANTHIMA MALEEMAO
judges: Before: HH Judge Yiu

principles.paraphrase_en: When DNA evidence is presented as a mixture from multiple individuals, the statistical likelihood ratio must be evaluated to determine the strength of support for a defendant being a contributor, acknowledging that such evidence can be very strong but not conclusive on its own.

The legal understanding of DNA evidence must account for the scientific possibility of secondary transfer, meaning DNA could be deposited on an item through indirect contact rather than direct handling by the defendant.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this Australian High Court decision as influential authority for the legal principle that the possibility of secondary DNA transfer cannot be ruled out.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM KWAI SANG
judges: Before: HH Judge Stanley Chan

principles.principle_label: Elements of unlawful sexual intercourse with girl under 13
principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits an offence under section 123 of the Crimes Ordinance if they have sexual intercourse with a girl who is under the age of 13, regardless of the victim's consent.
A defendant who pleads guilty to a charge is properly convicted on that charge without the need for a full trial on the merits.

principles.principle_label: Court's power to call for victim impact report
principles.paraphrase_en: In sentencing for sexual offences involving a young victim, the court has the discretion to adjourn the case and call for a victim impact assessment report to inform the sentencing decision.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAU HO KEI AND OTHERS
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing range for conspiracy to defraud
principles.paraphrase_en: In a case involving a company fraudulently claiming payment for non-existent 'ghost workers' over a sustained period, a sentencing range of 30 to 36 months' imprisonment can be correct.

principles.principle_label: Rejection of mitigation based on lack of skilled workers
principles.paraphrase_en: A mitigation claim that an offence was committed due to an inability to find skilled workers can be rejected if the evidence shows the individuals involved did not possess special skills qualifying them for the job.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing range established in Cheung Suet Ting for a similar fraud involving 'ghost workers'.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Li Wai Man
relationships.description: The judgment notes this case was cited by the defence in mitigation to argue for a lower sentence when an offence is committed by following instructions rather than greed.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. AN KAM FUNG
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI KAM FUNG (賴錦鋒)
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ and Mr Justice Chan NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: A trial judge has a duty to put an alternative verdict before the jury if there is evidence upon which a reasonable jury might return that alternative verdict, even if not raised by the parties or inconsistent with the defence case, to prevent over-conviction, under-conviction, or wrongful acquittal.

The threshold requirement for directing a jury on an alternative verdict is low: the evidence supporting the alternative must not be so incredible, tenuous, or uncertain that no reasonable jury could accept it. This low threshold is consistent with the prosecution's burden to prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt.

The way the defence presents its case on the facts is highly relevant in determining whether sufficient evidence exists for an obvious alternative verdict. If a factual alternative does not arise from how the defence deals with and presents the evidence, this will usually be decisive.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows and applies the principle established in Ho Hoi Shing regarding a judge's duty to direct on alternative verdicts.
The present judgment applies the principle and cautionary note from Chau Yui Ming regarding the approach to alternative verdicts.
The judgment cites Ho Hoi Shing for the proposition that the threshold for directing on an alternative verdict is a low one.

relationships.target_case_name: Various trafficking cases cited by applicant
relationships.description: The Court distinguishes various trafficking cases cited by the applicant on their facts, finding they do not support the applicant's argument.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KONG TSZ CHUNG RYAN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Andrew Chan J

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for ketamine trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: The sentencing starting point for trafficking in ketamine is determined primarily by the quantity of the drug, with reference to established precedents for specific weight ranges.

principles.principle_label: Aggravating factors in drug trafficking sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's role beyond that of a mere courier, such as involvement in repackaging, delivery, and arranging storage premises, constitutes aggravating factors that warrant an upward adjustment from the quantity-based starting point.
A one-third discount from the sentencing starting point is applied for a timely guilty plea.

When trafficking involves multiple types of dangerous drugs, a small quantity of one drug may be disregarded for sentencing purposes if it is negligible compared to a large quantity of another primary drug.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing enhancement for repeat trafficking offence
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's previous conviction for a similar trafficking offence is an aggravating factor that warrants an enhancement to the sentence beyond the quantity-based starting point.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing precedent from this case to determine a starting point for trafficking 39kg of ketamine.
The present judgment applies the sentencing precedent from this case to determine a starting point for trafficking 122kg of ketamine.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Lee Hin Long Owen
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing precedent from this case to determine a starting point for trafficking approximately 4.2kg of ketamine.

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. TONG WAI HUNG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Poon CJHC, Macrae VP and A Pang J in Court

principles.principle_label: Joint enterprise liability for riot/unlawful assembly
principles.paraphrase_en: The doctrine of joint enterprise applies to the offences of unlawful assembly and riot under sections 18 and 19 of the Public Order Ordinance. A party to a joint enterprise is criminally liable for acts done in furtherance of the common purpose, even if not physically present at the scene.

A fundamental principle of criminal law is that a secondary party (accessory) is guilty of the same offence as the principal offender. This principle applies to parties liable under the doctrine of joint enterprise for unlawful assembly and riot.

principles.principle_label: Legislative intent to maintain public order
principles.paraphrase_en: When construing whether joint enterprise liability applies to unlawful assembly and riot offences, the court must adopt an interpretation that gives full recognition to the public interest in maintaining public order and ensuring all perpetrators are caught, while avoiding over-charging innocent persons.

principles.principle_label: Primary offence provisions vs. liability principles
principles.paraphrase_en: Sections 18 and 19 of the Public Order Ordinance define the offences of unlawful assembly and riot with principal offenders in mind. The requirements for principal offenders at the scene do not displace or concern the liability of accessories or parties to a joint enterprise who may not be present.

principles.principle_label: Consistent treatment of accessories and joint enterprise parties
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no logical or policy justification for treating traditional accessories and parties to a joint enterprise differently under sections 18 and 19. Both are parties to the offences and should be held liable as such.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Jogee for the fundamental principle that a secondary party is guilty of the same offence as the principal.
The judgment applies the principle from Sze Kwan Lung that a party to a joint enterprise is criminally liable for acts done in furtherance of the joint enterprise, including for riot offences.
The judgment cites Stewart for the proposition that a party to a joint enterprise participates in the criminal act of another.
The judgment cites Chan Kam Shing, which itself quoted from R v Stewart, regarding participation in a joint enterprise.

The judgment distinguishes the principles in Leung Tin Kei as dealing with the liability of principal offenders at the scene, not with the liability of accessories or joint enterprise parties who may be absent.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG KONG FAI AND OTHERS
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam in Court

principles.principle_label: Knowledge requirement for handling stolen goods
principles.paraphrase_en: For the offense of handling stolen goods, the defendant's belief that the goods might be stolen, without taking steps to confirm or ascertain their origin, can be sufficient to establish the necessary knowledge or suspicion.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing uplift for repeated dishonesty offender
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant with a particularly bad record of previous convictions for dishonesty offenses is to be treated as a repeated offender, warranting an increase in the starting point of the sentence.
When adjusting a sentence, particularly when increasing it for an offender's bad record, the court must consider the overall totality of the sentence to ensure it is just and proportionate.

relationships.target_case_name: Unspecified (Appeal Committee of the Court of Final Appeal)
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the Appeal Committee's determination that a specific rule does not permit reduction of a sentence term below a minimum threshold.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SAFDER MAJAD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Bernard Chung in Court

principles.principle_label: Elements of reckless arson endangering life
principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of arson being reckless as to whether life would be endangered under sections 60(2), (3) and 63(1) of the Crimes Ordinance (Cap. 200) requires proof that the defendant, without lawful excuse, destroyed or damaged property by fire, intending or being reckless as to such destruction or damage, and was reckless as to whether the life of another would be endangered.

principles.principle_label: Admission of facts as basis for conviction
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's plea of guilty and admission of the Summary of Facts establishes the factual basis for the conviction, including the circumstances, actions, and mental state described in the charge.

principles.principle_label: Recklessness as to endangerment of life
principles.paraphrase_en: The offence is established where the defendant's act of setting a fire in a residential building containing other occupants demonstrates recklessness as to whether the life of another would be endangered, which can be inferred from the setting and the foreseeable consequences.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KATA MALUSI NICHOLAS
judges: Before: Hon Wilson Chan J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LO WING PING
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The offense of fraud under section 16A of the Theft Ordinance requires proof that the defendant, by deceit and with intent to defraud, induced another person to act, resulting in benefit to the defendant or prejudice (or substantial risk of prejudice) to that other person.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TANG YIK TSUN
judges: Before: DHCJ Vinci Lam, SC

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's admission to a Summary of Facts prepared by the prosecution constitutes formal acceptance of those facts for the purpose of sentencing.
The court must read and consider mitigation letters submitted on behalf of the defendant, which may demonstrate genuine remorse, rehabilitation, and family support.
While not excusing the offence, a defendant's stated motive (such as financial desperation) may be noted as part of the factual narrative admitted by the defendant.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. FENG KA HO AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Colin Wong

principles.principle_label: Strict liability for unlawful sexual intercourse with girl under 13
principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of unlawful sexual intercourse with a girl under the age of 13 is one of strict liability, meaning the prosecution does not need to prove the defendant knew or believed the victim was under 13.

principles.principle_label: Age of victim as essential element
principles.paraphrase_en: For a charge of unlawful sexual intercourse under section 123, the prosecution must prove the victim was a girl under the age of 13 at the time of the offence.
The offence of indecent conduct towards a child under the age of 16 requires proof that the defendant engaged in indecent conduct with or towards the child.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. THAPA MANISH AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: HH Judge E Yip

principles.paraphrase_en: The act of holding someone's neck, resulting in documented tenderness, can constitute the actus reus of common assault.

Substantial delay between the offence and trial may be raised as a factor potentially prejudicial to a defendant, requiring the court to consider the timeline of events including surrender, charging, and adjournments.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAU KA CHUN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.paraphrase_en: The offence under section 37D(1)(a) of the Immigration Ordinance requires the prosecution to prove that the defendant assisted the passage within Hong Kong of persons who were unauthorized entrants.

The actus reus of assisting the passage of unauthorized entrants can be established by conduct such as meeting them after they land, communicating with co-conspirators, and helping them with their luggage into a vehicle intended for their transport.

Individuals can be jointly charged for an offence where their actions, conducted in concert, collectively constitute the elements of the crime, such as when two drivers coordinate to meet and transport unauthorized entrants.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. IVANCHENKO DMYTRO
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Bernard Chung

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for dangerous drugs trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: The court established a baseline custodial sentence of three years imprisonment for each count of trafficking in dangerous drugs.
A one-third discount from the starting sentence is applied in recognition of a timely guilty plea.
When imposing consecutive sentences for multiple offences, the court must consider the overall total sentence to ensure it is not excessive.
For related but distinct offences, the court may order parts of the sentences for subsequent charges to run consecutively to the sentence for the primary charge.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YUNG KI CHEUNG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of burglary under section 11(1)(b) of the Theft Ordinance is established when a person enters part of a building as a trespasser and steals property therein.
A person can be a trespasser for burglary by entering part of a building that is closed to the public, even if the building's internal operations (like machines) are still functioning.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YU KIN HONG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of totality must be considered when assessing sentence, but the presence of other aggravating features can still justify enhancing a sentence even if the base sentence is already lengthy and just.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI JINTIAN
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.principle_label: Sentencing factors for young offenders in sexual cases
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing young offenders for sexual offenses, the court may consider leniency if the offense involved mutual agreement between parties of similar age, occurred in private, lacked threats or inducements, and involved responsible safety measures that minimized health risks.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing principles articulated by the Court of Appeal regarding leniency for young offenders in certain sexual offense circumstances.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MA CHUN MAN
judges: Before: Hon Poon CJHC, Pang and A Pang JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: NSL and local law convergence principle
principles.paraphrase_en: The National Security Law is intended to converge and be complementary with Hong Kong's local laws. Where the NSL does not provide specific rules on an issue, local legal principles apply.

For sentencing under Article 21 of the NSL, which distinguishes cases of 'serious nature' from 'minor nature', the classification is not defined in the NSL itself. Therefore, Hong Kong courts must apply local sentencing principles to make this determination.
The most fundamental consideration in sentencing is generally the gravamen (seriousness) of the offence itself.

principles.principle_label: Rule of law principle under NSL
principles.paraphrase_en: Offences endangering national security must be dealt with in adherence to the principle of the rule of law. Conviction and punishment must only be for acts that constitute offences under the law.

principles.principle_label: Mitigation for prior convictions and remorse
principles.paraphrase_en: A prior conviction does not itself aggravate the offence unless it shows the defendant to be a persistent offender. It merely removes the mitigating benefit of good character. Similarly, a failure to show remorse or make restitution merely means an absence of mitigating factors; it does not positively aggravate the sentence.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Ho Yung Yin regarding how prior convictions and lack of remorse affect sentencing mitigation.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI CHEE YING AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Toh , Hon D’Almada Remedios and Hon Alex Lee JJ in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For a continuing offence, the statutory limitation period for prosecution does not begin to run until the period of the continuing offence has ended. A prosecution may be initiated on any day during the commission of the offence or, subject to time limits, after it ends.

A charge of statutory conspiracy, being a continuing offence, is subject to the same rule: the limitation period commences only upon the cessation of the conspiracy, not from the date of the initial agreement.

principles.principle_label: Commencement of proceedings by laying information
principles.paraphrase_en: Criminal proceedings for an indictable offence are generally commenced by laying a complaint or information before a magistrate. The information is considered 'laid' when it is received at the office of the clerk to the justices by an authorised staff member.

principles.principle_label: Warrant or summons as court process
principles.paraphrase_en: Once an information is laid, the issuance of a warrant or summons is a matter for the court, not the prosecution. These are merely processes to bring the accused before the magistrate and are not required for proceedings to be considered instituted.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the CFA's principle that the limitation period for a continuing offence starts only after the offence period ends.
The present judgment follows the Court of Appeal's reasoning that for a conspiracy charge, the limitation period runs from the cessation of the conspiracy.

relationships.target_case_name: R v Manchester Stipendiary Magistrate, Ex parte Hill
relationships.description: The judgment applies the House of Lords' holding on when an information is considered laid for the purpose of commencing proceedings.
The judgment applies the Court of Appeal's endorsement and adoption of the Ex parte Hill principle.

relationships.target_case_name: R v Kennet Justices, ex parte Humphrey and another
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case in support of the proposition that instituting proceedings does not require the accused to be brought before a court.
The judgment cites this case for the principle that a warrant or summons is merely a process to bring the accused before the court.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SIT YI KI AND OTHERS
judges: Before: The Honourable Mr. Justice D. Yau

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEUNG YU WING
judges: Before: Hon Tam J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for a trafficking offence involving more than one type of dangerous drug, the court should adopt a combined approach to determine the appropriate starting point.
There are three recognized tests to cross-check whether a sentence for drug trafficking is manifestly inadequate: the absurdity test, the ratio test, and the conversion test.
The court must select the most appropriate cross-check test based on the circumstances of the case to determine the notional sentencing starting point.
When determining the final sentence within a guideline band, the court must assess the defendant's specific role in the offence.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing methodology and cross-check tests established in Chan Yuk Leong for cases involving multiple types of dangerous drugs.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEUNG TSZ CHUNG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: DHCJ Woodcock

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for armed robbery with weapon
principles.paraphrase_en: For an ordinary case of armed robbery where a dangerous weapon like a knife is displayed to the victim, the normal appropriate starting point for sentencing is 5 years' imprisonment.

principles.principle_label: Adjustment of starting point for weapon type and use
principles.paraphrase_en: The sentencing starting point for a robbery-related offence can be adjusted based on the nature of the weapon involved, its capability to cause harm, and the manner of its use against a victim.
A defendant who pleads guilty at the earliest opportunity is entitled to a full one-third discount on the sentence, whereas a plea entered after a trial date is set only merits a 25% discount.

When sentencing for multiple offences arising from the same course of events, the court must apply the totality principle. Sentences may run consecutively to reflect distinct, independent criminal acts, or concurrently where the criminality of one offence has already been factored into the sentence for another.

principles.principle_label: Equal culpability in conspiracy despite different roles
principles.paraphrase_en: In a conspiracy, participants can be found equally culpable despite having different functional roles, provided their involvement was pivotal to the execution of the common plan.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HU MINGJIE
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and K Yeung J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Statements or documents created by an alleged co-conspirator can be admitted as evidence against another accused charged with the same conspiracy, provided they were made in furtherance of the common purpose and there is other reasonable evidence of the accused's participation. They are not admissible to prove the truth of any assertions contained within them.
Save for exceptional cases, the sentence for trafficking in a dangerous drug should not normally exceed 35 years' imprisonment, as per revised sentencing guidelines.
Applicants or appellants sentenced on or after 18 September 2018 should benefit from revised sentencing guidelines established in a prior leading case.

principles.principle_label: Aggravating factor of recent similar conviction
principles.paraphrase_en: A previous criminal conviction for the same type of offence, particularly one committed shortly before the current offence, is a serious aggravating factor that must be taken into account when applying the totality principle in sentencing.
A sentence for a current offence can be enhanced to reflect a previous conviction for a related offence.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Ahern regarding the co-conspirators' rule for the admissibility of evidence.
The judgment follows the sentencing guideline from Sum Ka Wa which sets a normal maximum sentence for drug trafficking.
The judgment applies the principle from Lam Man Tak regarding who is entitled to benefit from revised sentencing guidelines.
The judgment cites Huang Ruifang (No 3) as the source of the revised sentencing guidelines for drug trafficking.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI SING YAN
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge A Wong in Court

principles.principle_label: Mens rea timing for agreement-based offences
principles.paraphrase_en: The mental state required for an offence is assessed based on the defendant's state of mind at the time the agreement was formed.

principles.principle_label: Inference of knowledge for drug importation
principles.paraphrase_en: A court cannot infer that a defendant was a knowing party to drug importation or was assisting such importation unless the admitted facts provide sufficient material to support such an inference.
The general desire to foster rehabilitation for young offenders should be considered in sentencing, even when specific statutory provisions for young offenders do not apply.

principles.principle_label: Mitigating value of youth for serious offences
principles.paraphrase_en: The defendant's young age may carry little significance in mitigating sentence when the offences committed are extremely serious.

relationships.description: The court applies the sentencing principle and approach established in this case.
The court applies the sentencing principle and approach established in this case.
The court follows the Court of Appeal's holding from this case regarding the inference of knowledge in drug importation offences.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KHO YIN TAK
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Edmond Lee in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Direct comparison of sentences in other individual cases is not meaningful because each case depends on its own specific facts and circumstances.
Sentencing guidelines from established cases can serve as a benchmark, and a sentence that aligns with this benchmark is not necessarily wrong in principle or manifestly excessive.
For offences not requiring a test of 'exceptional circumstances', the court must consider all circumstances of the offence and the defendant when deciding whether to impose a suspended sentence.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing principle for suspended sentences as stated in Secretary for Justice v Wade.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHUA SHIRLEY MASIGLA AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Wilful blindness, which equates to knowledge in law, is established only when a defendant suspects a fact, realizes its high probability, but deliberately refrains from obtaining confirmation specifically to be able to deny knowledge later. It requires an intention to cheat the administration of justice and is not merely suspicion or deliberate disregard of suspicious circumstances.
Suspicion alone does not equate to knowledge for establishing criminal liability. A wilful blindness direction should only be given when the issue is properly warranted by the evidence.
Acts and declarations by a co-conspirator made in furtherance of the conspiracy can be used as evidence to prove both the existence of the agreement and the participation of each alleged conspirator.

principles.principle_label: Independent evidence requirement for co-conspirator rule
principles.paraphrase_en: The co-conspirator's rule requires independent evidence of the conspiracy before acts and declarations of co-conspirators can be utilized against other defendants, though a specific direction to the jury on this requirement may not be fatal if there is an abundance of such independent evidence.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Harjani to note that the doctrine of wilful blindness has been subject to academic and judicial criticism.
The judgment applies the principle from Lee Wing Yan that wilful blindness directions are fraught with difficulties and require careful employment.
The judgment applies the warning from Law Wai Choi about the danger of treating deliberate disregard of suspicious circumstances as an automatic alternative to direct evidence of knowledge.
The judgment cites Ahern v The Queen for the principle that acts and declarations by co-conspirators in furtherance of the conspiracy can prove both agreement and participation.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SHING YIU FUNG
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The appropriate sentencing discount for a guilty plea depends on the stage at which the plea is indicated. An indication made after trial dates are fixed but before trial commencement typically warrants a discount in the range of 20% to 25% from the starting point.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing discount range established in Ngo Van Nam to determine the appropriate reduction for a guilty plea entered at a specific procedural stage.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG KIN CHUNG
judges: Before: Hon Poon CJHC, Pang JA and Anthea Pang JA in Court

principles.principle_label: NSL convergence principle with local laws
principles.paraphrase_en: The National Security Law is intended to operate in conjunction with Hong Kong's local laws, seeking convergence, compatibility, and complementarity. Local laws continue to apply normally unless expressly or by necessary implication displaced by the NSL, with NSL provisions prevailing in case of inconsistency.

principles.principle_label: Application of conspiracy provisions to NSL offences
principles.paraphrase_en: Through the convergence principle, section 159A of the Crimes Ordinance applies to make it an offence to conspire to incite others to commit crimes under the National Security Law, even though conspiracy is not expressly mentioned in the relevant NSL provisions.

The convergence principle applies to the interpretation of the NSL's sentencing provisions, meaning local sentencing laws and principles are intended to apply alongside relevant NSL provisions within the NSL framework.

Section 159C of the Crimes Ordinance was enacted to align penalties for conspiracy offences with those for substantive offences, ensuring sentences are subject to maximum terms and proportionate to offender culpability.

principles.principle_label: Section 159C operation within NSL sentencing framework
principles.paraphrase_en: The correct application of the convergence principle requires section 159C to operate within the sentencing framework established by NSL21 to achieve convergence, compatibility, and complementarity between the two sets of provisions.

relationships.description: The judgment follows the Court of Final Appeal's interpretation of the NSL's legislative intent regarding convergence with local laws.
The court notes that lower courts in this case failed to thoroughly consider the convergence principle regarding section 159C's operation within NSL sentencing frameworks.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Ng Gordon Ching-hang & 46 Others
relationships.description: The judgment questions the lower court's view that NSL sentencing ranges in section 159C cases are merely referential rather than mandatory.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM YAN YI AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: DHCJ A Wong

principles.principle_label: Sentencing approach for drug trafficking conspiracy
principles.paraphrase_en: When determining the notional sentence after trial for drug trafficking conspiracy, the court adopts a combined approach and assesses fairness by applying the absurdity test, the conversion test, and the ratio test.

A defendant's role in a drug trafficking syndicate must be assessed based on available evidence; where material is insufficient to show they were not relatively low in the conspiracy hierarchy, this may be a mitigating factor in sentencing.

For a sentencing enhancement to apply based on a co-defendant's status (such as being a minor), the court must be satisfied the defendant had actual knowledge of that status; mere association is insufficient if knowledge cannot be safely inferred.
principles.paragraph_hrase_en: For a sentencing enhancement to apply based on a co-defendant's status (such as being a minor), the court must be satisfied the defendant had actual knowledge of that status; mere association is insufficient if knowledge cannot be safely inferred.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG CHING WANG
judges: Before: Hon Wong J

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for trafficking in multiple types of dangerous drugs, the court should adopt a combined approach applying the absurdity test, conversion test, and ratio test, while considering aggravating features such as a wider market and the combined harmful effects on users.

principles.principle_label: Primary tariff reference for mixed drug cases
principles.paraphrase_en: In cases involving trafficking in multiple drugs, the sentencing court should primarily refer to the sentencing tariff for the most prominent drug type involved.

The conversion test may be applied to convert quantities of different drugs into a notional quantity of the primary drug for sentencing purposes, based on evidence such as cash rewards found and drugs in possession.
For trafficking in methamphetamine ('Ice'), where the narcotic content is between 300 to 600 grammes, the appropriate sentencing range is 15 to 20 years' imprisonment.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Islam S M Majharul
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the combined sentencing approach and tests established in this Court of Appeal case for multi-drug trafficking.
The judgment applies the sentencing tariff for methamphetamine ('Ice') trafficking established by this Court of Appeal decision.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN TSZ FUNG
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM HING CHIT
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, McWalters and Zervos JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The particulars of a conspiracy charge should specify the unlawful agreement the prosecution seeks to prove, not merely the substantive criminal offense intended. This focuses the trial on the specific agreement alleged.
The statutory test for allowing a criminal appeal is whether the conviction is 'unsafe or unsatisfactory'. The two terms are disjunctive, but case law does not establish they bear different meanings.

principles.principle_label: Lurking doubt as a route to unsafe verdict
principles.paraphrase_en: The 'lurking doubt' principle is one recognized route for the Court of Appeal to determine if a conviction is unsafe. It is not a separate test but a vivid expression of the statutory 'unsafe or unsatisfactory' standard.

The application of the 'lurking doubt' principle requires reasoned analysis, not a subjective judicial hunch. It should not be used to allow the appeal court to simply substitute its own view of guilt for the jury's verdict.

principles.principle_label: Miscarriage of justice and unsafe conviction
principles.paraphrase_en: If a conviction is found to be unsafe, the court cannot conclude that no miscarriage of justice has occurred. A miscarriage requires errors or irregularities capable of having a very substantial impact on the trial.

relationships.target_case_name: Stafford v Director of Public Prosecutions
relationships.description: The judgment cites Stafford for the summary of the appellate test, noting the 'lurking doubt' principle is a way of expressing the 'unsafe or unsatisfactory' standard.
The judgment cites Pope to illustrate the English Court of Appeal's effort to limit the scope of the 'lurking doubt' principle.
The judgment cites McIlkenny for the observation that the terms 'unsafe or unsatisfactory' are disjunctive but not held to bear different meanings.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HUI KWAI CHUNG, DICK AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J

principles.principle_label: Conspiracy to traffic dangerous drugs - elements
principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits the offence of conspiracy to traffic in a dangerous drug when they agree with another person to unlawfully traffic in a dangerous drug, such as cocaine, even if the specific date of the agreement is unknown but falls within a defined period.
A statement made by a defendant upon arrest and under caution is admissible as evidence against them, and can include explanations about their possession of drugs and knowledge of the circumstances.
Subsequent video-recorded interviews where a defendant admits to the details of a criminal agreement and their role in it are admissible as evidence of the conspiracy.

principles.principle_label: Agreed Summary of Facts as evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: An Agreed Summary of Facts presented at the time of a guilty plea forms the factual basis for sentencing and is binding on the defendants.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHUA SHIRLEY MASIGLA AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden and standard of proof direction
principles.paraphrase_en: A judge may give a general direction on the burden and standard of proof at the start of the summing-up, in line with specimen directions, and later refer to specific evidence while reminding the jury of those overarching principles.
The correct approach to a defendant's out-of-court statements, as established in Liberato v R and applied in Sze Kwan Lung v HKSAR, should be followed when directing a jury.

Specimen Direction 39, concerning confessions possibly induced by oppression, does not apply where there is no reasonable basis for the jury to conclude that oppression was or might have been present.

A judge has discretion to grant a sentencing discount for a defendant's assistance to the authorities, but granting a further discount for participation in a rehabilitation program may be wrong in principle based on subsequent authority.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Liberato regarding directions on a defendant's out-of-court statements.

relationships.target_case_name: Sze Kwan Lung & Others v HKSAR
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Hong Kong Court of Final Appeal's application of the Liberato direction from Sze Kwan Lung.
The judgment follows the subsequent majority judgment in Kilima Abubakar Abbas to find that a sentencing discount for a rehabilitation program was wrong in principle.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TONG HO WAN, SEAN
judges: Before: Recorder Martin Hui, SC

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for conspiracy to traffic in an unspecified quantity of dangerous drugs, the court must establish a starting point based on reasonable inferences and assumptions drawn from the case facts, and any ambiguity in those inferences should generally benefit the defendant.

principles.principle_label: Global approach to sentencing multiple charges
principles.paraphrase_en: In cases involving multiple drug trafficking charges, it is appropriate to adopt a global sentencing approach by aggregating the quantities of drugs from all charges to determine a single overall starting point.

When applying a global sentencing approach for multiple offenses, the court must consider the principle of totality to ensure the final sentence is proportionate to the offender's overall criminality.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing approach established in Tsang Ka Wing for conspiracy cases involving unspecified drug quantities.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI CHUN PONG
judges: Coram: Hon Barnes J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court may admit the contents of a mobile phone as evidence after hearing submissions on its admissibility.
The prosecution may rely on the testimony of a co-conspirator who has pleaded guilty and testifies as a tainted witness to establish the acts of another defendant.

principles.principle_label: Use of expert evidence for digital forensics
principles.paraphrase_en: Expert evidence can be used to establish the provenance of digital images, such as linking a photo to a specific mobile phone device.
Telegram messages between identified user accounts can be relied upon by the prosecution to prove communication between conspirators and to support inferences about their actions and intentions.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG HO LAM AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon S T Poon J

principles.paraphrase_en: The court has the discretion to order that sentences for multiple charges be served concurrently rather than consecutively, resulting in a single total sentence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ARIAS GUARDIA ROBERTO EMILIO
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, McWalters and Zervos JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For evidence of a co-conspirator's acts or declarations to be admissible against another accused under the co-conspirator's rule, there must first be independent evidence admissible against that accused which sufficiently links them to the conspiracy.

The co-conspirator's rule allows evidence of acts and declarations made by one conspirator in furtherance of the conspiracy to be used as evidence to prove the nature and extent of the conspiracy and the participation of other conspirators.

Out-of-court statements or documents may be admissible not for their truth, but where the coincidence between their contents and other proven facts is so exact that it reasonably supports an inference that the statements are true or relevant to a common plan.

principles.principle_label: Jury's role on inference from evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: Where evidence is relevant to an issue such as the existence of a conspiracy, it is for the jury to decide whether to draw the necessary inferences from that evidence.
The prejudicial effect of evidence can be mitigated by clear judicial directions that confine the jury's use of the evidence to a specific, permissible purpose.
principles.paragraph_hrase_en: The prejudicial effect of evidence can be mitigated by clear judicial directions that confine the jury's use of the evidence to a specific, permissible purpose.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Oei Hengky Wiryo that coincidence between out-of-court material and other evidence can support an inference of truth.
The judgment follows the Court of Final Appeal's ruling in Vivien Fan regarding the requirement for independent foundation evidence for the co-conspirator's rule.
The judgment cites Ahern v The Queen for the established principle of the co-conspirator's rule, noting its accuracy is not in doubt.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NAWAZ SARFRAZ AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon M Poon JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Co-conspirator rule - evidence of common purpose
principles.paraphrase_en: For the co-conspirator rule to apply, there must be some independent evidence, beyond the contested statement or document itself, indicating that the individuals shared a common unlawful purpose. This evidence does not need to prove the specific details of the agreement, but must show an agreement for some unlawful activity.
Before the co-conspirator rule can be invoked to admit evidence, the foundational evidence relied upon must itself be independently admissible.

principles.principle_label: Temporal proximity for inference of relevance
principles.paraphrase_en: Close timing between communications can support an inference that they are related and relevant to the same subject matter or ongoing activity.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Ahern regarding the requirement for independent evidence of a common unlawful purpose for the co-conspirator rule.
The judgment cites R v Smart to support a broad interpretation of what constitutes evidence of an agreement for the co-conspirator rule.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM TSZ HIM AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP and Zervos JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Risk of economic loss in fraud
principles.paraphrase_en: A victim of fraud can suffer not only actual economic loss but also be put at risk of further economic loss by acting on false representations, which they would not have done had they known the truth.

Participants in a joint criminal enterprise generally share equal culpability for the crime, irrespective of their specific roles, because the crime could not have been completed without each participant's contribution.
In sentencing for crimes committed as part of a joint enterprise, courts generally do not apportion blame or adjust sentences to any marked degree between participants based on differing roles.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Muhammad Rizwan and Ors
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from this case that sentencing courts generally do not apportion blame between participants in a joint enterprise.
The present judgment applies the sentencing principle from this case regarding equal culpability for participants in a joint enterprise.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YUNG CHI HANG
judges: Before: DHCJ A Wong

principles.paraphrase_en: When determining culpability in drug trafficking cases, the court assesses the defendant's specific role (such as storekeeper or courier) and the level of trust implied by the quantity of drugs handled, even if evidence of a senior hierarchical position is lacking.

A defendant's prior criminal record does not automatically constitute an aggravating sentencing feature, particularly when the current conviction is for a type of offence not previously committed, absent other aggravating circumstances.

principles.principle_label: Evidential standard for prior drug quantities
principles.paraphrase_en: The court requires sufficient evidence to be sure of the quantity of drugs involved in prior trafficking occasions; where a defendant's account is imprecise, the court will not rely on direct arithmetic extrapolation from vague units (like 'bags') to determine those quantities.

principles.principle_label: Consecutive sentence for conspiracy plus substantive offences
principles.paraphrase_en: A sentence for a conspiracy offence should generally be consecutive to sentences for the substantive offences committed pursuant to that conspiracy, as it represents additional culpability, subject to the totality principle.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Ngai Yiu Ching that a conspiracy offence warrants a consecutive sentence due to it representing culpability additional to the substantive trafficking charges.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM CHEUK HANG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge K Chan in Court
