principles.paraphrase_en: Active participation in a deception scheme, such as by adopting a false identity and conveying specific false information to a victim, can support the reasonable inference that the participant was briefed by and acted in concert with the principal orchestrator of the fraud.

A defendant's actions and statements, such as mentioning lawyers in the context of a demand for money, can demonstrate their awareness that the victim is acting under a specific false belief, which is a key element in establishing fraudulent intent.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WU SHING HONG
judges: Before: DHCJ A Wong

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing a young offender, the court should consider the general desire to foster rehabilitation, even if the specific statutory provision for sentencing in the absence of a conviction record does not apply.

principles.principle_label: Limits of mitigation for serious offences
principles.paraphrase_en: The mitigating weight of a defendant's youth and clean record can be significantly reduced or carry little significance when the offence committed is serious.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM TSZ KIN, KENT AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge must direct the jury about obvious alternative verdicts that are supported by the evidence. Whether an alternative verdict arises depends on how the defence case is presented; if the defence does not raise a factual basis for the alternative, this is usually decisive.
If a defendant raises the defence of duress, the judge must direct the jury that if they find the defendant's account of duress is true or might be true, they must acquit the defendant.

The prosecution may prove the existence of a criminal conspiracy by asking the jury to infer its existence from the evidence presented, such as CCTV footage showing defendants purchasing equipment relevant to the crime.

principles.principle_label: No alternative verdict where defence is duress
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no basis for a judge to put an alternative verdict to the jury when the defendant's sole defence is that they committed the offence but under duress.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle from Chau Yui Ming regarding a judge's duty to direct on alternative verdicts.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. RICO PAUL JASON LOPEZ
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for conspiracy offenses, the court must consider that the conspiracy was ongoing and would have continued until the participants were arrested, making it more serious than a single transaction.
A conspiracy involving multiple transactions is significantly more serious than a single act of trafficking.

relationships.description: The court bears in mind the sentencing approach stated by the Court of Appeal in this case.
The court applies the principle as modified by the Court of Appeal in this case.
The court takes into account the sentencing principles specified in this case.
The court takes into account the sentencing principles specified in this case.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ENRIQUEZ ANNA MAE JUSTIMBASTE
judges: Before: Hon Macrae Acting CJHC, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A person's mere presence in the vicinity of an object, even with knowledge of its existence, does not constitute possession. Possession requires actual physical custody or control over the object, coupled with knowledge and an intention to exercise that control.
Mere presence at a location where a crime is being committed is insufficient, by itself, to establish complicity in that crime or membership in a conspiracy to commit it.

relationships.description: The judgment references a relevant specimen direction for jury instructions on the concept of possession.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWUN CHI MING
judges: Before: Hon Woodcock J

principles.principle_label: Global approach to sentencing for multiple drug offenses
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple charges of trafficking in the same dangerous drug, it is appropriate and sensible to adopt a global approach by aggregating the quantities of drugs involved across all counts to determine the overall starting point.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing for conspiracy to traffic unspecified drug quantity
principles.paraphrase_en: In sentencing for a conspiracy to traffic in an unspecified quantity of dangerous drugs, the court must make its best effort to establish a starting point based on inferences and assumptions properly drawn from the case's specific facts, and where such inferences are not clear-cut, they should lean in the defendant's favor.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of aggravating and mitigating factors
principles.paraphrase_en: As part of the structured sentencing approach, the court must consider the presence of any aggravating factors or exceptional mitigating circumstances.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Tsang Ka Wing regarding how to determine a sentencing starting point for conspiracy to traffic in an unspecified quantity of dangerous drugs.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HON CHUN YAM
judges: Before: DHCJ Woodcock

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for drug trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: The starting point for sentencing in drug trafficking cases is normally determined by established sentencing tariffs and the quantity of dangerous drugs involved.
A guilty plea entered at the earliest opportunity constitutes a mitigating factor in sentencing, absent other exceptional mitigating or aggravating circumstances.

The offence of manufacturing dangerous drugs is more serious than trafficking alone, as those involved in manufacturing play a more significant role than mere couriers or storekeepers, and the scale of operation typically extends beyond the quantity seized at arrest.
Sentences for manufacturing dangerous drugs are generally far heavier than those for trafficking offences.
When imposing sentences for multiple offences, the court must consider the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is just and proportionate.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAU HON YEE
judges: Before: Hon Woodcock J

principles.paraphrase_en: The sentencing principle for drug offences is that sentences for manufacturing dangerous drugs are more severe than those for trafficking the same drugs.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Wong Chun Ping and Others as an authority supporting the sentencing principle that manufacturing offences attract heavier sentences than trafficking.

relationships.target_case_name: R v Cheung Wai Kwong and Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites R v Cheung Wai Kwong and Another as an authority supporting the sentencing principle that manufacturing offences attract heavier sentences than trafficking.
The judgment cites HKSAR v Ip Yuet Ho as an authority supporting the sentencing principle that manufacturing offences attract heavier sentences than trafficking.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOI HOI MING
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Bruce SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The starting point for bail decisions is that a court must order an accused person to be admitted to bail, as established by statute and reflecting the common law and constitutional right.
The statutory bail regime is underpinned by the fundamental principle that a person charged with a criminal offence is presumed innocent until proven guilty.
The general right to bail is subject to specific statutory exceptions and conditions set out in the relevant ordinance.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Article 87 of the Basic Law as the constitutional foundation for the statutory bail principle.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEE YUEN-FOOK AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and Wong J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge has a duty to provide adequate reasons for their decisions, with the required level of detail depending on the specific context and circumstances of the case.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of evidence by judge alone
principles.paraphrase_en: When a professional judge sitting alone assesses evidence, it can generally be assumed they are aware of the legal elements of the offence, and their reasons for accepting or rejecting evidence should be directed towards those elements.
When assessing evidence, it is usually preferable for a judge to begin by identifying the specific elements of the offence that are in dispute.

relationships.target_case_name: Oriental Daily Publisher Ltd v Commissioner for Television and Entertainment Licensing Authority
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that what constitutes adequate reasons depends on the context and circumstances.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Kong Wai Chun and Others
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that the necessary detail in a judge's reasons depends entirely on the nature of the evidence and issues.
The judgment applies the principle from this case regarding the preferable approach for a judge to take when assessing evidence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KHALIQ SALMAN (aka KHALIA SALMAN)
judges: Before: Hon Zervos Acting CJHC and Macrae VP in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant may be entitled to a sentencing discount for providing assistance to the authorities, including being prepared to give evidence against co-conspirators, even if the prosecution ultimately does not proceed against those individuals based on that evidence.

principles.principle_label: Jury's role in assessing witness credibility
principles.paraphrase_en: Issues concerning the credibility or reliability of a witness's account, including that of a defendant offering assistance, are matters for a jury to resolve, not for the court to pre-judge in sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SHE SUET YEE AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Wilson Chan J in Court

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for ketamine trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: For trafficking in 2,000 grams of ketamine, the sentencing starting point should be at least 18 years' imprisonment; for 3,000 grams, at least 20 years. For quantities exceeding 3,000 grams, the starting point may exceed 20 years, but the sentence increase should not be directly proportional to the drug quantity increase.

principles.principle_label: Hierarchy and role in drug trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's position in the hierarchy of a drug trafficking operation is a relevant sentencing factor. A defendant who recruits others, instructs them on operational matters (such as renting premises), and determines their remuneration occupies a higher level of responsibility.

principles.principle_label: Consecutive sentences for separate drug batches
principles.paraphrase_en: When offenses involve wholly separate and different batches of drugs stored at different locations, the principle of imposing consecutive sentences is appropriate, subject to the overall totality of the sentence.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing guidelines and starting points for ketamine trafficking established in Chan Ka Yiu.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MAU KA PO
judges: Before: Hon S T Poon J

principles.paraphrase_en: When determining an appropriate sentence in drug cases, the court may fairly base its calculation on the quantities of drugs seized, applying any inferences or assumptions in a manner that favors the defendant.

When imposing a sentence consecutive to an existing term, the court must consider the overall gravity of the offence and apply the totality principle to ensure the total sentence is just and proportionate.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG FUNG
judges: Before: Hon Poon CJHC, Zervos JA and Barnes J in Court

principles.principle_label: Provocation as partial defence to murder
principles.paraphrase_en: Provocation is a partial defence that reduces murder to manslaughter. The prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that the killing was not provoked. If the jury concludes the killing might have been provoked, they must return a verdict of manslaughter, not murder.

The existence of murderous intent (intent to kill or cause really serious bodily harm) is an essential element of murder that must be decided by the jury upon consideration of all evidence, even if the defence does not actively contest it.

principles.principle_label: Judge's duty to direct on alternative verdicts
principles.paraphrase_en: A judge has a general duty to direct the jury on an obvious alternative verdict to ensure an accused is not over-convicted or under-convicted, irrespective of whether the parties request such a direction.

principles.principle_label: Limits on duty to direct on alternative verdicts
principles.paraphrase_en: The duty to direct on an alternative verdict depends on how the defence case was conducted and whether there is sufficient evidence to support an obvious alternative. A direction may be omitted if it would confuse the jury, particularly when the defence is solely focused on a different, specific alternative.

relationships.description: The court distinguishes the application of legal principles on alternative verdicts from Ho Hoi Shing based on the different way the defence case was run in the present matter.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSANG CHEUNG-YAN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and Anthea Pang JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A participant in a joint enterprise can be held liable for murder if the victim's death results from an act carried out within the scope of that enterprise, even if the participant's own specific act did not directly cause the death.

In a joint enterprise, different participants can be convicted of different offences (e.g., murder vs. manslaughter) based on the jury's findings regarding their individual mental states (intent to kill or cause GBH vs. a lesser intent), even if they participated in the same underlying criminal act.

principles.principle_label: Murder by disposal of presumed corpse
principles.paraphrase_en: If a person, intending to kill or cause grievous bodily harm, renders another unconscious, mistakenly believes them to be dead, and then disposes of the body in a manner that causes death, they can be guilty of murder if the initial act and the disposal are part of one continuous course of conduct.

If no joint enterprise is established, each defendant's liability for murder must be assessed individually based on their own conduct and whether they personally had the intent to kill or cause grievous bodily harm, and whether that conduct caused the death.

The offence of assault occasioning actual bodily harm is committed when there is an assault (use or threat of violence without consent) that results in actual bodily harm, which is a lower threshold than causing grievous bodily harm with intent.

relationships.description: The judgment references Osland v R as an example where different verdicts were reached for co-accused in a joint enterprise murder case.

cross_references.query: grievous bodily harm murder intent HKSAR

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG KIE-CHUNG
judges: Before: Hon Anthea Pang J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM CHIU LAU
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG SHUN LAM
judges: Before: Hon Woodcock J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LIANG YAOQIANG (梁耀強)
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Tang NPJ and Mr Justice French NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of Final Appeal will not interfere with the Court of Appeal's exercise of discretion to order a retrial unless there is a serious error of law, principle, or approach, such as failing to consider a relevant factor or considering an irrelevant one.

principles.principle_label: Considerations for retrial - interests of justice
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering whether to order a retrial, the interests of justice include the accused's interests, the public interest in finality, the avoidance of undue delay and oppression, and the public interest in seeing those guilty of serious crimes brought to justice.

principles.principle_label: Attribution of counsel's fault to defendant
principles.paraphrase_en: Whether fault or tactical decisions by defence counsel should be attributed to the defendant depends on the facts of each case; attribution is not automatic, particularly when there is no evidence the defendant was party to the decision.

Under section 4 of the Homicide Ordinance, where there is evidence of provocation causing loss of self-control, the question of whether the provocation was enough to make a reasonable person 'do as he did' must be left to the jury, who must consider everything done and said.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing discount for guilty plea to manslaughter
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant who has consistently offered to plead guilty to manslaughter and has never sought complete acquittal should receive a full one-third sentencing discount.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HOSSAIN MD IMRAN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Woodcock J

principles.paraphrase_en: A conviction for murder requires proof beyond reasonable doubt that the defendant unlawfully killed another person with malice aforethought, which includes an intention to kill or cause grievous bodily harm.

The offence of child cruelty under section 27 of the Offences Against the Person Ordinance is established when a person wilfully assaults, ill-treats, neglects, abandons, or exposes a child in a manner likely to cause unnecessary suffering or injury to health.
A guilty verdict in a criminal trial for serious offences like murder and child cruelty must be unanimous among all jurors.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG CHUN MAN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and A Pang JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Extended joint enterprise murder mens rea
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal question presented is whether, for a secondary party to be guilty of murder under extended joint criminal enterprise principles, it is sufficient that they foresaw another participant might intentionally cause really serious injury, or whether it is necessary that they foresaw another participant might kill (cause death) with intent to kill or cause really serious injury.

principles.principle_label: Use of co-defendant's guilty plea as evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A legal question is raised regarding whether a jury is entitled to rely on a defendant's own guilty plea to a lesser offence as an admission against interest regarding a material fact in issue in the trial.

principles.principle_label: Use of accomplice's guilty plea as evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A legal question is raised regarding whether a prior guilty plea by an accomplice who testifies for the prosecution can be used by the jury as evidence supporting the prosecution case against a defendant.
Evidence that prosecution witnesses (accomplices) had pleaded guilty to manslaughter in the same case and were awaiting sentence was properly before the jury and was not a matter in dispute.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSANG CHEUNG YAN (曾祥欣)
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Stock NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant appealing on grounds of inconsistent verdicts bears the burden of demonstrating both that the verdicts are factually inconsistent and that the inconsistency is so severe that an appellate court cannot allow the challenged verdict to stand.

principles.principle_label: Joint enterprise with differing mens rea
principles.paraphrase_en: A jury is entitled to return different verdicts for co-defendants in a joint enterprise if it finds that, while they participated in a common plan, their individual intentions (mens rea) differed.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOW CHING YIN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, McWalters and Zervos JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof in criminal cases
principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution must prove the defendant's guilt beyond a reasonable doubt. If, after considering all evidence, a reasonable doubt exists as to the defendant's guilt, the prosecution has failed to meet its burden and the defendant must be acquitted.

principles.principle_label: Manslaughter by unlawful and dangerous act
principles.paraphrase_en: Manslaughter can be established by proving the defendant committed an unlawful and dangerous act. An act is considered dangerous if all sober and reasonable people would recognize it as such.

The court will generally defer to the reasonable professional judgment of experienced defence counsel regarding the selection of available defences, unless presented with evidence that such judgment was unreasonable or improper.

The mere presence or association of multiple individuals in a group does not establish a conspiracy to commit a crime. Individual responsibility must be proven separately, and some group members may be prosecution witnesses rather than co-conspirators.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN CHI SHING
judges: Before: Hon Wilson Chan J

principles.paraphrase_en: The offense of attempted murder requires proof of an act done with the specific intent to kill, going beyond mere preparation toward the commission of murder.
A guilty plea entered at the earliest opportunity in committal proceedings is a significant mitigating factor to be considered in sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. VALENCIA BERNIE MACATANGAY
judges: Before: Hon Tam J in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI KAN YAU
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Lies as evidence of murderous intent
principles.paraphrase_en: Subsequent lies told by a defendant do not necessarily demonstrate an intent to kill or cause grievous bodily harm; they may simply indicate a desire to distance oneself from a death for which one is responsible.
Lies told by a defendant do not inherently tend to show that the defendant was not acting under provocation.

principles.principle_label: Proper direction on lies in murder cases
principles.paraphrase_en: A jury should be directed that lies can support a prosecution case of murder only if the jury is sure the lies were told to conceal the fact of murder, rather than merely to conceal a connection with the death.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Taylor regarding the proper jury direction on the significance of lies in a murder case.
The judgment cites Richens in relation to the issue of lies and provocation.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI KAN YAU
judges: Before: Hon Yau J

principles.paraphrase_en: The actus reus of murder is established when the defendant's unlawful act causes the death of the victim, as demonstrated by the fatal wound being the direct cause of death.
The mental element for murder requires the prosecution to prove the defendant had the intention to kill or cause grievous bodily harm at the time of the act.
Confessions made by a defendant under caution in video-recorded interviews and during re-enactments are admissible as evidence against them, provided proper procedures are followed.

principles.principle_label: Evidence - expert opinion on victim's state
principles.paraphrase_en: Expert evidence from a pathologist and toxicologist regarding the victim's likely state of consciousness at the time of the attack, based on post-mortem findings and toxicology reports, is admissible and relevant to the circumstances of the killing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN MAN SUM IVAN
judges: Before: Hon Wong J

principles.paraphrase_en: When a jury returns a verdict of manslaughter based on provocation rather than murder, the sentencing judge must proceed on that factual basis and assess culpability accordingly.

Sentencing for manslaughter requires consideration of the whole circumstances, including the facts revealed by evidence, the background and circumstances leading to the incident, the defendant's personal background, and any mitigation.

principles.principle_label: Irrelevance of prior convictions on retrial
principles.paraphrase_en: Previous convictions from trials that were subsequently overturned on appeal have no bearing on the assessment of the defendant's culpability in the current proceeding.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWAN TAT YEE
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, D Pang and Zervos JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A jury must be directed to disregard a confession if they find it was or may have been obtained involuntarily, under oppression, or in circumstances likely to render it unreliable, even if they believe the confession is true.

In a murder trial, the prosecution bears the burden of proving the killing was not a result of provocation. The defendant is guilty of murder only if the jury is sure of the requisite intent and concludes the killing was not, and may not have been, due to provocation.

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof and defence evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution must prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt. A jury must be directed that even if they do not positively believe the defence evidence, they cannot find against the accused on an issue if that defence evidence leaves them in reasonable doubt about it.

When evidence of a prosecution witness conflicts with that of a defence witness, a judge must direct the jury that preferring the prosecution evidence does not, by itself, conclude the issue of guilt. The jury must still consider whether the prosecution has proved its case beyond reasonable doubt.

A jury must be directed that a defendant's decision not to give evidence should not be held against them, and they must decide the case based on whether the prosecution evidence proves guilt beyond reasonable doubt, while considering the defence evidence.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles from Mushtaq regarding the necessity of directing a jury to disregard confessions obtained in oppressive or unreliable circumstances.

The judgment applies the principle from Liberato that a jury must be directed that preferring prosecution evidence over defence evidence does not automatically satisfy the burden of proof beyond reasonable doubt.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSIM SUM-KIT, ADA
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and A Pang JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for diminished responsibility
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal burden of proving the partial defence of diminished responsibility rests on the defendant, and this imposition is constitutionally valid and sound in principle.

principles.principle_label: Diminished responsibility as an exception, not an element
principles.paraphrase_en: The defence of diminished responsibility does not require the defendant to disprove an element of the offence of murder, but to establish a special exception or excuse. The prosecution must still prove all elements of murder beyond reasonable doubt.

principles.principle_label: Test for justifying a reverse legal burden
principles.paraphrase_en: To determine if a reverse onus provision is justified, the court considers whether it encroaches on the presumption of innocence and, if so, whether that modification is proportionate. A key factor is whether the burden relates to an essential element of the offence or to a special defence or exception.

principles.principle_label: Presumption of innocence and special defences
principles.paraphrase_en: The presumption of innocence under Article 6(2) of the ECHR is directed at the offence charged. Imposing a legal burden on a defendant to prove a special defence like diminished responsibility is less objectionable as it does not alter the core ingredients of the offence that the prosecution must prove.

relationships.description: The judgment follows the analysis in R v Foye, holding it highly instructive on the validity of placing a legal burden on the defendant to prove defences like insanity or diminished responsibility.
The judgment follows R v Wilcocks in rejecting constitutional challenges to the legal burden of proof for diminished responsibility.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that upholds the legal burden on a defendant to prove insanity, analogously supporting the burden for diminished responsibility.
The judgment applies the test from this case to assess whether a reverse burden of proof is justified within reasonable limits.

relationships.target_case_name: R v Director of Public Prosecutions, Ex p Kebilene
relationships.description: The judgment cites Lord Hope's comments from this case regarding factors to balance when assessing reverse onus provisions.

relationships.target_case_name: The People (Director of Public Prosecutions) v Heffernan
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that raising diminished responsibility does not negate any element of the prosecution's case for murder.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI KAN YAU
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KHAW KIM SUN
judges: Before: DHCJ Keith

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG FUNG (王豐)
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ and Mr Justice Bokhary NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: For a murder conviction, the prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that the defendant was not acting under provocation. The defense succeeds if there is a reasonable possibility that the defendant lost self-control due to an unforgivable provocation, even if that scenario is not proven to have occurred.

The jury does not need to find that provocation definitely occurred, but only that it might have occurred. If the evidence raises a reasonable possibility of provocation, the prosecution must exclude that possibility to secure a murder conviction.

The overall circumstances of the killing, including the scene's condition, the nature of injuries, and the method of infliction, must be considered when determining whether provocation might have occurred. Frenzied or uncontrolled violence may support the possibility of loss of self-control.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LIMBU MACHINDRA
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and A Pang JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When directing a jury on the mental element for a crime of specific intent like murder, judges should generally avoid elaborating on the meaning of intent. A modified direction from Woollin may be used where necessary, instructing the jury that if they are sure the defendant foresaw death or serious harm as a virtual certainty from their voluntary act, they may find the necessary intent.

principles.principle_label: When a Nedrick/Woollin direction is not required
principles.paraphrase_en: A Nedrick or Woollin direction is not called for in every murder case. It is not required where the defendant's primary desire or motive was clearly to cause injury to the victim, and the sole question for the jury is whether they intended to cause really serious bodily harm.

In a case where the defendant's intoxication is relevant, the jury should be directed that if they believe the defendant was so drunk as to be incapable of forming the intent to kill or cause really serious bodily harm, they must acquit of murder. However, a drunken intent is still an intent if the jury is sure it was formed.

relationships.description: The judgment discusses the so-called Nedrick direction on inferring intent, which was subsequently modified.
The judgment adopts the modification from Woollin, notably substituting 'find' for 'infer' in the direction on intent.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SO HON TO, STEPHEN
judges: Before: Hon D’Almada Remedios J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG KIE CHUNG
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The term 'substantial' in the context of diminished responsibility is an ordinary English word that imports a question of degree for the jury to determine. It does not mean 'more than minimal' or 'more than trivial'; a merely trivial impairment is insufficient to establish the defence.

Ordinarily, a trial judge is not required to provide a detailed legal definition of the word 'substantial' to the jury, as it is an ordinary word they can understand. A direction should only be given if there is a risk the jury has misunderstood its import, in which case they should be told that a more than merely trivial impairment is insufficient.

Where the evidence only shows some 'partial impairment' of mental responsibility, a judge is not obliged to direct the jury that, as a matter of law, such partial impairment may be capable of supporting a defence of diminished responsibility.

principles.principle_label: Withdrawal of murder charge - rebutting diminished responsibility
principles.paraphrase_en: A judge has the power to withdraw a charge of murder from the jury at the close of evidence if there is no rational or proper evidential basis for the prosecution to rebut the defence of diminished responsibility. This is an application of the principle that juries may only convict on evidence capable of supporting a conviction.

principles.principle_label: Primacy of jury on diminished responsibility
principles.paraphrase_en: Whether an accused's mental responsibility was substantially impaired is ultimately a question for the jury to determine. The trial judge must exercise great caution and respect the jury's role as the primary decision-maker on this issue.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows Golds in holding that 'substantial' is an ordinary English word and that judges should not ordinarily define it for the jury.
The judgment applies the reasoning from David Squelch, which itself applied Golds, regarding when a direction on the meaning of 'substantial' is required.
The judgment cites Brennan for the principle that a murder charge may be withdrawn if there is no rational basis to rebut defence expert evidence on diminished responsibility.
The judgment cites Khan (Dawood) as authority supporting a judge's power to withdraw a murder charge from the jury in relation to a contested diminished responsibility defence.
The judgment cites Yeung Kuen Chi by analogy to explain why courts avoid over-explaining simple words like 'substantial' to juries.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWAN TAT YEE (關達儀)
judges: Before : Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Gleeson NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: A Liberato direction, which instructs a jury they must acquit if they believe the defence evidence or think it may be true, is only appropriate when the evidence in question is genuinely exculpatory. It should not be given for evidence that is merely supportive of the defence case but not conclusive.

When evidence conflicts, a judge must direct the jury that simply preferring prosecution evidence over defence evidence does not, by itself, discharge the prosecution's burden of proof. The prosecution must prove its case beyond reasonable doubt based on evidence the jury accepts, regardless of whether they reject the defence evidence.

Competing expert opinion evidence is neither true nor false in an absolute sense; it is opinion. Such evidence may be supportive of a party's case but is not conclusive. A jury should not be directed that they must accept a particular verdict if they think an expert opinion may be correct.

The purpose of a Liberato direction is to prevent the jury from confusing their evaluation of the credibility of evidence (who to believe) with the resolution of the legal issue of whether the prosecution has proved its case beyond reasonable doubt.

principles.principle_label: When a Liberato direction is required
principles.paraphrase_en: A Liberato direction is not called for in every case involving conflicting evidence. It is only required where, if the jury believes or thinks the defence evidence might be true, they would be obliged to acquit the defendant of the charged offence.

relationships.description: The judgment applies and clarifies the principles from Liberato regarding when a specific jury direction is necessary concerning defence evidence.
The judgment cites Johnson v Western Australia to note a potential shortcoming in using a template Liberato direction and to endorse a preferable formulation.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEE WAI MAN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, M Poon and A Pang JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A complete judicial direction on diminished responsibility must be given in accordance with the Specimen Directions in Jury Trials. Failure to provide the full direction, particularly regarding the effect of voluntary consumption of substances on the defense, constitutes a misdirection.

An offender who rejects a plea offer and proceeds to trial relying on a defense that, if successful, would result in a complete acquittal, is not normally entitled to the full one-third sentencing discount. Instead, a lesser discount should be applied.

relationships.description: The court finds this older judgment of very little assistance for the present sentencing considerations.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN KWAI SANG
judges: Before: Hon Woodcock J

principles.principle_label: Provocation as partial defense to murder
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant who kills another may be convicted of manslaughter rather than murder if they acted under provocation, meaning they lost self-control due to a sudden and temporary loss of self-control caused by things said or done.
For provocation to be left to the jury, there must be evidence that the defendant was provoked to lose self-control and that a reasonable person might have reacted similarly.
Provocation can arise from a cumulative series of acts or a course of conduct over time, culminating in a final triggering incident that causes the defendant to snap.
It is for the jury to decide, based on all the evidence, whether the defendant was provoked and whether a reasonable person might have been so provoked.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAW WAI MAN
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple offenses arising from related incidents, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is just and proportionate, considering the entire course of conduct rather than simply adding individual sentences.
A timely guilty plea warrants a reduction in the overall sentence imposed, reflecting the utilitarian value of saving court time and resources, and demonstrating remorse.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ARIF AQIB AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Yau J

principles.paraphrase_en: A person can be convicted of murder under common law as part of a joint enterprise if they participate in an attack with others, knowing that the attackers are armed with lethal weapons like knives and intend to use them, even if they do not personally inflict the fatal injuries.

principles.principle_label: Accessorial liability for wounding with intent
principles.paraphrase_en: A person can be convicted of wounding with intent under section 17(a) of the Offences Against the Person Ordinance as part of a joint criminal enterprise if they assist or guide attackers to the scene, knowing the attackers are armed with weapons and intend to cause grievous bodily harm.

principles.principle_label: Common law murder and statutory punishment
principles.paraphrase_en: Murder is an offence under common law, but its punishment is provided by statute, specifically section 2 of the Offences Against the Person Ordinance.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHIU CHUNG YUEN
judges: Before: Hon Woodcock J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG MAN KONG
judges: Before: Hon Johnny Chan J

principles.principle_label: Murder - intent to kill or cause grievous bodily harm
principles.paraphrase_en: A conviction for murder requires proof that the defendant, at the time of the act, intended either to kill the victim or to cause them grievous bodily harm.

Evidence of premeditation, such as purchasing a weapon and changing clothes to avoid recognition, is relevant to establishing the defendant's state of mind and intent prior to the commission of the offence.
The history of the relationship between the defendant and the victim, including a deterioration from friendship to hostility, can provide context for the defendant's motive and actions.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TAM HO NAM (譚浩南)
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ and Mr Justice Lam PJ

principles.paraphrase_en: A trial judge must not misdirect a jury by creating the impression that they must accept the defendant's evidence as true in order to find a defense like provocation established. The jury should be directed to consider all evidence, including the defendant's account, even if they do not fully believe it.

For a ground of appeal based on alleged misdirection to succeed, it must be reasonably arguable that the directions created a real danger of the jury misunderstanding the legal test. Mere repetition of arguments rejected by the Court of Appeal is insufficient unless it demonstrates the lower court's analysis was arguably wrong.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principles from Liberato regarding proper jury directions when evaluating defense evidence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. RAHMAN MD. RAIHAN
judges: Before: Hon Toh J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish the offence of criminal intimidation under section 24(a)(i) of the Crimes Ordinance, the prosecution must prove that the accused threatened another person with injury, with intent to cause alarm to that person or to cause that person to do or omit to do any act.

principles.principle_label: Elements of assault occasioning actual bodily harm
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish the offence of assault occasioning actual bodily harm under section 39 of the Offences against the Person Ordinance, the prosecution must prove an assault that resulted in actual bodily harm to the victim.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. GURUNG NIRAJ
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An action constitutes more than mere preparation for entering premises as a trespasser when it involves physically testing or manipulating a security mechanism (like a digital door lock) with the intent to gain unauthorized entry, especially when external barriers are not secured.
A defendant's own admission can provide sufficient evidence to establish the specific intent to steal required for the offense of burglary.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. K.D.D.H.
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEE WAI MAN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, M Poon JA and A Pang JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant cannot rely on the effects of voluntarily consumed alcohol to establish the defence of diminished responsibility, as the law holds individuals responsible for the effects of alcohol they choose to drink.

The existence of some voluntary intoxication does not automatically remove the defence of diminished responsibility; the jury must consider the broader evidence and whether the defendant's condition overrode their control.
The jury must take a broad common-sense view of all the evidence to determine whether alcohol consumption was voluntary or compelled by the defendant's condition.

relationships.description: The judgment aligns with the House of Lords' view that voluntary intoxication should not completely exclude the defence of diminished responsibility.
The judgment applies the principle from Wood that the law on voluntary intoxication and diminished responsibility is not absolute or 'black and white'.
The judgment applies the ratio from Stewart, clarifying that voluntary intoxication does not automatically negate the defence of diminished responsibility.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SIU WAI CHEONG
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and A Pang J in Court

principles.principle_label: Provocation requires causal link to loss of control
principles.paraphrase_en: For the defense of provocation to arise, the jury must find that the alleged provoking conduct actually caused the defendant to lose self-control.
When assessing whether an omission in jury directions on provocation constitutes an error, the court considers whether the omitted words could have caused conceivable prejudice to the defense.

principles.principle_label: Intoxication and specific intent for murder
principles.paraphrase_en: The jury must decide whether the defendant had the specific intent to kill or cause grievous bodily harm at the time of the act, even if intoxicated; if intoxication prevented formation of that intent, the defendant is not guilty of murder.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG KIE CHUNG
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI CHEUNG CHOI
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Lord Hoffmann NPJ

principles.principle_label: Hearsay nature of statements to experts
principles.paraphrase_en: Statements made by a defendant to an expert witness are admissible only as evidence of the factual basis for the expert's opinion, not as evidence of the truth of those statements.
In Hong Kong, the judiciary cannot create new exceptions to the hearsay rule; this is a matter for the legislature.

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of expert opinion with disputed foundation
principles.paraphrase_en: In a jury trial, where a defence like diminished responsibility is raised, it is rarely appropriate for a judge to rule an expert opinion inadmissible merely because its factual basis is disputed, as this would improperly withdraw the defence from the jury's consideration.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for diminished responsibility
principles.paraphrase_en: The burden of proving the partial defence of diminished responsibility lies on the defendant and must be discharged by admissible evidence.

principles.principle_label: Medical history not a hearsay exception
principles.paraphrase_en: Medical history or background information about an accused's mental condition does not fall within any common law exception to the hearsay rule.

relationships.description: The judgment affirms the Court of Appeal's holding in Kissel regarding the hearsay nature of statements to experts and the exclusion of one expert's opinion within another's report.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Wai Man & Others v HKSAR
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Bokhary PJ's caution against the judiciary creating new hearsay exceptions.
The judgment applies the same stance taken by Ribeiro PJ that the legislature, not the judiciary, creates hearsay exceptions.

relationships.target_case_name: Myers v Director of Public Prosecutions
relationships.description: The judgment cites the majority principle from Myers regarding the judicial role in relation to hearsay.
The judgment notes that the House of Lords in Blastland followed the principle from Myers regarding hearsay.
The judgment references that Kissel cited Ramsay v Watson for the principle that a defendant's statements to an expert are not evidence of their truth.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEE MAN LOK
judges: Before: Hon Kwok J in court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may impose a Hospital Order under section 45 of the Mental Health Ordinance as the most suitable disposal method after considering the nature of the offence, the defendant's character and background, psychiatric evidence, and other available sentencing options.

Where psychiatric evidence establishes that a defendant was suffering from a severe mental illness at the time of the offence, and that the illness substantially impaired their mental responsibility, rational judgment, and self-control, this is a significant mitigating factor in sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LIU KA KI
judges: Before: Hon Wilson Chan J in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TAM WAI MAN STEPHANIE
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing, courts should primarily compare the present case to past authorities involving factually similar circumstances, such as the killing of one's own child, and may find cases with dissimilar circumstances less helpful.

principles.principle_label: Relevance of factual similarity in sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: A key factor in determining the relevance of a past sentencing authority is the similarity of the method and circumstances of the offense, such as a premeditated plan involving suicide, administering sedatives, and causing death by carbon monoxide poisoning.

relationships.description: The present judgment finds the circumstances of the killing in Lee Fung Yee to be similar to the present case for the purpose of comparative sentencing analysis.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YANG LEYING
judges: Before: Hon Kwok J in court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may impose a hospital order under section 45 of the Mental Health Ordinance when, after considering the nature of the offense, the defendant's character and background, psychiatric evidence, and other available sentencing options, it determines this to be the most suitable disposal method.

Psychiatric evidence regarding a defendant's mental state, insight into illness, compliance with treatment, and need for supervised care is a relevant factor in determining the appropriate sentencing disposition.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG PO YAN
judges: Coram: Hon Pang JA, M Poon JA and Andrew Chan J in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for insanity defense
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal burden of proving the defense of insanity rests on the defendant, based on the presumption that every person is sane and responsible for their crimes.

principles.principle_label: Presumption of innocence and prosecution burden
principles.paraphrase_en: In criminal cases, the general burden of proof remains on the prosecution due to the presumption of innocence protected by the Basic Law and the Hong Kong Bill of Rights Ordinance.
The presumption of sanity applies to both the general issue of guilt and any special issues concerning the defendant's mental state.

principles.principle_label: Reverse burden and presumption of innocence
principles.paraphrase_en: Imposing a reverse legal burden of proof on the defendant regarding a defense may infringe upon the defendant's right to the presumption of innocence.
The prosecution is not limited to medical evidence to prove a defendant's mental state; it may rely on witness testimony, recorded interviews, and other relevant evidence.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for its discussion on the burden of proof for the defenses of insanity and diminished responsibility.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI CHEUNG CHOI
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, McWalters and Zervos JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Evidential threshold for suicide pact defence
principles.paraphrase_en: For the partial defence of suicide pact to be left to the jury, there must be sufficient evidence that the deceased had a settled intention of dying and was a willing party to a common agreement to die together. Speculative possibility is insufficient.

principles.principle_label: Admissibility of psychiatric evidence based on unproven history
principles.paraphrase_en: Expert psychiatric opinion based on a patient's history may be admissible even if the factual foundation of that history is not independently proven, though its weight may be affected. Exclusion is not mandatory in such circumstances.

principles.principle_label: Non-hearsay use of statements to experts
principles.paraphrase_en: Statements made to a psychiatrist are admissible as non-hearsay when their relevance lies solely in the fact they were said (to explain the basis of the expert's opinion), not in proving the truth of the facts asserted within them.

relationships.description: The judgment references this Australian ruling in the context of arguments about the admissibility of an accused's statements to psychiatrists.
The court disagrees that the principle from this case on non-hearsay evidence has the broad application argued by counsel, confining it to its specific context.
The judgment applies the two categories of admissibility for expert evidence based on patient history established in this Australian authority.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG MAN KONG
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An expert witness is permitted to give opinion evidence on matters within their specialized field, whereas ordinary witnesses are generally restricted to testifying about facts they directly observed or heard.
The jury is not obliged to accept an expert witness's opinion, especially if the factual basis for that opinion conflicts with the jury's own findings of fact.
Factors indicating a planned, determined, and persistent attack, followed by further violence, can support a finding that the defendant did not lose self-control.
Leave to appeal must be refused if the proposed ground of appeal is not reasonably arguable.
An applicant has the right to renew a refused leave application to the Court of Appeal, but the court may order loss of time spent in custody if the renewal is unjustified.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG MAN YI MANDY
judges: Before: Hon Yeung VP, D Pang JA and M Poon J in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for insanity defence
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal burden of proof (persuasive burden) is placed on the defendant who raises the defence of insanity. This is an exception to the general principle that the prosecution bears the burden of proving guilt.
Everyone is presumed in law to be sane and to possess sufficient mental faculties to be held criminally responsible for their actions.

principles.principle_label: Proportionality of reverse onus for insanity
principles.paraphrase_en: Imposing a persuasive burden on a defendant to prove the defence of insanity is a reasonable and proportionate restriction on the right to be presumed innocent.
cited_statutes: Article 11(1) of the Hong Kong Bill of Rights Ordinance

principles.principle_label: General burden of proof in criminal cases
principles.paraphrase_en: The fundamental principle in criminal prosecutions is that the burden of proving the defendant's guilt rests on the prosecution.

relationships.description: The judgment affirms the principle from Woolmington that the prosecution bears the general burden of proof in criminal cases.
The judgment applies the exception established in M’Naghten’s case, which places the burden of proof for insanity on the defendant.
The judgment follows the Hong Kong precedent set in Lam Kwong Choi, which imposed a persuasive burden on a defendant raising insanity.
The judgment cites Chaulk to support the conclusion that reversing the onus for insanity is a reasonable restriction on the presumption of innocence.
The judgment cites Heffernan, which concurred with analyses that the reverse burden for insanity satisfies the proportionality test.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM KWOK-WING
judges: Before: Hon D’Almada Remedios J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. BUI VAN-CUONG
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and Anthea Pang JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For the defense of provocation to arise, there must be provoking conduct that caused the defendant to lose self-control. If no such provocation exists, the defendant is guilty of murder.

When assessing provocation, the jury must consider whether a person of ordinary self-control would have reacted as the accused did, taking into account the accused's entire factual situation including history, experiences, background, and personal attributes.
The law of provocation should not indirectly incorporate the concept of diminished responsibility, as these are distinct legal defenses with different requirements.

relationships.description: The judgment references Lord Simon's approach in Camplin regarding consideration of the 'entire factual situation' when assessing provocation.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KWOK WAI YIN
judges: Before: Hon Wong J

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHIU LAI HA
judges: Before: Hon D’Almada Remedios J

principles.paraphrase_en: A hospital order under section 45 of the Mental Health Ordinance may be the most suitable sentence for manslaughter when psychiatric evidence supports it and considering the offender's circumstances, character, and available sentencing options.

When sentencing for manslaughter, the court must consider all relevant circumstances including the nature of the offence, the defendant's character and antecedents, personal mitigation, psychiatric evidence, and alternative sentencing methods.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI CHEUNG CHOI (李長再)
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ and Mr Justice Lam PJ

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge is only required to leave the defence of suicide pact to the jury if there is a sufficient evidential basis to support it.
The established legal principles concerning alternative verdicts govern when a judge must direct a jury on an alternative defence.

relationships.description: The judgment applies established principles concerning alternative verdicts to determine if a suicide pact defence should have been left to the jury.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HO SAI YU
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant charged with murder may be convicted of manslaughter if they plead guilty to manslaughter on the basis of diminished responsibility and the prosecution accepts the plea.

For a homicide conviction, the prosecution must prove that the defendant's unlawful acts caused the victim's death, even if a pre-existing medical condition made the victim more vulnerable to the fatal injuries.
A defendant's statement made under police caution, where they admit to the act and provide a motive, is admissible as evidence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. FUNG CHUN KWAI
judges: Before: Hon Wilson Chan J in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG MEI TAK
judges: Before: Hon Toh J

principles.paraphrase_en: A jury's verdict of manslaughter on the grounds of diminished responsibility is accepted as the factual and legal basis for sentencing, shifting the focus from murder to the defendant's impaired mental responsibility.

The court may consider psychiatric and psychological reports to understand the defendant's background and character, including the absence of a stereotypical criminal disposition, as part of the sentencing exercise.

Expert assessments of a high risk of violent recidivism, based on factors like unpredictable violent outbursts, lack of warning signs, and inability to manage anger, are relevant considerations for sentencing and risk management.
Evidence that a defendant retains a definite ability to exert self-control when choosing to do so is a pertinent factor for the court to evaluate in the sentencing context.

In sentencing for a violent offence, the court treats the quick escalation of grievances into extreme violence, coupled with limited remorse and a lack of empathy towards the victim, as indicators of high risk and seriousness.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LIU SIU TJIN
judges: Before: Hon Toh J

principles.paraphrase_en: The court may approve the prosecution's acceptance of a plea to manslaughter instead of murder when psychiatric reports establish a basis of diminished responsibility under the relevant ordinance.

A long-standing diagnosis of paranoid schizophrenia, characterized by symptoms such as suspiciousness and referential delusions, can form the factual and medical foundation for a finding of diminished responsibility.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HO PO KWONG
judges: Before: Hon Yau J

principles.principle_label: Murder requires proof of specific intent
principles.paraphrase_en: To secure a murder conviction, the prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that the defendant unlawfully killed the victim with the specific intent to kill or cause grievous bodily harm.

principles.principle_label: Diminished responsibility as a partial defence
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant may raise the defence of diminished responsibility, arguing that their mental responsibility for the killing was substantially impaired by an abnormality of mind, which if accepted, reduces murder to manslaughter.
A defendant may raise the defence of provocation, arguing that they were provoked to lose self-control, which if accepted, reduces murder to manslaughter.
It is for the jury to determine, based on all the evidence, whether the prosecution has proven the requisite intent for murder.

principles.principle_label: Time limit for CFA leave application
principles.paraphrase_en: An application for leave to appeal to the Court of Final Appeal must be filed within 28 days from the date of the Court of Appeal's decision.

An application for certification of a point of law by the Court of Appeal should be made immediately after its judgment is delivered, as delay may hinder compliance with the time limit for applying for leave to appeal to the Court of Final Appeal.

principles.paraphrase_en: When assessing provocation, the jury must first determine if the defendant was provoked, then evaluate the gravity of that provocation from the defendant's perspective considering all circumstances and any personal characteristics affecting its impact, and finally decide whether an ordinary person with normal self-control would have reacted as the defendant did.

principles.paraphrase_en: A Liberato direction to the jury is required only when the case turns on choosing between the credibility of the prosecution's account and the defendant's account.
The Liberato direction does not apply to situations involving inconsistent statements by a defense witness or conflicting evidence presented by the defense itself.

principles.paraphrase_en: If evidence is relevant and admissible, and the jury is entitled to consider it and assign appropriate weight, it is generally artificial to withhold the document itself from the jury unless specific prejudicial content is identified.

principles.principle_label: Judicial discretion to exclude prejudicial evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: The court retains discretion to exclude a document from the jury if it contains actually prejudicial material that outweighs its probative value.
The burden lies with the party seeking to exclude a document to provide evidence or submissions demonstrating specific prejudice.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEN XUEPING

principles.paraphrase_en: A plea of guilty to manslaughter based on the defence of diminished responsibility under the Homicide Ordinance can be accepted by the prosecution in satisfaction of a murder charge.

For the defence of diminished responsibility to apply, the defendant must have been suffering from an abnormality of mind (such as a depressive disorder) arising from an underlying condition, which substantially impaired their mental responsibility for the acts leading to the killing.

When sentencing for manslaughter on grounds of diminished responsibility, the court should consider calling for psychiatric and other expert reports to assess the defendant's mental state and appropriate sentencing options.

A hospital order under the relevant ordinance is a recommended sentencing option where psychiatric reports indicate it is appropriate for a defendant convicted of an offence linked to mental disorder.

principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of Appeal retains discretion to decide whether an application for leave to appeal against conviction or sentence should continue or abate following the death of the applicant/appellant, and must consider all relevant factors in exercising that discretion.

principles.principle_label: Factors for continuing appeal after death
principles.paraphrase_en: When exercising discretion regarding an appeal after an appellant's death, the court should consider factors set out in relevant authorities such as Cheng Chee Tock Theodore and Smith.
The court must ensure that any continued appeal proceeds on a proper adversarial basis, with proper legal representation and argument of the issues.
The willingness of another party to be substituted as appellant is relevant if the court considers substitution necessary for the appeal to continue.
The court may consider the availability of legal aid to ensure appropriate legal representation for a continued appeal after an appellant's death.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows and applies the approach established in Tang Tik Hung regarding appeals after an appellant's death.
The judgment applies factors set out in Cheng Chee Tock Theodore when exercising discretion on continuing appeals after death.
The judgment applies factors from Smith as helpful guidance for exercising discretion on appeals after death.

principles.paraphrase_en: A jury may only draw an inference against a defendant if it is the only reasonable inference that can be drawn from the proven facts. If there is a reasonable inference in the defendant's favor as well as an adverse one, the adverse inference must not be drawn.
A party may not cross-examine their own witness unless the court finds the witness is hostile, meaning the witness is not desirous of telling the truth at the instance of the party calling them.

If a witness shows themselves to be decidedly adverse, the trial judge has a discretion at common law to allow them to be cross-examined, and has discretion over the mode of examination to best serve the purposes of justice.

principles.principle_label: Prohibition on knowingly calling hostile witness for inadmissible statement
principles.paraphrase_en: It is wrong in principle for the prosecution to knowingly call a witness who is hostile for the purpose of putting before the jury a prior statement that is itself inadmissible to prove facts against the accused.

The test for applying the proviso to dismiss an appeal despite an error is whether a reasonable and properly instructed jury, acting on the evidence properly before it, would inevitably have convicted.

relationships.description: The judgment references statements of principle from this case concerning inferential reasoning in criminal trials.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that it is wrong to knowingly call a hostile witness to put an inadmissible prior statement before the jury.
The judgment follows the holding from this case regarding the judge's discretion to allow cross-examination of a decidedly adverse witness.
The judgment distinguishes the present case from Henderson, which involved controversial expert evidence as the sole determining factor of guilt.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI WUN YIN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae Acting CJHC, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Prosecuting counsel has a duty to conduct cross-examination responsibly and properly, and it is contrary to fundamental fairness to contest a matter known to be true or supported by substantive evidence.
A jury must be directed not to assume a defendant's knowledge or participation in a conspiracy based solely on their spousal relationship with a co-conspirator.
A defendant cannot be found guilty of conspiracy simply because they were lazy or negligent in their duties as a company director; specific knowledge and participation must be proven.
Cross-examination is a crucial part of the adversarial process, allowing parties to test the veracity and reliability of evidence and to assess witness credibility.

relationships.description: The judgment references Lyttle for the proposition that counsel's wide discretion in cross-examination is constrained by a duty to act responsibly and properly.

principles.paraphrase_en: The doctrine of proportionality operates at the constitutional level in Hong Kong to determine the permissible limits of restrictions on rights guaranteed by the Basic Law and the Hong Kong Bill of Rights.

principles.principle_label: Judicial acts not subject to constitutional review
principles.paraphrase_en: Judicial acts of the HKSAR courts are not subject to constitutional review for inconsistency with entrenched rights, as the courts exercise independent judicial power under the Basic Law.
The first stage in a constitutional challenge is to determine whether the impugned measure engages a constitutionally protected right; if it does not, the challenge fails at this stage.
The outcome of a proportionality analysis depends on the specific measure and context, with challenges to different rules relying on the same protected right potentially leading to different results.

relationships.target_case_name: Kwok Wing Hang v Chief Executive in Council
relationships.description: The present judgment notes that the Court of Final Appeal in Kwok Wing Hang disagreed with the Court of Appeal's proportionality assessment and held certain offences to be proportionate and valid.

relationships.target_case_name: Chiang Lily v Secretary for Justice
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Chiang Lily that certain powers vested by the Basic Law are not constitutionally reviewable.

relationships.target_case_name: Tam Sze Leung v Commissioner of Police
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from Tam Sze Leung that a constitutional right is not engaged if the impugned act is not attributable to a public authority.

relationships.target_case_name: Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Ltd v New World Development Co Ltd & Others
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an example where a right was not engaged because the body in question was not a 'court'.

relationships.target_case_name: Medical Council of Hong Kong v Helen Chan
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an example where a practice was held not to infringe the right to an independent and impartial tribunal.

relationships.target_case_name: Kong Yunming v Director of Social Welfare
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an example of a successful rule challenge on proportionality grounds where a policy change was found to lack a legitimate aim.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LO LAI CHAU
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge is only required to give a self-defence direction to the jury if there is a proper evidential foundation for it in the case. The absence of any suggestion that the defendant was acting in self-defence means no such direction is necessary.

principles.principle_label: Jury direction on guilty plea to lesser offence
principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant has pleaded guilty to a lesser offence like manslaughter, but is on trial for a greater offence like murder, the judge must carefully consider what, if anything, to tell the jury about that plea, based on the evidential foundation.

A precedent concerning jury directions based on a single piece of circumstantial evidence is distinguishable where the factual circumstances are entirely different, such as when the defence at trial provided an alternative innocent explanation for the conduct in question.

relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes Nguyen Anh Nga because it involved different circumstances where the defendant's reaction was the sole circumstantial evidence and the defence offered an innocent explanation.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LO LAI CHAU
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge is not required to leave the issue of self-defense to the jury when the defense case at trial was not presented on that basis and there is no evidence to support it.

principles.principle_label: Disclosure of guilty plea to jury
principles.paraphrase_en: Mentioning a guilty plea to manslaughter to the jury during closing submissions is a tactical decision by the defense, not a consequence of a judicial ruling on the availability of a self-defense plea.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from a Privy Council case that a self-defense direction is not required if the issue was not raised at trial and lacks evidential support.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN MAN SUM IVAN
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, McWalters JA and Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When evidence exists upon which a jury could find provocation, counsel for either the Crown or the defendant has a duty to bring it to the judge's attention before the summing-up. The judge must then consider if the evidence justifies leaving the issue of provocation to the jury, and if so, is statutorily required to do so.

principles.principle_label: Threshold for leaving provocation to jury
principles.paraphrase_en: The judge must first determine if there is any evidence from which a reasonable jury could conclude the defendant was actually provoked to lose self-control. If no such evidence exists, the judge should not leave provocation to the jury. If such evidence exists, the issue must be left for the jury's consideration.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of evidence for loss of self-control
principles.paraphrase_en: The judge's responsibility in assessing whether to leave provocation to the jury is to examine all the evidence, taking it at its most favourable to the defendant, and ask whether the jury might conclude he had lost self-control. The defendant's own account is not conclusive; the judge must consider the totality of the evidence.

principles.principle_label: Judge's role on provoking conduct evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: If there is evidence on which a jury could find there was provoking conduct, the judge's role is limited to assessing the factual possibility of such conduct. It is not for the judge to determine whether the accused was in fact provoked by that conduct; that is a matter for the jury.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the Privy Council's reasoning on the judge's limited role when evidence of provoking conduct exists.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TAM HO NAM
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and M Poon JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant gives evidence, the jury must be directed that they can act on the defendant's evidence even if they only consider it might be true, not only if they find it to be true. The failure to give this direction creates a risk the jury might wrongly think they must accept the defendant's evidence as true to find a defence established.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of jury directions as a whole
principles.paraphrase_en: When evaluating whether a required direction (like the Liberato direction) was properly given, the summing-up must be viewed in its entirety to determine if the essential message was effectively conveyed to the jury.
A jury can only consider and act on a defendant's admissions made in a video-recorded interview if they are satisfied both that the admissions were made voluntarily and that they are true.

Hong Kong trial judges retain a broad discretion and a wide margin of assessment in deciding whether to give a good character or propensity direction to the jury, and are not bound by an absolute obligation to do so as in some other jurisdictions.

For the partial defence of provocation, which reduces murder to manslaughter, the prosecution bears the burden of proving beyond reasonable doubt that the killing was not a result of provocation. The defendant does not have to establish that it was.

relationships.description: The judgment references the principle from Jim Fai v HKSAR that a simple comparison of a defendant's accounts without proper direction may offend established legal principles.
Cited alongside Jim Fai v HKSAR for the principle that comparing a defendant's accounts without more may be problematic.
Referred to for the principle that the adequacy of a jury direction depends on whether it contains all required elements, not necessarily its specific wording.
Cited for the holding that Hong Kong judges have broad discretion regarding character directions and are not bound by the English Vye obligation.
The judgment notes that the obligation imposed on English judges by Vye to give good character directions does not apply to Hong Kong courts.
The judgment indicates the Court of Final Appeal in Pang Hiu San approved the common law position as stated in R v Mushtaq.

principles.paraphrase_en: Evidence of an accused's conduct after an offence does not lose its probative value merely because it is equally consistent with multiple inferences or alternative explanations. Such evidence can still be submitted for the jury's consideration, provided a proper caution is given regarding its use.
It is generally unnecessary for a judge to give a jury any special direction on how to approach circumstantial evidence.
A decision or course of action taken by defence counsel at trial, which represents a reasonable forensic choice available to them, will not typically found a basis for appeal.

An appellate court will not engage in speculation about whether a different forensic strategy by trial counsel (such as seeking an advance ruling) would have produced a different outcome, as such arguments are based on multiple layers of hypothetical scenarios.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this Canadian case for the proposition that post-offence conduct evidence may have no probative value if equally explained by consciousness of guilt for multiple offences where culpability for one is admitted.
The judgment cites this subsequent Canadian Supreme Court case which explained the circumstances warranting a specific direction to a jury regarding post-offence conduct.
The judgment applies the principle from this case, reinforced by Bokhary NPJ, that special jury directions on circumstantial evidence are normally unnecessary.
The judgment applies the reasoning from this Australian case to reject an argument based on counsel's failure to seek a preliminary ruling, dismissing it as speculative.

principles.paraphrase_en: A parent commits the offense of wilful ill-treatment or neglect of a child when they fail to provide adequate and proper medical treatment over a period, in a manner likely to cause the child unnecessary suffering or injury to health.
A conviction requires the prosecution to prove its case against the defendant beyond a reasonable doubt.
A court may find a witness not credible or reliable based on an assessment of their evidence, and such a finding can be supported by specific points from the testimony.

principles.principle_label: Fact-finding based on totality of evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A court's factual findings, including those relating to the elements of an offense, must be made by considering the totality of the evidence as a whole.
