principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of burglary under Hong Kong law requires entering a building as a trespasser with intent to commit theft, or having entered as a trespasser, actually stealing property.

principles.principle_label: Admission of facts in guilty plea
principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant pleads guilty, the court accepts the summary of facts admitted by the defendant as the factual basis for sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHOW WAN HI

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for joint enterprise
principles.paraphrase_en: The prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt either an agreement between co-accused concerning the commission of an offense or that the accused acted in concert with others.

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof in criminal cases
principles.paraphrase_en: A conviction requires the prosecution to prove its case beyond reasonable doubt; mere evidence suggesting possible involvement is insufficient.

principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits burglary under section 11(1)(b) of the Theft Ordinance when they enter a building as a trespasser and steal property therein.
A defendant's admission under caution, including in video-recorded interviews, can constitute sufficient evidence to establish the facts of the offense.

principles.principle_label: Conviction on plea and agreed facts
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant can be convicted based on their guilty plea and agreement to a revised summary of facts.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for handling stolen goods
principles.paraphrase_en: A starting point of 3 years' imprisonment may be appropriate for handling stolen goods where the offender has a significant criminal record and the handling occurs very shortly after the theft.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing for handling unrelated stolen goods
principles.paraphrase_en: A starting point of 12 months' imprisonment may be appropriate for handling stolen goods that are unrelated to other offences for which the offender is being sentenced, even when the goods are of moderate value.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an example where the Court of Appeal upheld a 3-year starting point for handling stolen goods.

principles.paraphrase_en: The standard baseline sentence for domestic burglary is three years' imprisonment, which inherently accounts for a degree of premeditation and planning.
The baseline sentence for burglary can be increased for professional criminals who execute meticulously planned burglaries involving high-value property.
A sentencing discount may be granted if the offender's mental illness contributed to the offence, reflecting their diminished culpability due to an abnormal state of mind.
The standard sentence for unlawful remaining in Hong Kong is 15 months' imprisonment for a first offender pleading guilty.

principles.principle_label: Consecutive sentences for burglary and unlawful remaining
principles.paraphrase_en: The sentence for burglary should generally be consecutive to the sentence for unlawfully remaining in Hong Kong, with adjustments permitted under the totality principle.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Sim Ka Wing that the same sentencing approach applies to both domestic and non-domestic burglary.
The judgment follows the Court of Appeal's ruling in Chiu Peng Richard regarding sentencing discounts for offenders with mental illness.
The judgment applies the established rule from Lau Pang that sentences for burglary and unlawful remaining should be consecutive.

principles.paraphrase_en: For a first offender committing a single offence of burglary of domestic premises, the appropriate sentencing starting point is three years' imprisonment.

principles.principle_label: Lowering starting point for opportunistic burglary
principles.paraphrase_en: The conventional sentencing starting point for burglary can be reduced in cases involving an opportunistic burglar, similar to a sneak thief who enters an open premises to steal available items.

principles.paraphrase_en: For burglary under section 11(1)(a) of the Theft Ordinance, the prosecution must prove the defendant entered a building or part of a building as a trespasser with the intent to commit theft.
Entering a market stall without the owner's consent or lawful authority constitutes entering as a trespasser for the purpose of burglary.
Theft requires the dishonest appropriation of property belonging to another with the intention of permanently depriving the other of it.
CCTV footage and eyewitness observations can provide direct evidence of the defendant's actions and movements during the alleged offence.
When a defendant pleads guilty, the court accepts the prosecution's summary of facts as the basis for conviction, provided no material dispute exists.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN HIU KIN

principles.paraphrase_en: The offense of burglary under section 11 of the Theft Ordinance requires entering a building as a trespasser with the intent to commit theft, inflict grievous bodily harm, or do unlawful damage within the building.
Using a document on a vehicle with intent to deceive, contrary to section 111 of the Road Traffic Ordinance, involves displaying false registration plates to mislead identification.
A defendant's admission to a summary of facts provides the factual basis for sentencing, including details of the offense and circumstances.
In sentencing, the court must consider personal mitigation factors such as the defendant's personal circumstances, employment, family situation, and expressions of remorse.
principles.paragraph_hrase_en: In sentencing, the court must consider personal mitigation factors such as the defendant's personal circumstances, employment, family situation, and expressions of remorse.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WANG BAO

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish burglary under section 11(1)(b) of the Theft Ordinance, the prosecution must prove the defendant entered a building or part of a building as a trespasser and stole anything therein.
A person enters as a trespasser for the purpose of burglary if they enter premises without permission or authority from the lawful occupier.
The offence of theft requires proof of dishonest appropriation of property belonging to another with the intention of permanently depriving the other of it.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SINGH LAIK
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Peony Wong

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for non-domestic burglary
principles.paraphrase_en: The established sentencing guideline for burglary of non-domestic premises is a starting point of 30 months' imprisonment, which already accounts for a certain level of planning and premeditation.

A sufficient degree of planning and premeditation for sentencing purposes is established when a defendant, upon seeing an opportunity, decides to equip themselves with a tool specifically for the purpose of committing burglary, even if the overall plan is not highly sophisticated.

A defendant's extensive record of previous convictions for dishonesty-related offences, including burglary or attempted burglary, constitutes an aggravating feature in sentencing for a new burglary offence.

principles.principle_label: Aggravating factor: Offence soon after release
principles.paraphrase_en: Committing an offence of the same nature a very short time after release from a prison sentence for similar offences is a further aggravating factor, indicating the previous sentence's failure to deter the defendant.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing guideline from this case, which sets a 30-month starting point for burglary of non-domestic premises.
The present judgment applies the principles from this case regarding the identification of aggravating factors for burglary offences.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI MING LEONG
judges: Before: Hon D’Almada Remedios J

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for a new offense, the court must consider the totality of the criminal conduct and the cumulative effect of sentences, especially when the defendant is already serving a sentence for a separate offense. The court can order part of a new sentence to run consecutively and part concurrently to achieve an appropriate overall sentence.

principles.principle_label: Consecutive vs concurrent sentencing for separate offenses
principles.paraphrase_en: When offenses are separate and different in nature, even if committed close in time, the default position is that sentences should run consecutively. However, the court has discretion to adjust this to reflect the overall criminality.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WAN WAI FUNG

principles.paraphrase_en: The offense of burglary is established when a person enters a building as a trespasser with intent to commit theft, or having entered as a trespasser, actually steals or attempts to steal property.
A defendant's personal circumstances, including financial hardship, family responsibilities, health issues, and recent bereavement, may be considered as mitigating factors in sentencing.
A guilty plea is a significant mitigating factor that should be taken into account when determining sentence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. REN MINGHU
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Casewell

principles.paraphrase_en: The established sentencing guideline for burglary or attempted burglary of a residential building is a starting point of 3 years' imprisonment after trial for a defendant with a clear record and no aggravating or mitigating factors.

principles.principle_label: Departure from tariff for external access
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may justify a lower sentencing starting point than the standard tariff where the defendant only accessed external scaffolding and did not enter or attempt to enter the residential units, as this is less likely to cause alarm or a confrontation within a home.
A defendant's plea of guilty entitles them to a one-third discount from the sentencing starting point adopted by the court.
An aggravating factor in sentencing for burglary can exist where the defendant entered Hong Kong with the sole purpose of committing the burglary.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites this Court of Appeal case as authority supporting a departure from the normal sentencing tariff where the defendant only accessed external scaffolding.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NAVNEET SINGH

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for opportunistic burglary
principles.paraphrase_en: A lower sentencing starting point may be appropriate for a burglary characterized as opportunistic, particularly where the offender is akin to a 'sneak thief' rather than engaging in more serious planned criminal activity.

relationships.description: The present judgment references Cheung To Ming as authority supporting a lower sentencing starting point for opportunistic burglary resembling sneak theft.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. MUHAMMAD SHEHBAZ (also known as MUHMMAD SAHBAZ and MUHMMAD SHAMBAZ)
judges: Before: HH Judge Dufton

principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of burglary under Hong Kong law requires entry into a building as a trespasser with intent to commit theft, or having entered as a trespasser, actually stealing property.
A defendant's personal circumstances, including family ties, health conditions, and family support, are relevant factors that the court must consider when determining an appropriate sentence.
The motive behind committing an offence, such as financial desperation due to a family member's medical needs, may be presented in mitigation and considered by the court during sentencing.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSE LOK LAI, TONY AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: HH Judge Dufton

principles.paraphrase_en: The offence of burglary under section 11 of the Theft Ordinance requires entry into a building as a trespasser with intent to commit theft, or having entered as a trespasser, actually stealing or attempting to steal.

Multiple defendants can be jointly charged with burglary when they participate together in the unlawful entry and theft, with their individual roles (such as lookout or active entry) constituting participation in the joint enterprise.

principles.principle_label: Admission of facts in guilty plea
principles.paraphrase_en: When defendants plead guilty, the summary of facts admitted by them forms the factual basis for sentencing and establishes the particulars of the offences without need for trial evidence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HUI YEE LUNG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Casewell

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSE CHUN WAH AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: HH Judge A. J. Woodcock

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge has the discretion to exclude a voluntary video-recorded interview from evidence based on the circumstances of its acquisition, even if it was given voluntarily.

A procedural breach in conducting a video-recorded interview (such as repeating a prior statement) does not automatically require exclusion; the court must consider whether the breach caused harm or prejudice to the defendant.

principles.principle_label: Mens rea for joint enterprise burglary
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's knowledge and participation in a joint burglary can be inferred from their conduct, including their attire, actions of searching and damaging property inside a closed shop to find valuables, and the overall circumstances.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WUN SHU FAI (aka WAN SHU FAI)
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Ip

principles.paraphrase_en: The use of a specific tool (glass-hammer) to break into premises indicates some planning, but the overall execution must be sufficiently sophisticated to justify a sentence enhancement on that basis alone.

Committing an offence within a short period (less than five months) after release from custody is an aggravating factor in sentencing, particularly when the offender has an extensive and relevant criminal record.

A defendant's extensive previous convictions for offences of dishonesty (such as multiple thefts and burglaries) demonstrates a lack of deterrence and is relevant to sentencing for a new similar offence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. GADARA JASPAL SINGH
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple offences arising from separate cases, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final aggregate sentence reflects the offender's overall criminality and is not crushing.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing range for theft and burglary
principles.paraphrase_en: The starting points for individual sentences for theft and burglary offences must be consistent with established sentencing ranges for offences of that kind.
An appellate court will not interfere with a sentence unless it is wrong in principle or manifestly excessive.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case as authority for the established sentencing range for theft and burglary offences.
The present judgment cites this case as authority for the established sentencing range for theft and burglary offences.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. PINTO
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP and Zervos JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For the offense of burglary, the prosecution must prove the defendant entered a building as a trespasser, which requires the building to be private property belonging to another person and not a common area.

When sentencing for burglary, the court may consider the low value of stolen items if the offender was interrupted before completing the intended theft, but the potential for greater loss in an occupied building compared to an abandoned one remains a relevant factor.
A trespass is not considered merely opportunistic if it occurs in an occupied building rather than a vacant one, affecting the assessment of the offense's seriousness.

relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes the facts from Chau Man Ying because the building here was occupied and the trespass was not opportunistic.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG WAI KIT

principles.paraphrase_en: For a defendant with no prior criminal record convicted after trial of non-domestic burglary, the sentencing guideline establishes a starting point of 30 months' imprisonment.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHUNG HIU TUNG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge K Lo

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's personal motive, such as committing offenses to financially support another person, is not a valid mitigating factor when the defendant is an adult with prior legal experience and made a conscious choice to commit the crimes.

principles.principle_label: Mitigation - remorse and guilty plea discount
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's expression of remorse is generally considered to be already accounted for within the standard one-third sentencing discount granted for a guilty plea.

When sentencing for multiple offenses, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the aggregate sentence is just and proportionate to the overall criminality, considering factors like total loss, duration of offending, and whether the offender is persistent.
A court may treat a defendant's claimed determination to reform with reservation and skepticism if it is inconsistent with the defendant's past criminal record.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Ng Wing Ki Winky and another

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WU TIN LONG, MIKE

principles.paraphrase_en: Courts must generally impose an immediate custodial sentence for commercial fraud offenses, even for first-time offenders, unless exceptional circumstances exist.

principles.principle_label: Factors determining sentence length for fraud
principles.paraphrase_en: The length of a custodial sentence for commercial fraud is determined by multiple factors including the nature, duration, and sophistication of the fraud, the offender's role, its market impact, cross-border implications, and the risk to victims' property interests.

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. KONG CHI-KIU
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos JA and M Poon JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The Court of Appeal's power to review sentences is intended to resolve disputes over sentencing principles and to correct significant errors in the exercise of sentencing discretion, not to intervene merely because of minor differences in starting points.

When an offender commits a completely different type of offence from other charges, and the offence involves an additional element such as breach of trust, the sentence for that offence should normally be ordered to run consecutively, or at least partly consecutively, to the sentences for the other offences, subject to the totality principle.
A breach of trust in a sentencing context typically refers to a situation where the offender, occupying a position of trust, abuses that privileged position to steal from or defraud others.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Cheng Tsz Hin
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case regarding the proper scope of appellate review of sentences.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Chan Kim Chung Nelson
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority on a point of sentencing law.
The judgment cites this case as a comparative example involving fraud and money laundering charges.
The judgment cites this English case as authority for the common law definition of 'breach of trust' in sentencing.
The judgment cites this unreported Hong Kong case for an opinion on when a situation may not be strictly considered a 'breach of trust'.

candidates.case_name: SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE v. CHUNG PUI-KIT, BILLY
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP, Zervos and A Pang JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Courts should not set rigid sentencing guidelines for internet fraud due to the wide variety of such crimes, but a key sentencing purpose is deterrence, both specific and general, which typically requires an immediate custodial sentence.

A higher degree of sophistication and persistence in committing a fraud, compared to a simple version of the offence, is an aggravating factor that should be reflected in an increased starting point for sentence.

principles.principle_label: Clear record and sentencing starting point
principles.paraphrase_en: An offender's clear record should not be used to determine the appropriate starting point for a sentence; the starting point should reflect the inherent seriousness of the offence.

principles.principle_label: Discount for guilty plea and good character
principles.paraphrase_en: The mitigating value of an offender's good character is typically subsumed within the standard one-third discount given for a timely guilty plea.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the principle that a clear record should not be used to determine the sentencing starting point.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI SUET YING
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Casewell

principles.paraphrase_en: For commercial fraud cases, established precedent indicates that an immediate custodial sentence is generally appropriate even for first-time offenders, absent exceptional circumstances.
The length of a sentence for fraud is determined by considering factors including the nature, duration, and sophistication of the fraud, the offender's role, and the impact on the victim.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM SEE CHUNG STEPHEN

principles.paraphrase_en: The limitation on aggregate sentences imposed by a District Court judge under section 82(2)(a) of the District Court Ordinance applies only when sentences are imposed by the same judge on the same occasion.

principles.principle_label: Prosecutorial discretion in charging separate cases
principles.paraphrase_en: The fairness and propriety of the prosecution bringing separate cases in the District Court, rather than a single case, is a matter that can be subject to legal challenge.
The totality principle in sentencing aims to achieve a just and balanced overall sentence that does not punish the offender disproportionately for their total offending.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing for preying on the community
principles.paraphrase_en: Where an offender preys upon the community, the sentence should seek to protect the community from their actions, which may at times require a severe sentence.

relationships.description: The present judgment accepts and applies the legal principle established in Wong Wan Shan regarding the District Court's sentencing jurisdiction.
The judgment in Wong Wan Shan, which is followed here, cited Yuen Shek On as authority for the limitation on aggregate sentences.
The judgment in Wong Wan Shan, which is followed here, also cited Chan Fook Hung on the same sentencing jurisdiction point.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LI KA WING
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Amy Chan

principles.paraphrase_en: A significant delay between arrest and charge that causes the defendant a substantial period of uncertain suspense can constitute a valid mitigating factor in sentencing.
When offences are committed on separate and distinct occasions, the sentences for them should, in principle, be ordered to run consecutively.

Even where consecutive sentences are appropriate, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final aggregate sentence is not excessive and properly reflects the overall criminality of the case.
A defendant's family difficulties or the ill health of a family member, while a personal concern, does not automatically constitute a valid mitigating factor that warrants a sentence reduction.

In assessing whether prosecution delay is unreasonable, the court will consider the complexity of the investigation, including factors such as overseas elements, multiple arrests, and the need to seek legal advice, and may find no unreasonable delay exists.

relationships.description: The judgment references the principle from this case regarding delay causing uncertain suspense as a potential mitigating factor.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHENG MING LIM
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Ivy Chui

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant may be found to have received a benefit from fraudulent activity even if they do not hold direct ownership or employment in the company that received the proceeds, particularly where there are close personal relationships and the defendant was aware of policies prohibiting conflicts of interest.

Receipt of a company staff handbook containing policies against operating competing businesses or creating conflicts of interest can establish that a defendant was fully aware of such prohibitions, which is relevant to assessing their conduct in fraud-related matters.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. FOK TSZ HIN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Colin Wong

principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits attempted fraud when they, with intent to defraud, engage in a course of conduct that is more than merely preparatory to the commission of the full offence of fraud, even if the intended victim becomes suspicious and the scheme is interrupted.

judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Casewell in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court should not depart from established sentencing guidelines unless there are specific and justifiable mitigating or aggravating circumstances present in the case.
A one-third reduction in sentence is the appropriate discount for a timely guilty plea.

When sentencing for unlicensed driving, the court should consider the absence of an accident or dangerous driving as a relevant mitigating factor, but the lack of an urgent need to drive is not a mitigating factor.
The totality principle must be applied when ordering sentences for multiple offences to be served consecutively, to ensure the final aggregate sentence is just and proportionate.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSEA WAI HUNG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Bernard Chung

principles.paraphrase_en: For commercial fraud offenses, an immediate custodial sentence is the standard starting point, even for first-time offenders, unless exceptional circumstances exist.

principles.principle_label: Factors determining sentence length for fraud
principles.paraphrase_en: The length of a custodial sentence for commercial fraud is determined by factors including the nature, duration, and sophistication of the fraud, the offender's role, its market impact, cross-border implications, and the risk to victims' property interests.
When offenses are separate and distinct, involving different victims and occurring on different dates, the sentences for them should generally be ordered to run consecutively.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WAN KWUN HO
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge W. H. Ko

principles.principle_label: Elements of obtaining property by deception
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish the offense of obtaining property by deception under section 17 of the Theft Ordinance, the prosecution must prove that the defendant dishonestly obtained property belonging to another with the intention of permanently depriving that other of it, and that the obtaining was achieved through a deception.

A deception involves a false representation, whether express or implied, made by words or conduct, that is known or believed to be false by the person making it, or is made recklessly as to its truth.
The deception must operate on the mind of the victim and be a significant cause of the victim's decision to part with the property. The victim's action (or inaction) must be induced by the deception.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites the facts of Peter Raymond Baird as an illustrative example of a scheme involving deception and the inducement of inaction to obtain property.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHEUNG KWOK HUNG
judges: Before: HH Judge A. J. Woodcock

principles.principle_label: Sentencing for fraud targeting gullible victims
principles.paraphrase_en: Fraudulent schemes that specifically prey on gullible victims warrant heavy and deterrent sentences, typically starting at four years' imprisonment or more.
The court must determine an appropriate starting point for sentencing that reflects all relevant factors before considering mitigation or aggravation.
When sentencing for fraud, the court must consider factors including the offender's role and involvement, the duration of the fraudulent scheme, and the total financial loss suffered by victims.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Tsoi Shu and Others
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing principle from Tsoi Shu that frauds preying on gullible victims attract heavy deterrent sentences starting at four years or more.

judges: Before: Deputy District Judge M Chow in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple offenses across different cases, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final combined sentence reflects the defendant's overall criminality and is just and proportionate.

principles.paraphrase_en: An adult who is legally represented when making an immigration application is expected to know, or to have been advised, that they must keep their passport safe and must produce genuine documents when applying for an extension of stay.

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof for forgery allegations
principles.paraphrase_en: When a party alleges forgery, they bear the burden of adducing evidence of sufficient cogency to raise the issue, taking into account the inherent improbability of serious misconduct. The standard from Re H & Others applies by analogy, requiring evidence commensurate with the seriousness of the allegation.

principles.principle_label: Inference from circumstantial evidence in forgery cases
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a challenge to the genuineness of a signature is based solely on circumstantial evidence, any inference of forgery must be properly grounded in primary facts. The court must avoid conjecture and only draw compelling inferences sufficient to overcome the inherent improbability of serious misconduct.

The court must critically assess forensic handwriting expert evidence, including considering challenges to the expert's methodology, such as whether comparative analysis of stroke proportions is arbitrary or meaningful.
In determining whether a document is forged, the court must consider the inherent probabilities, all expert evidence, and the absence of direct evidence explaining the document's creation.

relationships.target_case_name: Re H & Others (Minors) (Sexual Abuse: Standard of Proof)
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the standard of proof principle from Re H by analogy to allegations of forgery, requiring evidence of commensurate cogency given the seriousness of the misconduct.

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for possession of an offensive weapon, there is no fixed sentencing guideline; the court must consider all relevant circumstances, including the type of weapon, the potential injury it could cause, and the defendant's purpose for possessing it.
The court must apply the totality principle when determining sentence, ensuring the final sentence reflects the defendant's overall culpability and is just and proportionate.

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof for forgery allegations
principles.paraphrase_en: The party alleging forgery bears the burden of proving it with evidence of a cogency commensurate with the inherent improbability of such serious misconduct, applying the standard from Re H by analogy.

principles.principle_label: Inference from circumstantial evidence in forgery cases
principles.paraphrase_en: Inferences of forgery drawn from circumstantial evidence must be logically and reasonably justified by the primary facts found; compelling inferences are required to overcome the inherent improbability of serious misconduct.

principles.principle_label: No duty to explain document contents before execution
principles.paraphrase_en: There is generally no duty on a lender or the borrower's solicitors to explain the contents of a loan or security document to the borrower prior to its execution.

principles.principle_label: Effect of principal debtor clause in guarantee
principles.paraphrase_en: A principal debtor clause in a guarantee renders the guarantor liable as a primary debtor, not merely a secondary surety, and can validate the guarantee even if the underlying contract with the principal debtor is unenforceable.

principles.principle_label: Conclusive evidence clauses in banking documents
principles.paraphrase_en: A certificate signed by a duly authorised bank officer stating amounts due under a loan or guarantee is conclusive evidence of the debt if the contract contains a conclusive evidence clause.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the standard of proof from Re H by analogy to allegations of forgery, requiring cogent evidence commensurate with the seriousness of the allegation.

The primary purpose of disqualifying an offender from driving is forward-looking and preventive, aiming to protect future road users from a person who has demonstrated they pose a real risk, rather than being a backward-looking punitive measure.

When sentencing for multiple charges, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is appropriate and not excessive for the defendant's total criminal conduct.
