principles.paraphrase_en: The plaintiff bears the burden of proving their case on the balance of probabilities; if the cause of an incident remains in doubt, the plaintiff fails to discharge that burden.

The court is not bound by the mere issuance of sick leave certificates; they are only one piece of evidence that must be evaluated in light of all available evidence, and the court must form its own opinion based on the totality of the evidence.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case in support of the proposition that if the cause of an incident remains in doubt, the plaintiff fails to discharge the burden of proof.

relationships.target_case_name: Tam Fu Yip Fip v Sincere Engineering & Trading Company Limited
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that sick leave certificates are not binding and must be evaluated alongside all evidence.

relationships.target_case_name: Rai Surya Prakash v Pacific Crown Security Services Ltd & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as further authority for the principle that the court must evaluate sick leave certificates based on the totality of evidence.

candidates.case_name: ACTIN TRADING LTD v. THE INCORPORATED OWNERS OF CHIU TAT FACTORY BUILDING AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Tracy Chan in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The plaintiff must prove both factual causation (that the defendant's act caused the damage) and legal causation (that the damage was a reasonably foreseeable consequence of the defendant's act).
The standard of care required is what is reasonably expected in all the circumstances; a plaintiff cannot impose a standard that exceeds this reasonable expectation.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for breach of statutory duty
principles.paraphrase_en: The plaintiff bears the burden of proving, on the evidence, that the defendant was in breach of a statutory duty imposed by ordinances such as the Building Management Ordinance.
Damages claimed must be of a kind that was reasonably foreseeable to the defendant; a blanket percentage surcharge on all items may be considered unforeseeable.

A court cannot rely on a damage report to find that items were damaged and determine the extent of damage if the report's findings are not substantiated by clear evidence, such as photographs showing the alleged damage.

candidates.case_name: HUANG WEIHONG v. TAO KEE ENGINEERING COMPANY LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Leung J in Chambers

candidates.case_name: CHAN PAK YUEN v. LAM MAN KIT
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Leung in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A report from a treating doctor is admissible to prove factual matters such as the fact of treatment, the nature of the treatment provided, the diagnosis reached, the treatment plan recommended, and that these were communicated to the patient. These are considered matters of fact, even though they involve the doctor's expertise. However, opinions expressed in such a report beyond these factual matters are not admissible as expert evidence.

In personal injury actions, a party may warn an opposing party about the risk of incurring incidental costs if they adduce evidence, such as expert reports, without the required leave or in contravention of a court order.

candidates.case_name: LAW SIN HA v. CONFIDENTIAL MATERIALS DESTRUCTION SERVICE LTD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Charles Wong in Court

principles.principle_label: Surveillance evidence value in psychiatric cases
principles.paraphrase_en: Surveillance footage generally has limited utility in proving the genuineness of a claimant's psychiatric impairments, but may be relevant in specific circumstances where it captures behavior directly related to the claimed symptoms.
For an injured employee to receive compensation for temporary incapacity, it is a precondition that the incapacity results from the injury; the employee bears the burden of establishing causation.
The statutory presumption in section 10(2) of the Employees' Compensation Ordinance relates only to the certified period of absence necessary, not to the cause of the absence.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of recurring bonuses as earnings
principles.paraphrase_en: Discretionary bonuses or incentives may be considered part of an employee's earnings under the Employees' Compensation Ordinance if they exhibit a recurring nature and pattern over time.

principles.principle_label: Medical Board's consideration in Form 9 review
principles.paraphrase_en: When a Medical Board issues a Form 9 review assessment certifying a period of sick leave different from a prior Form 7, it is inferred that the Board has taken into account its reviewed diagnosis, including the reasonableness of sick leave for the certified impairment.

relationships.target_case_name: Ale Thak Prasad v Tsang Chin Keung & Another
relationships.description: The judgment references this case in the context of discussing the subjective nature of PTSD symptoms and the reliance of treating doctors on a patient's reported symptoms.

relationships.target_case_name: Tse Lai Sing v Tung Wah Group of Hospitals
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case alongside Ale Thak Prasad regarding the assessment of subjective psychiatric symptoms.

relationships.target_case_name: Lai Yuen Hing v Lo Chi Hung & Another
relationships.description: This case is cited as authority for the principle that an injured employee must establish causation between injury and incapacity to receive compensation.

relationships.target_case_name: LKK Trans Ltd v Wong Hoi Chung
relationships.description: The judgment references this case in a comparative discussion concerning the statutory presumption under section 10(5) of the Employees' Compensation Ordinance.

candidates.case_name: B K AMRIT v. G-CLADDS LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Marlene Ng J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Appellate review of case management decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will not lightly interfere with a case management decision made by a master or judge, especially concerning expert evidence arrangements, unless there are good reasons to do so.

principles.principle_label: Oral expert evidence as case management decision
principles.paraphrase_en: The decision on whether to require oral expert evidence at trial is a pre-eminently case-sensitive case management matter, and limited reliance should be placed on other case authorities when making this determination in a specific case.

principles.principle_label: Guiding principle for oral expert evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: The guiding principle for allowing oral expert evidence at trial is whether it is necessary in the interests of justice for the proper and just resolution of the case, bearing in mind the function of expert evidence.

Causation is a matter to be determined by the judge using a common-sense approach and applying the civil standard of proof (balance of probabilities) to the medical expert evidence; it is not to be determined by the medical experts themselves, who may apply a different standard.

relationships.target_case_name: Chau Chin To Chadow v Wing Fung Financial Group Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment refers to this case as containing relevant legal principles on expert evidence, which Master Roy Yu noted in the lower court proceedings.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheung Oi Yan Ruby v Wong Hoi Sum
relationships.description: The judgment cites observations by Kwan JA on the potential difficulties of resolving expert disagreements based on joint reports alone without oral evidence.
The judgment cites this authority where oral expert evidence was deemed necessary on a key liability issue where experts continued to disagree in their joint statement.

relationships.target_case_name: Lee Kin-kai, a patient by his father and next friend Li Wah v Ocean Tramping Co Ltd t/a Ocean Tramping Workshop
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that causation is for the judge to determine using common sense and the balance of probabilities standard, not for medical experts.

relationships.target_case_name: Chan Shek Ho v Shiu Ho-chi & ors
relationships.description: The judge notes holding similar views on causation in this prior case.

candidates.case_name: TAIPING TRUSTEES LTD v. BOSC INTERNATIONAL COMPANY LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon B Chu J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: Expert evidence is admissible and necessary to assist the court in determining highly technical factual issues, particularly where the court would be reluctant to find a breach of professional duty without evidence on the standards within that profession.

An appellate court will only intervene in a case management decision from a lower court if the decision was based on an error of principle or was clearly wrong, as the appellant faces a very high hurdle to demonstrate such an error.

principles.principle_label: De novo hearing for master's appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: On an appeal from a case management decision of a master to a judge in chambers, the judge conducts a de novo hearing, treating the matter as if it came before him for the first time.
Expert evidence should not be admitted if the question can be determined based on factual evidence and the inferences drawn from it, and where expert evidence would not be helpful.
Questions posed to an expert should generally avoid asking for an answer to the ultimate issue the court must decide, unless it is necessary and appropriate in the specific circumstances of the case.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Kar Gee Mimi v Severn Villa Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles reiterated in this case regarding the high threshold for appellate intervention in case management decisions.

relationships.target_case_name: Peace Mark (Holdings) Ltd v Chau Cham Wong Patrick
relationships.description: The judgment cites this unreported case for the principle that expert questions should generally avoid answering the ultimate issue for the court.

relationships.target_case_name: Formosa Taffeta Co Ltd v Banque Indosuez
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the same principle concerning the framing of expert questions to avoid the ultimate issue.

candidates.case_name: ICBC INTERNATIONAL WEALTH INVESTMENT LTD v. ROLLCASH INVESTMENTS LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Norman Nip SC in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The principle from Canada Steamship Lines Ltd v The King, which requires clear and express language for an indemnity clause to protect a party from the consequences of its own negligence or wrongful acts, does not apply where the party seeking indemnity is not alleged to have been negligent or to have committed a wrongful act.

The concept of 'proximate cause' as developed in insurance law, which seeks to identify the direct, dominant, and efficient cause of a loss for coverage purposes, is not generally applicable or helpful in interpreting contractual provisions outside the specific context of insurance contracts.
The test for implying a term into a contract for business efficacy is not one of absolute necessity, but rather whether, without the term, the contract would lack commercial or practical coherence.

relationships.target_case_name: Canada Steamship Lines Ltd v The King
relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes Canada Steamship by finding its principle on interpreting indemnity clauses for negligence inapplicable where no negligence or wrongful act is alleged.

relationships.target_case_name: Gillespie Brothers & Co Ltd v Roy Bowles Transport Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites Gillespie Brothers for its explanation, referencing Canada Steamship, of the improbability that a party intends to absolve another from the consequences of its negligence.

relationships.target_case_name: Smith & Anr v South Wales Switchgear Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites Smith v South Wales Switchgear for its holding, in the context of indemnity clauses, that it is even more improbable a party would intend to indemnify another against the consequences of the other's negligence.

candidates.case_name: TSUI YUEN (formerly known as HO WAI HUNG) v. HO TSE WAI, PHILIP LI & PARTNERS (A FIRM) (formerly known as HO, TSE & WAI & PARTNERS)
judges: Before: Hon Yuen, Barma & G Lam JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A deliberate and informed act by a third party, intended to exploit a situation created by the defendant's breach of duty, does not break the chain of legal causation if the defendant's duty was specifically imposed by law to guard against that very type of act.

relationships.description: The present judgment adopts the House of Lords' reasoning on causation where a defendant breaches a duty to guard against a specific deliberate act.

candidates.case_name: IP KIT LEUNG v. CHAN SZE LAM trading as WANG CHUN ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION DEVELOPMENT COMPANY
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Kenneth KY Lam in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff cannot recover damages for injuries caused solely by their own unreasonable and negligent actions, where no other person is legally responsible.
The determination of liability in negligence cases is highly dependent on the specific facts of each case, and precedents may not be directly applicable to factually distinct scenarios.

A court may order costs on a full indemnity basis against a plaintiff where the action was clearly without merit and should never have been commenced, resulting in a waste of the defendant's costs and judicial resources.

relationships.target_case_name: Sayers v Harlow Urban District Council
relationships.description: The court distinguishes the present case, finding no resemblance because here the plaintiff's injury resulted solely from his own unreasonable acts.

relationships.target_case_name: Sit Kan Tat v World-Class Talent Limited
relationships.description: The court finds this and other cited cases unhelpful for the present scenario, which involves a plaintiff harming himself by his own acts.

candidates.case_name: CHEUNG HING v. ROTH & SONS ESTATES MANAGEMENT LTD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Bonnie Cheng in Open Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court cannot find a specific cause of an accident more probable than not based on lay descriptions of noise and water volume alone, without expert evidence to rule out other possible causes.

principles.principle_label: Scope of duty of care for omissions
principles.paraphrase_en: The scope of a defendant's duty of care in cases of omission is defined by the specific risk of harm the defendant had a duty to protect the claimant against, and a lack of foreseeability of that specific risk weighs against finding such a duty.
A party may owe a duty of care under the neighbourhood principle to users of a common area, which can include an obligation to regularly maintain pipes to prevent risks of harm.

When assessing contributory negligence, the court considers whether the plaintiff failed to take reasonable care for their own safety, with the apportionment reflecting the degree of that failure in the circumstances.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Meadows v Khan that the scope of duty for omissions is defined by the specific risk of harm the defendant was obliged to guard against.

candidates.case_name: CHAN PAT HUNG EVA suing by her attorney, PUN SAU YUK v. WONG HAU KI
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Teresa Wu in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for water leakage
principles.paraphrase_en: The claimant bears the legal burden to prove, on the balance of probabilities, that water leakage originated from the defendant's property. There is no presumption it comes from the flat above, and the defendant is not required to identify the true source or prove it did not come from their property. The doctrine of res ipsa loquitur does not apply where multiple potential sources exist.
An occupier of land 'continues' a nuisance if, with actual or presumed knowledge of its existence, they fail to take reasonable steps to end it within a reasonable time.

The duty owed between neighbours is determined by what is fair, just, and reasonable in all circumstances. This includes considering the foreseeable risk, available preventive measures, their costs, and the parties' resources. The standard is what a reasonable person in the defendant's position would have done, making the label of nuisance or negligence largely insignificant.

The claimant must prove the defendant's wrongful conduct caused the claimed damage. This requires establishing both factual causation (using the 'but for' test) and legal causation (considering whether any intervening act broke the chain of causation).

Where a known problem like water leakage will not resolve itself, it is reasonably foreseeable that failure to cooperate in taking proper remedial measures will cause the damage to persist and result in continuing loss.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the House of Lords principle that an occupier continues a nuisance if they fail to take reasonable steps to end it after knowing or being presumed to know of it.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Ping Chun v Giant King Corporation Limited
relationships.description: The judgment cites this unreported District Court case as an example where a defendant was held liable in negligence for failing to take steps to rectify water seepage.

candidates.case_name: FUNG CHI HUNG v. YUN KWAN CONSTRUCTION ENGINEERING LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Mr Recorder Jason Pow SC in Court

principles.principle_label: Employer's duty to ensure equipment safety
principles.paraphrase_en: An employer breaches statutory duties by failing to ensure that equipment, such as a chain block, is in good condition, of sound material, adequate strength, free from patent defects, and safe for use.

principles.principle_label: Employer's duty to provide safe system of work
principles.paraphrase_en: An employer's duty to provide a safe system of work includes taking adequate measures to prevent workers from falling from heights, and this duty is not discharged merely by having a prohibition policy if it is not actively enforced.

principles.principle_label: Employer's duty to provide training and supervision
principles.paraphrase_en: An employer breaches its duty by failing to provide reasonable training and supervision to a worker regarding safety while performing specific tasks at a construction site.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for statutory breach
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal burden of proving that they had not breached relevant statutory duties rests on the defendants alleging compliance.

principles.principle_label: Causation and safe system of work
principles.paraphrase_en: A departure from an approved work method does not, by itself, cause an accident if a safe system of work was nonetheless provided; it merely creates the site environment against which the adequacy of the safety system is assessed.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case in the context of arguments regarding contributory negligence and novus actus interveniens.

relationships.target_case_name: So Chung Kwong v Ho Kuen & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case in the context of arguments regarding contributory negligence.

relationships.target_case_name: Ng Tim Yip Kevin v Chan Ho Yin
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case in the context of arguments regarding contributory negligence.

relationships.target_case_name: Poon Hau Kei v Hsin Chong Construction Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case in the context of arguments regarding contributory negligence.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Wing Chow v Lee Wing Hang Patrick
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case in the context of arguments regarding contributory negligence.

candidates.case_name: WONG MEI YUK v. THE INCORPORATED OWNERS OF LOWER WONG TAI SIN (1) ESTATE
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge David Chan in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff must establish a causal connection between the defendant's alleged breach of duty and the damage suffered. Separate incidents may be found to have distinct causes, breaking the chain of causation.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for factual findings
principles.paraphrase_en: The court must make factual findings based on the evidence presented. It cannot speculate or make findings for which there is no supporting evidence.
The court must critically evaluate expert evidence, including considering uncertainties about the evidence's authorship and the logical consistency of the expert's conclusions.

principles.principle_label: Duty to mitigate after damage occurs
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's post-incident actions are irrelevant to establishing liability if the damage has already occurred and the cause was not a breach of duty. Further discussion on such actions is academic where the damage is complete.
The fundamental principles of pleadings must be adhered to, as they define the issues for trial and provide a framework for the court's analysis.

relationships.target_case_name: Lam Suk Yee & Anor v The Incorporated Owners of Kam Kin Mansion
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that a failure to investigate and repair a known problem can form the basis of a nuisance claim.

candidates.case_name: CHEUNG WAN OI KANDY v. LAM CHUNG MAN MANDY AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Andrew Li in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For a claim in deceit (fraudulent misrepresentation) to succeed, the claimant must prove the defendant did not honestly believe the representation was true. Fraud exists if the defendant knew it was false, suspected it might be false, or was reckless as to its truth. A positive, honest belief in the statement's truth, even if unreasonable, negates fraud, as does a situation where the defendant knew the truth but had genuinely forgotten it when making the statement.

The court assesses the credibility and reliability of witnesses based on the consistency of their evidence with contemporaneous documents, inherent probability, and their demeanor. A witness whose evidence is inconsistent, contradictory, exaggerated, and contradicted by documents may be found unreliable. Conversely, a witness whose core evidence is clear, cogent, logical, and consistent with documents and common sense may be found credible.

An employer (principal) is not vicariously liable for an employee's (agent's) fraudulent misrepresentation if the employee was acting outside the scope of their authority. This includes situations where the employee makes representations concerning matters for which they are not licensed and not authorized to act.

candidates.case_name: OSMAN MOHAMMED ARAB AND ANOTHER v. NG SHUI CHING, IRENE AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Lam VP, Cheung and Chu JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Appellate review of concurrent factual findings
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will not review concurrent findings of fact made by both the trial judge and the Court of Appeal unless there has been a miscarriage of justice or a violation of legal or procedural principles that justifies such intervention.
Costs on an indemnity basis may be awarded when an application is so obviously without merit, particularly where it challenges factual findings rather than raising genuine points of law or principle.

relationships.target_case_name: Chinachem Charitable Foundation v Chan Chun Chuen
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that concurrent factual findings should not be reviewed absent a miscarriage of justice.

relationships.target_case_name: Suen Shu Tai v Tam Fung Tai (No 2)
relationships.description: The judgment cites this authority to support the established principle regarding appellate restraint in reviewing concurrent factual findings.

relationships.target_case_name: Sun Crown Trading Limited v Holyrood Limited
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from this case to support awarding indemnity costs for a hopeless application challenging factual findings.

candidates.case_name: THE INCORPORATED OWNERS OF SUN HING BUILDING v. JOIN PROFIT (HONG KONG) LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Cheung and G Lam JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Implied term for termination of contractual licence
principles.paraphrase_en: Whether a contractual licence is revocable is a question of contract construction. If the contract contains no express termination provision, the court will imply a term appropriate to the circumstances, considering the nature of the contract as a starting point.

An implied term making an agreement terminable unilaterally by reasonable notice must be necessary. It is not sufficient to imply such a term merely because one party finds it commercially or legally desirable; the agreement must be absurd or lack commercial coherence without it.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proving reasonableness of notice
principles.paraphrase_en: Where an agreement is terminable by reasonable notice, the party seeking to terminate bears the burden of proving that the notice given was reasonable.

principles.principle_label: No general rule for invalid notice
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no general rule that an invalid notice to terminate a licence (e.g., one giving too short a period) is always wholly ineffective. The subsequent passage of time until legal proceedings or trial may, depending on the circumstances, provide sufficient notice.

The only general proposition regarding termination of a licence is that it can be terminated by the grantor giving the licensee a reasonable notice to quit, after which the licensee becomes a trespasser.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Bellotti that a licence can be terminated by the grantor giving reasonable notice, after which the licensee becomes a trespasser.

relationships.target_case_name: Canadian Pacific Railway Co v The King
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes this case, noting it does not establish a general rule that a valid notice with a specified termination date is always necessary.

candidates.case_name: LEUNG YEE SHAN AND OTHERS v. LEUNG KIN WING
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge James Kwan in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Elements of common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust requires three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and claimant regarding beneficial ownership, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner departs from that intention.

principles.principle_label: Two situations for common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust may arise in two situations: first, where there is an agreement, arrangement or understanding between parties about beneficial ownership before acquisition (or exceptionally later), based on evidence of express discussions; second, where there is no such agreement but the court infers a common intention from conduct.

principles.principle_label: Resulting trust presumption from financial contribution
principles.paraphrase_en: A resulting trust arises where a person pays wholly or partly for property vested in another's name (or joint names), creating a presumption they did not intend a gift; the property is held in trust for them in shares proportionate to their contributions, but this presumption is easily rebuttable.

principles.principle_label: Applicability of trusts to HOS flats
principles.paraphrase_en: Home Ownership Scheme flats can, in principle, be subject to constructive or resulting trusts in favour of contributors to the purchase price, which serves the public interest by allowing low-income purchasers to receive assistance with deposits and mortgage payments.

relationships.target_case_name: Primecredit Ltd v Yeung Chun Pang Barry
relationships.description: The judgment applies Cheung JA's analysis of the two situations where a common intention constructive trust may arise.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheuk Shu Yin v Yip Po Wan and another
relationships.description: The judgment cites Lord Hoffmann's observation that HOS flats can be subject to constructive or resulting trusts.
The judgment applies the established principles regarding resulting trusts, particularly situation A where payment for property creates a presumption of trust.

candidates.case_name: SIT LAI YUK v. TSE HAU TANG
judges: Coram: His Honour Judge H. Au-Yeung in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For a common intention constructive trust to arise, there must be a shared intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding beneficial interests, followed by detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, making it unconscionable for the owner to depart from it.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for trust claims
principles.paraphrase_en: Where property is registered in the defendant's name, the plaintiff bears the legal burden of proving, on the balance of probabilities, that the defendant holds the property on trust for the plaintiff.

relationships.target_case_name: Luo Xing Juan v. Estate of Hui Shui See
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the authoritative statement of law regarding common intention constructive trusts from Luo Xing Juan.

candidates.case_name: WONG LEI AH v. LEE JIM WING
judges: Before: Hon Lam VP, Cheung and Chu JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Intention to possess from payment of rent
principles.paraphrase_en: A squatter's willingness to pay rent to the owner indicates a lack of the necessary intention to possess the land for the purpose of establishing adverse possession, as it demonstrates the squatter views their possession as deriving from the owner's lawful title.

principles.principle_label: Relevance of payments to intention to possess
principles.paraphrase_en: Payments made by a squatter, such as for building renovation, improvement funds, or repairs, are relevant factors to be considered in the overall assessment of whether the requisite intention to possess has been established.

principles.principle_label: Timing of payments in adverse possession
principles.paraphrase_en: The timing of payments or actions by a squatter, such as making a first significant payment many years after occupation began, is a relevant circumstance when evaluating the intention to possess.

principles.principle_label: Holistic assessment of intention to possess
principles.paraphrase_en: No single factor, such as a willingness to pay rent, a failure to pay rates, or a failure to consider the property as one's own, is determinative or inherently inconsistent with an intention to possess; the assessment must be made based on all the circumstances.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Tak Yue v Kung Kwok Wai David
relationships.description: The present judgment adopts the principle from this case that a squatter's willingness to pay rent indicates a lack of intention to possess for adverse possession.

candidates.case_name: WONG CHEE SING (OR WONG CHE SING) (by his next friend WONG TAK KWAN LILIAN) v. HUNG WAH (孔华) TRADING AS TAK HING LUNG MEDICINE CO. AND ANOTHER
judges: Before : Hon Kwan VP, Cheung and G Lam JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish a claim for adverse possession, a claimant must demonstrate both factual possession of the land and an intention to possess it.
Factual possession requires the alleged possessor to have dealt with the land as an occupying owner would be expected to, and to have excluded others from doing so.

principles.principle_label: Intention to possess vs. intention to own
principles.paraphrase_en: The requisite mental element for adverse possession is an intention to possess the land, not an intention to own it. A mistaken belief about one's legal title (e.g., believing oneself to be a purchaser or tenant) does not preclude a claim if the factual elements are met.

When assessing whether acts are sufficient to constitute factual possession, the court must consider what the squatter actually did, not what they could have done. The mere failure to carry out improvements or pay government charges is not necessarily determinative.

relationships.target_case_name: J A Pye (Oxford) Ltd v Graham
relationships.description: The judgment cites this authority for its agreement with the statements of law on adverse possession made by Slade J.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheung Kwong Yuen v Sun Hui Fang
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that a mistaken claim of lawful tenancy or purchase does not bar an adverse possession claim.

relationships.target_case_name: Poon Chi Hang v Lai Ho Sun
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reasoning from this case regarding the proper assessment of a squatter's acts for establishing factual possession.

candidates.case_name: CHAN CHUN NI v. CHAN NGAI KEUNG
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal title to property determines the beneficial interest unless proven otherwise. The party claiming a different beneficial arrangement bears the burden of proof.

principles.principle_label: Elements of common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: A constructive trust based on common intention requires: (1) a shared intention between the property owner and claimant regarding beneficial interests, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner departs from that intention.

principles.principle_label: Presumption of resulting trust from payment
principles.paraphrase_en: When a person voluntarily pays for property vested in another's name (or joint names), the law presumes they did not intend a gift. The property is held on resulting trust for the payer, proportionate to their contribution. This presumption can be rebutted by evidence of a contrary intention.

principles.principle_label: Mortgage contribution timing for resulting trust
principles.paraphrase_en: For a resulting trust analysis, money borrowed on mortgage at the time of purchase is considered a contribution by the person obligated to repay the loan at that time. Subsequent mortgage payments by someone not originally obligated do not count as a purchase contribution.

relationships.target_case_name: Luo Xing Juan v Estate of Hui Shui See
relationships.description: The judgment cites this authority for the legal principles governing common intention constructive trusts.

candidates.case_name: LEUNG YIU MAN v. CHU KUEN
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Kenneth Wong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust requires three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding beneficial ownership, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner departs from that intention.

A common intention agreement or arrangement for a constructive trust must be based on evidence of express discussions between the parties, even if the terms are imperfectly remembered or imprecise. Such an agreement is typically found prior to acquisition, or exceptionally at a later date.

When determining claims for a common intention constructive trust or a resulting trust, the court must adopt a holistic approach rather than a piecemeal analysis of numerous factual issues, to avoid losing sight of the broader context.

The parties' initial intentions regarding beneficial ownership of a property may change over time, for example if one party finances a substantial improvement or extension that significantly alters the property.

candidates.case_name: THE CHINESE CULTURAL STUDIES ASSOCIATION formerly known as THE CHINESE CULTURAL STUDIES ASSOCIATION LTD v. CHEUNG LAI NGAN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge C. K. Siu in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff may only apply for summary judgment under Order 14 rule 1 of the Rules of the District Court if the defendant has first given notice of intention to defend the action.

candidates.case_name: PANG KIT PING, the administratrix of the estate of CHAN YUK HO, deceased AND ANOTHER v. CHAN CHIN BONG DOMINIC
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Grace Chow in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The rule against granting declaratory relief in default judgments is a rule of practice, not law, and yields to the court's duty to do full justice to the plaintiff. Where a declaration is the primary relief sought, it may be necessary to grant it, but its terms must not exceed what the plaintiff is entitled to and what justice requires.

principles.principle_label: Elements of common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: A constructive trust based on common intention requires three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding their shared beneficial interests, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner departs from that intention.

principles.principle_label: Situations for common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust can arise in two situations: first, where there is an agreement, arrangement, or understanding between the parties about beneficial ownership reached before acquisition (or exceptionally later), based on evidence of express discussions; second, where the court infers or imputes a common intention from the parties' conduct.

relationships.target_case_name: Primecredit Ltd v Yeung Chun Pang Barry
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principles from Primecredit Ltd regarding the two situations in which a common intention constructive trust may arise.

candidates.case_name: CHAU KWAN LAM v. CHAU KA YEE CARIE
judges: Before: Hon Lisa Wong J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The legal owner of a property is presumed to also be the beneficial owner. A party claiming a beneficial interest contrary to the legal title bears the burden of proving that interest on the balance of probabilities.

principles.principle_label: Common intention constructive trust - fundamental principle
principles.paraphrase_en: The doctrine of common intention constructive trust is fundamentally governed by the equitable principle of preventing unconscionable conduct. The court must assess all elements of the doctrine holistically.
For a common intention constructive trust, the relevant intention is the actual intention commonly held by the parties at the material time.
In ascertaining the parties' common intention, the court considers discussions, statements, or actions from which an agreement or common understanding can be inferred.
An imperfectly remembered or imprecise agreement between parties is not necessarily a barrier to a court finding that an express agreement, arrangement, or understanding existed.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Lord Neuberger's dissenting view on the need for compelling evidence to infer a change in beneficial ownership shares after acquisition.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that the court must look at all elements of the doctrine in the round, with equity's concern to prevent unconscionable conduct permeating them.

relationships.target_case_name: Luo Xing Juan v Estate of Hui Shui See
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that what matters is the intention commonly held by the parties.

relationships.target_case_name: Paragon Finance Plc v DB Thakerar & Co
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the principle that the commonly held intention of the parties is what matters.
The judgment applies principles from this case for ascertaining the common intention of parties in a property dispute.

candidates.case_name: SO CHUI WAH v. QU XUANBAI
judges: Coram: Deputy High Court Judge H. Au-Yeung in Court

principles.principle_label: Constructive trust based on common intention
principles.paraphrase_en: A constructive trust arises from the common intention of the property owner and the claimant regarding their beneficial interests in the property, coupled with the claimant's detrimental reliance on that intention, making it unconscionable for the owner to deny that interest.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for trust claims
principles.paraphrase_en: Where property is registered in the defendant's name, the plaintiff bears the burden of proving, on the balance of probabilities, that the defendant holds the property on trust for the plaintiff.

relationships.target_case_name: Luo Xing Juan v. Estate of Hui Shui See
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principles for establishing a constructive trust based on common intention as stated in that authority.

candidates.case_name: MA HOW JEE v. THE PERSONAL REPRESENTATIVES OF MAH SHUN CHEW, DECEASED

principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant establishes factual possession for adverse possession by demonstrating exclusive physical control and use of the property, evidenced by acts such as paying rates and utility bills in their name over a substantial period.

The requisite intention to possess (animus possidendi) for adverse possession is established by acts that demonstrate the claimant is treating the property as their own, such as changing locks and undertaking maintenance responsibilities.

candidates.case_name: LAU WAI KEI v. YIU SZE WAN

principles.paraphrase_en: A constructive trust based on common intention arises from the shared understanding between the property owner and the claimant regarding their beneficial interests in the property.

principles.principle_label: Presumption of resulting trust on financial contribution
principles.paraphrase_en: When a person makes a voluntary payment or contributes to property purchase that is vested in another's name or jointly, there is a presumption they did not intend a gift, creating a resulting trust in proportion to their contribution, unless rebutted by evidence of contrary intention or advancement.

principles.principle_label: Establishment of common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust arises when there is an agreement, arrangement, or understanding between the parties regarding the beneficial ownership of the property.

candidates.case_name: ZHAI JIN CHENG v. WANG LIJUN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Suen SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust arises from three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding shared beneficial interests, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner departs from that intention.
Where property is transferred without payment of consideration, a resulting trust in favour of the transferor may be presumed.

A third party may apply to intervene in an action to discharge a charging order on the ground that the judgment debtor held no beneficial interest in the charged property capable of being subject to the order.
A plaintiff may apply for judgment against a defendant who defaults in serving a defence, pursuant to Order 19 rule 7 of the Rules of the High Court.

candidates.case_name: POON MEI YUNG v. CHOI OI YAU
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Aidan Tam in Court

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for beneficial interest
principles.paraphrase_en: When property is registered in a defendant's sole name, the legal presumption is that the beneficial interest follows the legal title. The plaintiff bears the burden of proving, on the balance of probabilities, that the defendant holds the property on trust for them, thereby separating beneficial from legal ownership.

principles.principle_label: Requirements for common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish a common intention constructive trust, a plaintiff must demonstrate: (1) a common intention between the plaintiff and property owner at the time of purchase (or later) that beneficial ownership differs from legal ownership; (2) the plaintiff altered their position in detrimental reliance on that common intention; and (3) it would be unconscionable for the legal owner to assert full ownership.

The doctrine of common intention constructive trust has two limbs: first, based on an express agreement or understanding between parties regarding beneficial ownership; second, where no express agreement exists, the court may infer a common intention from the parties' conduct.

principles.principle_label: Primacy of common intention over resulting trust
principles.paraphrase_en: In domestic property disputes, if the matter can be resolved by reference to common intention constructive trust, it is unnecessary to resort to resulting trust principles.

When a person makes a voluntary payment towards property vested in another's name, the law presumes they did not intend a gift, giving rise to a resulting trust in proportion to their contribution. This presumption can be rebutted by evidence of an intention to benefit the legal owner or by the counter-presumption of advancement.

relationships.target_case_name: Liu Wai Keung v Liu Wai Man
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that the plaintiff bears the burden of proving beneficial ownership differs from legal ownership where property is in the defendant's name.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheung Lai Mui v Cheung Wai Shing
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for outlining the requirements for establishing a proprietary estoppel claim.

principles.paraphrase_en: An express trust is created when a property owner agrees to hold property for the benefit of another person and to have no personal interest in it.
When property is transferred to a person who agrees to hold it on trust for the transferor, that person may hold it on resulting or constructive trust for the transferor's absolute benefit.

A common intention constructive trust requires three elements: (1) a common intention between property owner and claimant regarding beneficial interests; (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention; and (3) unconscionability of the owner departing from that intention.
A common intention constructive trust can arise when parties reach an agreement, arrangement, or understanding about beneficial ownership before or (exceptionally) after property acquisition.
Where no agreement on beneficial ownership exists, a court may infer common intention entirely from the parties' conduct regarding the property.

principles.principle_label: Elements of common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: A constructive trust based on common intention requires three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding beneficial interests, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner departs from that intention.

A common intention agreement or arrangement for beneficial ownership must be based on evidence of express discussions between the parties, even if those discussions are imperfectly remembered or imprecise in their terms.

principles.principle_label: Change of intention through property improvement
principles.paraphrase_en: The parties' initial intentions regarding beneficial ownership may change if one party finances or constructs a substantial improvement or extension to the property, significantly altering its nature from the original acquisition.

A claimant asserting sole beneficial ownership must establish they had the financial means to acquire the property and that there was a common intention inconsistent with the registered co-ownership arrangement.

A presumption of resulting trust cannot succeed in the absence of documented evidence of financial contribution to the purchase price, especially when the claimant's oral evidence is implausible and inconsistent.

candidates.case_name: YYT v. CCM AND OTHERS
judges: Coram: Her Honour Judge Elaine Liu in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: Assets generated or acquired during a marriage using funds earned during the marriage are prima facie considered matrimonial property for the purposes of ancillary relief.

principles.principle_label: Beneficial ownership relevance to ancillary relief
principles.paraphrase_en: A determination of beneficial ownership of property held in the name of a third party provides a useful basis for consideration in ancillary relief applications. If a spouse is found to be the beneficial owner, the property should prima facie form part of the matrimonial assets for distribution, and vice versa.

The dispute over whether an asset should be included in or excluded from the matrimonial pot for distribution is better determined at the ancillary relief trial, when all evidence relevant to the statutory factors is adduced and the court can consider all relevant factors holistically.

principles.principle_label: Scope of trial limited by order
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's determination in a trial is confined to the specific questions covered by the relevant court order. Issues not encompassed by that order, even if related, should not be determined in that trial.

relationships.description: The judgment notes the practice adopted in this case of determining the matrimonial pot issue in the same trial as the beneficial ownership issue.

candidates.case_name: LAI SUK KING v. THE PERSONAL REPRESENTATIVE OF THE ESTATE OF LEUNG YUNG, DECEASED
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Leung in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A witness who demonstrates solid firsthand knowledge of facts and gives straightforward evidence, supported by documentary evidence and uncontradicted at trial, can be accepted as credible on the balance of probabilities.

A de facto parent-child relationship can be established through long-term cohabitation and being raised as one's own child, which can persist even after discovering different biological parentage, provided the biological parent plays no role in the individual's life.

principles.principle_label: Intention to create legal relations in domestic context
principles.paraphrase_en: While a domestic context does not automatically preclude the formation of an intention to create legally enforceable relations, such intention must be affirmatively established; representations made within family relationships may lack the requisite intention to form a binding agreement.

A claimant may establish 100% beneficial ownership of property through a common intention constructive trust by proving the common intention of the parties regarding ownership, even in the absence of a formal agreement.
Proprietary estoppel can serve as an alternative basis for establishing beneficial ownership when a party has relied on representations to their detriment, even if other legal claims fail.

candidates.case_name: AD HOC COMMITTEE (comprising CMB Wing Lung Bank Ltd and The Export- Import Bank of China) v. CHINA LIFE TRUSTEES LIMITED
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Gummow NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: A Quistclose trust arises when money is transferred with the objective intention that it be used solely for a specific purpose, not when there is an intention to retain a beneficial interest. The court must look for this objective intention, not a subjective intention to retain beneficial interest.

A Quistclose trust arises where property is transferred with the parties' objective intention that it be applied exclusively for a specific purpose. The recipient holds the property on trust, subject to that purpose. If the purpose fails, the property is held on trust for the transferor.

An express undertaking by the recipient to use funds solely for a specific purpose is not required for a Quistclose trust. The trust arises if the circumstances, assessed objectively, justify inferring that the payment was intended to be subject to such conditions.

principles.principle_label: Nature of Quistclose trust (resulting trust analysis)
principles.paraphrase_en: A Quistclose trust is properly analysed as a resulting trust for the transferor from the outset, with the transferee having a mandate (or power) to apply the money for the stated purpose. The transferor does not part with the entire beneficial interest; the transferee has no beneficial interest, only a limited authority to use the money for the purpose.

The essential question in every case is whether the parties intended the money to be at the free disposition of the recipient. A trust arises if the money was to be used exclusively for a particular purpose, making the recipient a fiduciary in respect of it.

relationships.target_case_name: Barclays Bank Ltd v Quistclose Investments Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies and elaborates on the principles established in Quistclose regarding trusts arising from payments for a specific purpose.
The judgment follows and treats as authoritative Lord Millett's analysis in Twinsectra that a Quistclose trust is a resulting trust with a mandate.
The judgment cites Toovey v Milne as an early authority, approved in Quistclose, showing a trust can arise without an express stipulation for repayment.

relationships.target_case_name: Prickly Bay Waterside Ltd v British American Insurance Company Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites the Privy Council decision in Prickly Bay, which applied and endorsed the Twinsectra analysis as the core understanding of Quistclose trusts.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for beneficial interest departure
principles.paraphrase_en: When a property is held in joint names without a declaration of trust, there is a prima facie presumption of joint and equal beneficial ownership. The party seeking to show that the beneficial title departs from the legal title bears the burden of proof, typically by establishing a common intention constructive trust or a resulting trust.

principles.principle_label: Resulting trust vs common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: Where there is clear evidence of a common intention between parties regarding property ownership, and one party has acted to their detriment in reliance on that intention, the doctrine of resulting trust has no room to operate. The analysis should instead focus on the common intention constructive trust.

principles.principle_label: Whole course of conduct in determining shared intention
principles.paraphrase_en: In determining the shared intention of parties regarding beneficial ownership of a property, the court must consider the parties' whole course of conduct in relation to the property. This includes looking at the history of their affairs concerning other jointly held assets and their distribution, not just the acquisition of the property in question.

When searching for a couple's shared intention regarding beneficial interests in a property, the court should consider factors beyond direct financial contributions. These include the parties' earnings and borrowing power at acquisition, prevailing social policies (like property tax and stamp duty), and the overall financial arrangements for all properties the couple holds.

principles.principle_label: Equivocal statements insufficient for sole ownership
principles.paraphrase_en: A statement by one party that they will make all mortgage repayments is too equivocal to establish sole beneficial ownership, especially when considered against the couple's usual financial arrangements and the property's use as a family home.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Stack v. Dowden that joint legal ownership of a domestic property creates a prima facie presumption of joint and equal beneficial ownership.

principles.principle_label: Service requirement for default judgment summons
principles.paraphrase_en: Independent service of a summons seeking default judgment is not required; the summons can be served together with the originating process.

principles.principle_label: Common intention constructive trust - agreement basis
principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust can arise where, prior to acquisition (or exceptionally later), the parties reach an agreement, arrangement, or understanding regarding the beneficial ownership of the property.

principles.principle_label: Common intention constructive trust - conduct basis
principles.paraphrase_en: Where there is no evidence of an agreement on beneficial ownership, a common intention constructive trust may be inferred solely from the conduct of the parties.

principles.paraphrase_en: When determining whether a disposition was made to defeat a claim for financial provision, the court must ascertain the subjective intention of the party making the disposition at the time it was made, rather than focusing on the consequences of that act.

Historical land records, such as conveyances registered as gifts, can be used as evidence to establish the chain of ownership and the nature of property as ancestral land held within a family over generations.

principles.principle_label: Intention to defeat claim not established
principles.paraphrase_en: A disposition of property will not be found to have been made with the intention of defeating a claim for financial provision if the court is satisfied, on the facts, that such an intention did not exist at the time of the disposition.

principles.principle_label: Presumption of beneficial interest following legal title
principles.paraphrase_en: The starting point in equity is that the beneficial ownership of property is presumed to follow the legal ownership. A person claiming a beneficial interest different from the legal title bears the burden of proving this on the balance of probabilities.

principles.principle_label: Requirements for common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish a common intention constructive trust, a claimant must show: (1) a common intention between the claimant and the legal owner at the time of purchase (or later) that beneficial ownership would differ from legal ownership; (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention; and (3) that it would be unconscionable for the legal owner to assert full ownership.

principles.principle_label: Two limbs of common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: The doctrine of common intention constructive trust has two limbs: first, based on an express agreement or understanding between the parties; second, based on an inference drawn from the parties' conduct, which may include direct financial contributions to the purchase price.

principles.principle_label: Presumption of advancement in family context
principles.paraphrase_en: In the context of a transfer of property between family members (such as father and son), the presumption of advancement may apply, meaning the transfer is presumed to be a gift unless there is evidence to rebut that presumption.

principles.principle_label: Court's refusal to rule on non-issues
principles.paraphrase_en: A court will not make rulings on disputes that are not properly before it, particularly where the relevant parties are not joined to the action and the relief sought does not relate to those issues.

principles.paraphrase_en: The court may grant declaratory relief in a default judgment if it is the primary relief sought and necessary to achieve full justice for the plaintiff, but the declaration must be narrowly tailored to what is strictly necessary and justified.

principles.principle_label: Elements of common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust requires three elements: (1) a shared intention between the property owner and claimant regarding beneficial ownership, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner were to deny that intention.

A common intention constructive trust can arise from an express agreement or understanding between parties about beneficial ownership, which must be evidenced by discussions between them, even if recalled imperfectly or expressed imprecisely.

principles.paraphrase_en: A constructive trust based on common intention requires three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding beneficial ownership, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner were to depart from that intention.

An agreement or arrangement forming the basis of a common intention constructive trust must be evidenced by express discussions between the parties, even if those discussions are imperfectly remembered or imprecise in their terms.

principles.principle_label: Relevance of third-party knowledge to trust
principles.paraphrase_en: The existence of a common intention constructive trust is determined by the intention between the property owner and the claimant; it is not negated merely because a third party (such as a subsequent spouse) was unaware of the arrangement.

principles.principle_label: Resulting trust presumption from financial contribution
principles.paraphrase_en: A resulting trust arises when a person makes a voluntary payment toward property purchased in another's name, creating a presumption they did not intend a gift; this presumption is rebuttable and typically aligns beneficial ownership with financial contributions.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for trust claims
principles.paraphrase_en: The claimant bears the burden of proving both the existence of a common intention/agreement and the alleged financial contributions to support claims of common intention constructive trust or purchase money resulting trust.

principles.principle_label: Elements of common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust requires three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding shared beneficial interests in the property, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner were to depart from that common intention.

A common intention constructive trust can arise from an agreement, arrangement, or understanding between the parties about beneficial ownership, reached before acquisition or exceptionally later. Finding such an agreement must be based on evidence of express discussions, even if imperfectly remembered or imprecise in terms.
The formation date of a common intention agreement need not be precisely identified; its existence can be deduced or inferred from the parties' conduct.

principles.paraphrase_en: When a person dies intestate leaving children but no surviving spouse, the residuary estate is held on statutory trusts for the children in equal shares.
Property given to a child by way of advancement or on marriage by the intestate must, subject to a contrary intention, be brought into account against that child's share of the intestate estate.

principles.principle_label: Test for contrary intention to hotchpot
principles.paraphrase_en: The test for whether a gift should not be brought into hotchpot is subjective, requiring the court to infer from all circumstances whether the intestate intended to prefer the recipient over other children, not merely to anticipate their future inheritance.

principles.principle_label: Interpretation of layperson's understanding of property
principles.paraphrase_en: When assessing a deceased layperson's intention regarding property, the court should not apply an unduly technical legal understanding of property holding concepts, but should consider the person's own understanding.

principles.paraphrase_en: For a common intention constructive trust to be established, there must be an objectively probable common intention between the parties regarding beneficial ownership, not merely a unilateral belief by one party.

principles.principle_label: Adverse possession and ex turpi causa
principles.paraphrase_en: In an adverse possession claim, the applicability of the ex turpi causa defense depends on a 'range of factors approach' considering all relevant circumstances, rather than a strict 'reliance approach'.

principles.principle_label: Adoption of Patel v Mirza approach
principles.paraphrase_en: Hong Kong has adopted the 'range of factors approach' (also known as the proportionality approach) from Patel v Mirza for assessing illegality defenses, replacing the previous 'reliance approach' from Tinsley v Miligan.
When applying the law regarding illegality defenses, courts must maintain a sense of proportionality to avoid excessive consequences.

relationships.description: The judgment adopts the 'range of factors approach' from Patel v Mirza for assessing the ex turpi causa defense in adverse possession claims.
The judgment states that the 'reliance approach' from Tinsley v Miligan has been replaced in Hong Kong by the Patel v Mirza 'range of factors approach'.

candidates.case_name: WONG YIN TAK v. YIP WAI MAN

principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust requires three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding beneficial ownership, (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention, and (3) unconscionability if the owner departs from that intention.
The common intention agreement or understanding for a constructive trust must generally be reached before the property's acquisition, though exceptionally it may be formed later.
A finding of a common intention agreement must be based on evidence of express discussions between the parties, even if those discussions are imperfectly remembered or imprecise in their terms.
A resulting trust may be pleaded as an alternative claim to a constructive trust, but a court may find it unnecessary to decide if the constructive trust claim is established.
Unjust enrichment may be advanced as a fall-back argument, but a court may find it inapplicable where a proprietary trust claim succeeds.

candidates.case_name: FU LAM LAM v. FU TING TING

principles.principle_label: Common intention constructive trust - two limbs
principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust can arise in two ways: first, through an express agreement, arrangement, or understanding between parties regarding beneficial ownership; second, through conduct from which the court can infer a common intention where no express agreement exists.

principles.principle_label: Post-acquisition common intention - no exceptionality standard
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no separate legal standard or requirement to prove 'exceptionality' for establishing a common intention constructive trust based on a post-acquisition agreement or understanding. Such cases are possible but may be less common than those involving pre-acquisition intentions.

An appellate court will only interfere with a trial judge's findings of fact if the conclusion was unsupported by evidence, based on a misunderstanding of evidence, or one that no reasonable judge could have reached. Merely arguing that findings were against the weight of evidence is insufficient.

principles.principle_label: Leave to appeal - reasonable prospect test
principles.paraphrase_en: Leave to appeal should not be granted unless the intended appeal has a reasonable prospect of success or there is some other compelling reason in the interests of justice for it to be heard.

candidates.case_name: LEUNG KAM v. WONG YUK LING
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Alfred Cheng in Court

principles.principle_label: Common intention constructive trust - general principles
principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust arises when: (1) there is a common intention between the parties, either before acquisition or exceptionally later, that beneficial ownership differs from legal ownership; (2) the claimant acts to their detriment in reliance on that intention; and (3) it would be unconscionable for the legal owner to deny the claimant's interest. The burden of proof lies with the claimant to show this on the balance of probabilities.

A resulting trust can arise from the absence of an intention to benefit the recipient, or from the presumed intention of the parties. A legal presumption arises that a person providing purchase money did not intend a gift, so the property is held on trust for the provider, proportionate to contributions if multiple parties contributed.

principles.principle_label: Equity follows the law - presumption and burden
principles.paraphrase_en: The starting presumption is that beneficial interest follows legal interest. Where property is registered in a defendant's name, the plaintiff bears the burden of proving, on balance of probabilities, that the defendant holds it on trust such that beneficial ownership differs from legal ownership.

principles.principle_label: HOS restrictions vs. creation of equitable interests
principles.paraphrase_en: The statutory restriction on alienation of Home Ownership Scheme (HOS) flats (s.17B) aims to prevent quick profit-making resale, not to prohibit the creation of equitable interests through family resource pooling for purchase. Creating a constructive trust over an HOS flat does not violate the alienation restriction, as equitable owners cannot force a conveyance of the legal estate during the restriction period.

principles.principle_label: Failure to prove common intention - legal title prevails
principles.paraphrase_en: If a claimant fails to prove the existence of a common intention necessary for a constructive trust, the defendant's case based on legal title should prevail because equity follows the law.

relationships.target_case_name: Kwan Kwai Lin v Yeung On Ki

The judgment cites this case as authority for the principle that the plaintiff bears the burden of proof when beneficial ownership is claimed to differ from legal ownership.
The judgment cites this case alongside others as authority for the burden of proof regarding beneficial interest differing from legal title.

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's power to amend an order under the slip rule is limited to correcting errors in expressing the court's manifest intention as it appeared at the time the order was made.
The court possesses an inherent power to clarify its original order after it has been sealed, provided the court's intention from the judgment is clear and no prejudice is caused to the other party.

principles.principle_label: Discretionary nature of slip rule relief
principles.paraphrase_en: Granting relief under the slip rule is a discretionary remedy, which the court may refuse if intervening circumstances would make rectification inexpedient or inequitable.

principles.paraphrase_en: The intention required for establishing a legal claim, such as adverse possession or the creation of a trust, must be determined objectively, and self-serving declarations by the party are not reliable evidence.

principles.principle_label: Charitable trust for religion without formal declaration
principles.paraphrase_en: A charitable trust for the advancement of religion can be formed over land intended for a place of public worship, even in the absence of a formal declaration of trust.

principles.principle_label: Factual reliance on charity for adverse possession defence
principles.paraphrase_en: In resisting a claim of adverse possession, a party may rely on the factual existence and operation of a charitable purpose over the property for decades, regardless of whether a legally enforceable charitable trust is ultimately established.

A person's intention regarding property can be objectively inferred from the surrounding circumstances and documented actions, and a court will not readily accept a suggested change of mind that contradicts the clear, documented record.

A person's use of property in a manner consistent with a charitable or permissive purpose, and not to the exclusion of all others for their own benefit, is inconsistent with a claim of possessing the property as their own.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Powell v McFarlane for the principle that a squatter's self-serving declarations about their intention are not reliable evidence.
The judgment cites this case in relation to the application of statutory provisions concerning the validity of charitable trusts over land.

relationships.target_case_name: Ma Wah Yan & Another v Dharma Realm Budd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this authority for the proposition that a charitable trust for religion can exist without a formal declaration if land is held for a place of public worship.

principles.paraphrase_en: Where property is acquired and held by individuals in their names, the court may find it is held on a common intention constructive trust for a group or organization if there is a shared understanding that the registered owners are merely nominees holding title for the collective benefit of that group.

principles.principle_label: Inference of nominee arrangement from joint tenancy
principles.paraphrase_en: If individuals who are not close relatives contribute to a purchase price but hold property as joint tenants, this can indicate they did not intend to retain beneficial ownership for themselves, as a joint tenancy would allow the last survivor to take the entire interest to the exclusion of the others' estates, which is an unlikely intention for mere business associates.

principles.principle_label: Evidence of common intention from conduct and roles
principles.paraphrase_en: The common intention for a constructive trust can be inferred from the circumstances, including the fact that the registered owners held senior positions in the organization alleged to be the beneficial owner, indicating they were chosen as representatives rather than random individuals.

Members of an unincorporated association can be found to be privy to a common intention regarding property held for their benefit, and can be content for the association's management committee to manage the property on that basis.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that a common intention can be based on express discussions about how property is to be held.
The judgment cites this case alongside Leung Hang Lin for the same point regarding finding common intention from express discussions.
The judgment cites this case for principles of proprietary estoppel, including that its elements are not watertight compartments and that equity aims to prevent unconscionable conduct.
The judgment refers to this case for a pertinent point, though the specific legal principle cited is not detailed in the provided paragraphs.

principles.paraphrase_en: A joint tenancy can be severed, converting it into a tenancy in common, by one party serving a notice of severance on the other, provided the notice demonstrates an unequivocal intention to sever and the other party raises no objection.

principles.principle_label: Elements of common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: A constructive trust based on common intention requires three elements: (1) a common intention between the property owner and the claimant regarding their shared beneficial interests; (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention; and (3) unconscionability if the property owner were to depart from that intention.

A finding of a common intention regarding beneficial ownership must be based on evidence of express discussions between the parties, even if those discussions are imperfectly remembered or imprecise in their terms.

The presumption that equity follows the law (i.e., the presumption of resulting trust where legal title dictates beneficial interest) can be displaced in exceptional cases by evidence of a change in the parties' common intention at a later date.

An informal document, such as a power of attorney, can serve as clear and compelling evidence of an express agreement between parties regarding beneficial ownership of property, even if the document itself is not legally effective for its primary purpose.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: The party claiming a beneficial interest different from the legal title must prove on balance of probabilities: (1) a common intention at purchase or later regarding beneficial ownership; (2) detrimental reliance on that common intention; and (3) that it would be unconscionable for the legal owner to assert title contrary to that intention.

Common intention can be established by evidence of an express agreement, arrangement, or understanding, or inferred from the parties' conduct, such as direct financial contributions or non-monetary contributions to the family home. Cultural context, such as traditional Chinese family norms where explicit property discussions are uncommon, may be relevant.
A claim for a common intention constructive trust can be undermined by evidence of the claimant's own prior inconsistent statements or positions regarding the beneficial ownership of the property.

For a common intention constructive trust, the claimant must show they significantly altered their position in detrimental reliance on the common intention. Merely continuing an existing course of conduct or having a fleeting intention to change is insufficient.

Where property is held by co-owners, the court may order partition, order a sale, or refuse to make any order under the Partition Ordinance. The court must assess the practicality of partition and determine objectively whether a sale is beneficial to all co-owners, not merely based on a co-owner's dissent.
