principles.paraphrase_en: A common intention constructive trust requires an agreement, arrangement, or understanding between the parties regarding beneficial ownership, typically reached before or at the time of acquisition, though exceptionally it may arise later.

A common intention formed between parties A and B regarding property legally owned by a third party C cannot, in principle, impose a constructive trust on C to hold the property for A and B, as C is not a party to and does not unconscionably depart from that intention.

The court may draw adverse inferences against a claim of beneficial co-ownership where the legal holding arrangements (such as sole registration) are inconsistent with the alleged common intention, especially when there was a clear opportunity to register the property jointly or as tenants-in-common.

principles.principle_label: Burden to identify trust property and beneficiary
principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant asserting a beneficial interest in property held on trust must clearly identify both the specific property claimed and the entity or person (e.g., a specific company or partnership) on whose behalf the property is allegedly held.

If a claim based on a common intention constructive trust fails, an alternative claim may arise that properties purchased and funded with partnership money are held on trust for the partners of that partnership.

candidates.case_name: CHENG CHING FONG v. TING SHUK KAY PEGGY, Executrix of the Will of TING SOK HANG, deceased

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for beneficial interest
principles.paraphrase_en: The starting legal presumption is that the beneficial interest in a property follows the legal title. A claimant who asserts a different beneficial ownership bears the burden of proving, on the balance of probabilities, that the legal owner holds the property on trust for them.

principles.principle_label: Requirements for common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish a common intention constructive trust, a claimant must prove: (1) a common intention between the claimant and the legal owner (at purchase or later) that beneficial ownership differs from legal title; (2) detrimental reliance by the claimant on that common intention; and (3) that it would be unconscionable for the legal owner to rely solely on their legal title.

principles.principle_label: Two limbs of common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: The doctrine of common intention constructive trust has two limbs. The first is based on evidence of an express agreement or understanding between the parties regarding beneficial ownership. The second allows the court to infer such a common intention from the conduct of the parties, often involving direct financial contributions to the purchase.

Where a person makes a voluntary payment for the purchase of property vested in another's name, or contributes to a joint purchase, there is a presumption of a resulting trust. This means the property is held for the person who provided the money, in proportion to their contribution. This presumption can be rebutted by evidence of a gift or the counter-presumption of advancement.
principles.paragraph_hrase_en: Where a person makes a voluntary payment for the purchase of property vested in another's name, or contributes to a joint purchase, there is a presumption of a resulting trust. This means the property is held for the person who provided the money, in proportion to their contribution. This presumption can be rebutted by evidence of a gift or the counter-presumption of advancement.

principles.principle_label: Severance of joint tenancy and common intention
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish a common intention constructive trust that prevents a joint tenant from exercising their legal right to sever the joint tenancy, a very clear and specific common intention to that effect must be proven. A mere agreement to purchase as joint tenants is insufficient to negate the inherent right of severance.

principles.paraphrase_en: The party claiming a beneficial interest in property, despite not being the legal owner, bears the burden of proving that interest.

principles.principle_label: Presumption of beneficial interest following legal title
principles.paraphrase_en: The starting presumption in equity is that the beneficial ownership of property aligns with the legal ownership. When property is registered in a defendant's name, the plaintiff bears the burden of proving, on the balance of probabilities, that a trust exists such that the beneficial ownership differs from the legal title.

principles.principle_label: Requirements for common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish a common intention constructive trust, a plaintiff must demonstrate: (1) a common intention held with the property owner at the time of purchase (or exceptionally later) that beneficial ownership would differ from legal ownership; (2) that the plaintiff altered their position in detrimental reliance on that common intention; and (3) that it would be unconscionable for the legal owner to assert full ownership.

principles.principle_label: Two limbs of common intention constructive trust
principles.paraphrase_en: The doctrine of common intention constructive trust can arise under two limbs: first, based on evidence of an express agreement, arrangement, or understanding between the parties regarding beneficial ownership; second, where no such express evidence exists, the court may infer a common intention from the conduct of the parties, with some authority indicating direct contributions to the purchase price may be relevant under this limb.

principles.principle_label: Burden and standard of proof for trust claims
principles.paraphrase_en: The party claiming a beneficial interest in property that differs from the legal title bears the burden of proving their claim on the balance of probabilities.

principles.principle_label: Severance by act on own share
principles.paraphrase_en: A joint tenancy can be severed when one joint tenant performs an act upon their own share that has a final or irrevocable character, thereby relinquishing their right of survivorship.
A joint tenancy may be severed by a mutual agreement between the joint tenants, which need not be in writing or specifically enforceable to be effective.

Severance can occur through a course of dealing where one party makes it clear to the other that they intend the property to be held as tenants in common, even if this does not amount to a formal agreement.
A joint tenancy cannot be severed merely by the making of a will by one joint tenant.

principles.principle_label: Discretion to set aside default judgment
principles.paraphrase_en: The court has discretion to set aside a default judgment, balancing the importance of procedural compliance against the principle that a party should not be deprived of an adjudication on the merits.

principles.paraphrase_en: For service of a writ under Order 10 rule 1 to be valid, the defendant must be within the jurisdiction at the time the writ is served, not merely at the time the document is placed in a letterbox.

principles.principle_label: Curing defective service by subsequent knowledge
principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff may prove valid service by showing that the defendant subsequently came within the jurisdiction and gained knowledge of the writ, thereby curing an initial defect in service.

principles.principle_label: Invalid service by postal insertion when defendant abroad
principles.paraphrase_en: Service of a writ by insertion into the letterbox of a defendant's usual address is irregular and invalid if the defendant was not within the jurisdiction at the time of service.

principles.principle_label: Rejection of actual knowledge as sole validation
principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's actual knowledge of proceedings alone does not validate service that was otherwise irregular; the formal requirements for service must still be met.

principles.principle_label: Reversal of guilty plea - interests of justice
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may allow a defendant to withdraw a guilty plea if it is in the interests of justice to do so, particularly where the defendant has consistently maintained a lack of knowledge of an essential element of the offence, the offence is serious, and the potential sentence upon conviction is lengthy.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of truthfulness in plea reversal
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering an application to reverse a guilty plea, the court may grant the application if it finds, after inquiry, that the defendant is or may be telling the truth regarding a claim that goes to the heart of the offence, such as a lack of knowledge of the nature of the items possessed.

candidates.case_name: YIP WAI TAK VIVIAN v. LEE KA WO ESMOND AND OTHERS

principles.principle_label: Reasonable expectation of privacy in complaints
principles.paraphrase_en: A person subject to a complaint has a reasonable expectation that the fact and details of the complaint remain private and confidential until final determination, with disclosure limited only to those strictly necessary for the determination process.
Where there is disagreement about context in defamation cases, the test is what a reasonable person, considering all circumstances, would understand from the material read or seen.

principles.principle_label: Qualified privilege for domestic tribunal communications
principles.paraphrase_en: Communications of complaints and adjudications between members of an association and its domestic tribunal are privileged, provided publication is to persons with a legitimate interest in the subject matter.

principles.principle_label: Moral/social duty test for qualified privilege
principles.paraphrase_en: A moral or social duty for qualified privilege exists if the great mass of right-minded people in the defendant's position would have considered it their duty to make the communication, even if not legally enforceable.

principles.principle_label: Honest belief defense in qualified privilege
principles.paraphrase_en: If a defendant honestly believes a statement to be true, malice is not established merely because the belief was unreasonable, hasty, foolish, or based on insufficient research.

relationships.description: The judgment follows Lord Diplock's authority on qualified privilege regarding honest belief and malice.

principles.paraphrase_en: A party is not entitled to advance a serious allegation of fraud at trial if it was not properly pleaded, as doing so deprives the opposing party of a fair opportunity to prepare a proper defense.

principles.principle_label: Standard for establishing fraudulent breach of trust
principles.paraphrase_en: For a claim based on fraudulent or reckless breach of duties to succeed, there must be evidence to show the transaction was fraudulent. The absence of such evidence, coupled with a credible explanation of honest belief in the transaction's benefit, is fatal to the claim.

principles.principle_label: Relevance of prior regulatory findings in civil fraud claims
principles.paraphrase_en: Findings from prior regulatory proceedings that did not pursue or establish fraud are of little relevance to a subsequent civil action where the claim is specifically based on fraudulent or reckless breach of duties.

A director may reasonably rely on the professional advice and analysis of a subordinate officer (such as a financial controller) in making an investment decision, and such reliance can form part of an honest belief that the decision is in the company's best interests.

A director acting as a responsible businessman is not necessarily required to renegotiate a contractually agreed purchase price due to subsequent market fluctuations, as upholding contractual integrity is a valid commercial consideration.

relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes the findings in the prior regulatory proceeding as irrelevant because that proceeding did not pursue or establish fraud, unlike the current civil claim.

principles.principle_label: Misfeasance in public office - reckless indifference
principles.paraphrase_en: For the tort of misfeasance in public office, reckless indifference to the consequences of an act is sufficient to establish liability, as it is considered as blameworthy as deliberately intending those consequences.
A claim that lacks any legal foundation and cannot possibly succeed may be struck out as being frivolous, vexatious, or an abuse of the court's process.

principles.principle_label: Distinction between tort claim and inquest application
principles.paraphrase_en: An application to the court to order an inquest to be held is a different type of legal proceeding from a claim in tort, governed by different legal principles and procedures.

When considering an application to extend time for a review of taxation, the court should consider: (1) the length of the delay; (2) the reasons for the delay; (3) the merits of the proposed review; and (4) the prejudice to the other party.

Good reasons to re-open a taxation under Order 62 rule 17B exist where a party's failure to object or appear at the taxation hearing was not due to their fault or was caused by factors beyond their control.

principles.principle_label: Exclusion of carelessness as good reason
principles.paraphrase_en: Carelessness, oversight, or pre-occupation do not constitute good reasons for granting an extension of time or re-opening a matter.

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof for election illegal conduct
principles.paraphrase_en: A contravention of section 27(1) of the Elections (Corrupt and Illegal Conduct) Ordinance must be established by evidence that meets a standard equivalent to the criminal standard of proof.

principles.principle_label: Relief for non-compliance with election rules
principles.paraphrase_en: A candidate seeking relief from the consequences of non-compliance with election rules must establish that the non-compliance resulted from inadvertence. Relief will not be granted if the evidence suggests recklessness or a failure to treat election obligations with due significance, and no good reason is shown.

candidates.case_name: 張侃 v. 尚品匯(香港)國際貿易有限公司
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP and Chow JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A person making a representation can be considered an agent in principle, but this is exceptionally rare. For this to occur, two conditions must be met: first, the principal must have granted the alleged agent actual or apparent authority to make that specific representation; second, the representation itself must be clear and unequivocal when assessed against the practical realities of the case.

candidates.case_name: LO PO WAI, HARRY v. WONG YEE CHUK
judges: Before: Hon K Yeung J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The general principles for sentencing in contempt of court cases, as established in prior case law, are applicable to the present case.
A finding of contempt for non-compliance with court orders requires proof beyond reasonable doubt.
Steps taken by a contemnor to remedy the non-compliance, such as producing previously withheld documents, can constitute a mitigating factor in sentencing.
The absence of an intention to defy a court order can be raised as a mitigating factor in contempt proceedings.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the general sentencing principles for contempt of court as set out in this case.

relationships.target_case_name: Alan Chung Wah Tang v Lee Siu Fong
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the sentencing principles for contempt which were cited and applied in this case.

candidates.case_name: WOO KWOK PING v. THE INCORPORATED MANAGEMENT COMMITTEE OF TSUEN WAN TRADE ASSOCIATION PRIMARY SCHOOL
judges: Before: His Honour Judge H. Au-Yeung (Paper Disposal)

principles.principle_label: No private law cause of action from Education Ordinance
principles.paraphrase_en: Sections 55 to 57 of the Education Ordinance do not create a private law cause of action for an employee.

principles.principle_label: Contractual duty to comply with statutory procedure
principles.paraphrase_en: An employer's statutory right to terminate employment for cause may be subject to a contractual duty to first comply with specific statutory procedures, unless such a duty is expressly excluded from the contract.

principles.principle_label: Interlocutory rulings not determinative of substantive rights
principles.paraphrase_en: Observations made in the context of an application for an interlocutory injunction, which focus on whether there is a serious question to be tried, are not determinative rulings on the substantive legal rights in question.

Multiple instances of misconduct, when considered together, can cumulatively amount to a grave breach of duty or gross misconduct sufficient to justify summary dismissal, even if individual instances might not reach that threshold alone.

relationships.target_case_name: Woo Kwok Ping v The Incorporated Management Committee of Tsuen Wan Trade Association Primary School (No.2)
relationships.description: The present judgment clarifies that a cited paragraph from this case was setting out a party's argument, not making a substantive ruling.

relationships.target_case_name: Woo Kwok Ping v The Incorporated Management Committee of Tsuen Wan Trade Association Primary School
relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes this case as it concerned an interlocutory injunction application and did not rule on the creation of a private law cause of action.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheung Chi Keung v The Hospital Authority
relationships.description: The judgment references this case for the proposition that a statutory right of termination can be modified by contractual terms.

candidates.case_name: LEUNG CHI CHING CANDY v. YEUNG HON SING
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Chu JA and Au JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For the defence of qualified privilege to be defeated by malice, it must be shown that the defendant did not believe the defamatory statement was true or was indifferent to its truth or falsity. The burden of proving this improper motive is a heavy one and not lightly satisfied.

principles.principle_label: Test for malice from irrelevant matter
principles.paraphrase_en: When irrelevant defamatory matter is included in a privileged communication, it is a factor to be considered in deciding whether malice can be inferred. The test is not strict logical relevance, but whether, in all circumstances, it can be inferred the defendant did not believe it true or knew it was irrelevant to the privileged occasion.
The finding of malice to defeat qualified privilege requires the application of a subjective test, dependent on the defendant's actual state of mind and intention at the time of publication.
An appellate court deprecates attempts to re-argue submissions on evidence and factual aspects that were not accepted by the trial judge, merely by giving them greater or different emphasis.

There is a strong presumption of judicial integrity and impartiality. Cogent evidence is required to rebut this presumption, and allegations that a judge failed to exercise independent judgment should not be made lightly.

candidates.case_name: SANTA MONICA EQUITY INC. v. CHRISTELLE FILA NZOLOLO
judges: Before: Hon Anthony Chan J in Court

principles.principle_label: Committal for forgery in civil proceedings
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may entertain an application for committal based on alleged forgery of a document adduced in related civil proceedings, such as an application to set aside a statutory demand.

Where a respondent has been properly served with all relevant documents pursuant to a court order for substituted service but chooses not to participate, the court may order the hearing to proceed in their absence.

principles.principle_label: Burden and standard for proving forgery
principles.paraphrase_en: The party alleging forgery bears the burden of proving it, and expert handwriting analysis may be commissioned to assess the authenticity of a disputed document and signature.

candidates.case_name: 張侃 V. 尚品滙(香港)國際貿易有限公司
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Gary C C Lam in Court (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A representation binding a company can be made by a person with actual authority to manage the company's business generally or in relation to the specific contract matter. In principle, an agent can make such a representation about their own authority, though this is considered very rare and unusual in practice.

The purpose of section 117 of the relevant ordinance is to balance protecting outsiders who deal with a company in good faith against protecting the company from directors acting outside their authority. The legislative intent is to limit the application of this section to 'directors' in the plural.
The receipt of a loan by a defendant can be characterized as a ministerial receipt, meaning it is a routine administrative act performed without the exercise of independent discretion.

The law distinguishes when an agent's knowledge is attributed to the principal. Knowledge of fraud by an agent is not attributed to the principal when the principal sues the agent for losses from that fraud. However, such knowledge is attributed to the principal when a third party brings a claim against the principal based on the agent's fraud.

relationships.target_case_name: Moulin Global Eyecare Trading Ltd v CIR
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principles from Moulin Global regarding the attribution of an agent's knowledge to the principal in fraud cases.
The judgment cites the authoritative textbook Bowstead & Reynolds on Agency to support the legal principle on attribution of knowledge.

candidates.case_name: BRETON JEAN v. HK BELLAWINGS.JET LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Patrick Siu in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For a statement to be defamatory, it must meet a threshold of seriousness that excludes trivial claims. The imputation must tend to injure a person's reputation in their business, employment, trade, profession, calling, or office.
The meaning conveyed by allegedly defamatory words is determined from the perspective of an ordinary, reasonable, fair-minded reader, guided by established principles.

An occasion is protected by qualified privilege where the person making the communication has a legal, social, or moral duty or interest to make it to the recipient, and the recipient has a corresponding duty or interest to receive it. There is no closed list of such occasions.

Whether an occasion is privileged is determined by applying the general principle to all the circumstances, viewed with contemporary eyes, as social conditions affect what the public interest requires to be protected.

principles.principle_label: Malice to defeat qualified privilege - two-stage test
principles.paraphrase_en: To rebut a defence of qualified privilege by proving malice, two sequential questions must be answered: first, whether the occasion was privileged and for what purpose (a question of law); second, whether the defendant's actual purpose in publishing was that privileged purpose (a question of fact).

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for guidance on how to determine the meaning conveyed by words to an ordinary, reasonable reader in a defamation claim.

relationships.target_case_name: Leung Chi Ching Candy v Yeung Hon Sing
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that finding malice to defeat qualified privilege requires applying a subjective test focused on the defendant's state of mind and intention at the time of publication.

candidates.case_name: SAMANTHA JANE BRADLEY v. SIR ELLY KADOORIE & SONS LIMITED (FOR AND ON BEHALF OF ITSELF, ITS CURRENT AND FORMER OFFICERS, EMPLOYEES AND AGENTS, INCLUDING ITS LEGAL REPRESENTATIVES, MESSRS SIMMONS & SIMMONS)
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Lam PJ, Mr Justice Bokhary NPJ, Mr Justice Chan NPJ and Lord Neuberger of Abbotsbury NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: A court has jurisdiction to grant a freestanding injunction to restrain alleged harassment, even if the plaintiff does not have a personal cause of action, where it is necessary to uphold the administration of justice or prevent abuse of process.

A public authority (or analogous entity) can apply for an injunction to prevent conduct that interferes with its performance of its statutory or public functions, where it is just and convenient to do so, even without a personal cause of action.

principles.principle_label: Employer's duty to prevent workplace harassment
principles.paraphrase_en: An employer's common law duty to provide a safe system of work extends to taking reasonable steps to prevent reasonably foreseeable harassment of an employee by third parties, including other employees or customers.

Where an employer has a duty to protect an employee from foreseeable harm, including harassment, the court may grant an injunction to enable the employer to discharge that duty, even if the employer lacks a personal cause of action for the tort committed against the employee.

principles.principle_label: Analogous jurisdiction to Attorney General injunctions
principles.paraphrase_en: The jurisdiction to grant an injunction to an employer to protect an employee from harassment, despite the employer having no personal cause of action, is analogous to the court granting an injunction to the Attorney General to enforce the law in the public interest.

relationships.target_case_name: Broadmoor Special Hospital Authority v Robinson
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Broadmoor that a public authority can seek an injunction to prevent interference with its statutory functions.

relationships.target_case_name: Abbasi v Newcastle upon Tyne Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust
relationships.description: The judgment follows the UK Supreme Court's affirmation in Abbasi of the Broadmoor principle regarding injunctions to protect statutory functions.
The judgment cites R v Rollins as endorsing Lord Woolf MR's statement of principle from the Broadmoor case.
The judgment applies the principle from Walsh that an employer can be responsible for failing to foresee and prevent criminal assaults on an employee.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LO SING CHEE (also known as LO SHING CHI)
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SETO CHI KWONG ak SZETO CHI KWONG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Casewell in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: There are three established tests for determining whether a sentence is manifestly excessive: the absurdity test, the conversion test, and the ratio test.

Sentencing for drug offenses should not follow a rigid arithmetical approach based solely on narcotic quantity. Instead, a more discretionary approach is required, assessing the individual's personal role and culpability.
A six-stage approach is appropriate for sentencing in certain drug cases, moving beyond a purely quantity-based calculation.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the three tests for manifestly excessive sentence established in Chang Yuet Yu.
The present judgment follows the approach from Herry Yusuph, which rejected a rigid arithmetical sentencing method based on drug quantity.

candidates.case_name: REGISTRAR OF THE HONG KONG INSTITUTE OF CERTIFIED PUBLIC ACCOUNTANTS v. CHAN YUI HANG
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Yuen and Chow JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Timing requirement for annual creditors' meetings
principles.paraphrase_en: An annual meeting of creditors in a winding-up must be held within three months of the anniversary date of the commencement of the winding-up. A meeting held outside this period is invalid.

For a creditors' meeting to be valid, the procedural requirements under the Companies (Winding Up) Rules must be strictly complied with, including proper gazettal of the meeting notice and the drawing up and keeping of minutes.

A liquidator cannot delegate away their ultimate responsibility for the conduct of the liquidation. They remain responsible for the work and must retain overall control, even if specific tasks are contracted out.

Liquidator's fees must be properly fixed by the creditors in accordance with the statutory scheme. Approval by a single 'major creditor' at an irregular meeting does not constitute proper approval under the ordinance.

A party who indicates an intention to call witnesses must identify the precise facts in dispute and the relevant evidence those witnesses can give. A failure to do so, or to take steps to secure their attendance, justifies proceeding without them.

candidates.case_name: MAMMY PANCAKE COMPANY LTD v. CARLA DAY LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Fung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: Claims of fraud or dishonesty must be pleaded with sufficient detail; stating facts that could also indicate honest conduct is inadequate.
Where a defendant deliberately evades service, such as by returning properly sent mail to a provided address, the court may deem service to be validly effected.

candidates.case_name: GALLERIA (HONG KONG), LTD AND ANOTHER v. DBS BANK LTD., HONG KONG BRANCH Defendant
judges: Before: Hon Kwan Ag CJHC, Cheung JA and Au JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The correct approach to assess dishonesty in a dishonest assistance claim involves a two-stage test: first, ascertain what the defendant actually knew, and second, objectively assess whether, based on that knowledge, the defendant's conduct was dishonest.

principles.principle_label: Knowing receipt barred by valid contract
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim in knowing receipt cannot succeed where the defendant's receipt of the property was made pursuant to a valid and binding contract, unless that contract has been set aside.

relationships.target_case_name: Criterion Properties v Stratford UK Properties
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Criterion that a knowing receipt claim is barred by a valid and binding contract governing the receipt.

candidates.case_name: POLYLINE DEVELOPMENT LTD v. CHING LIN CHUEN AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Mr Recorder Manzoni SC in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Striking out pleadings - reasonable cause of action
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering an application to strike out a pleading for disclosing no reasonable cause of action, the court must assume the pleaded facts are true and cannot consider evidence. A pleading should not be struck out merely for lacking particulars; instead, an order for particulars should be made.

An allegation of fraud or dishonesty must be sufficiently particularized with primary facts. The test at the pleading stage is whether the pleaded facts, if proven, would make an inference of dishonesty more likely than one of innocence or negligence. The ultimate assessment of whether the evidence justifies the inference is for trial.

Where property is transferred voluntarily without consideration, a resulting trust is presumed in favor of the transferor. This presumption can be rebutted by evidence of an intention to make a gift or by the presumption of advancement.

principles.principle_label: Striking out - bound to fail test
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim should only be struck out as an abuse of process or frivolous if it is shown to be bound to fail. The fact that a claim is based on conjecture is not itself a bar; the key is whether the claim has no realistic prospect of success.

Declaratory statements in a deed intended to be contractually binding can found an estoppel, consistent with the principle of party autonomy underlying contract law. This applies similarly to estoppel by convention arising from express or implied contractual agreements.

relationships.target_case_name: Asgain Co Ltd v Cheng Yan Kan (No 2)
relationships.description: The present judgment cites this Court of Appeal case as upholding the principle of estoppel by deed or convention in Hong Kong.

relationships.target_case_name: Chung Pui Tong v Qian Zhen
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case per Mimmie Chan J as supporting the principle of estoppel by deed or convention.

relationships.target_case_name: Szeto Wing Hong v Maintown Industries Ltd & Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case per Linda Chan J as further authority upholding the principle of estoppel by deed or convention.

candidates.case_name: TANG YUAN YI v. TOKYO INTERNATIONAL INVESTMENT LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge William Wong SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The fraud exception to summary judgment is engaged only if the plaintiff must allege and prove fraud against the defendant to establish or maintain its claim. It is not engaged merely because the claim arises from a background of fraud perpetrated by a third party.

A court should not liberally infer that a party is making an allegation of deliberate dishonesty. Without an express allegation, dishonesty should only be inferred if the act or omission complained of is inherently or implicitly dishonest by its very nature.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of all materials for fraud exception
principles.paraphrase_en: When determining if the fraud exception applies, the court must consider all materials, including subsequent pleadings and affidavits in reply, not just the initial claim.

principles.principle_label: Unjust enrichment claim independent of fraud
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim in unjust enrichment against a recipient of funds can be established without the plaintiff needing to allege or prove fraud against that defendant, even if the funds originated from a scam perpetrated by a third party.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the test from Universal Capital Bank to determine whether the fraud exception to summary judgment is engaged.
The judgment applies the principles established in Zimmer regarding when the fraud exception is engaged in an unjust enrichment claim.

relationships.target_case_name: Laerdal Medical Limited v Hong Kong Haocheng International Trade Limited
relationships.description: The judgment follows the reasoning in Laerdal, which applied Zimmer and Universal Capital Bank to hold the fraud exception was not engaged in a similar email fraud/unjust enrichment case.

relationships.target_case_name: Menfond Electronic Art & Computer Design Co., Ltd v Wong Wang Tat Victor & Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites Menfond for the principle that an allegation of dishonesty should not be inferred liberally without an express charge.

candidates.case_name: KWONG YI LING (suing on behalf of herself and all other shareholders in the 6th Defendant except the 1st and 2nd Defendants) v. LAU KWUN LEUNG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Linda Chan J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Scope of assistance in breach of trust
principles.paraphrase_en: The concept of 'assistance' in a breach of trust is not limited to acts that occur before or at the same time as the breach. The nature of the specific breach determines what constitutes assistance.

relationships.description: The judgment references the text's explanation that assistance normally precedes or is contemporaneous with a breach of trust.
The judgment cites this text for its alternative statement of the principle concerning assistance in a breach of trust.

candidates.case_name: THE LAW SOCIETY OF HONG KONG v. A SOLICITOR
judges: Before : Hon Yuen JA, Barma JA and Chow JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The professional disciplinary body is in a uniquely advantageous position to assess and discharge its duties in disciplinary matters.
Orders made by a solicitors' disciplinary tribunal are not primarily punitive; their purposes are to prevent repetition of the offence and to uphold the reputation of the legal profession.

relationships.description: The Tribunal applied the principle from this case regarding the non-punitive purpose of disciplinary orders.

candidates.case_name: WELLSON SILK TRADING LTD v. CHEUNG WAI YING AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Ng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A minority shareholder may initiate a statutory derivative action in the name of a company after obtaining leave from the court.
In a statutory derivative action, the relief sought (such as setting aside a sale of property) is pursued in the name of the company, not the individual shareholder who obtained leave.

candidates.case_name: KWOK HIU KWAN v. CONVOY GLOBAL HOLDINGS LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.principle_label: Director's duty of good faith - subjective test with objective elements
principles.paraphrase_en: A director's duty to act in good faith in the company's best interests is generally subjective, but becomes more difficult to prove if the challenged action caused substantial detriment to the company. If there is no evidence the director actually considered the company's interests, the test becomes objective: whether an intelligent and honest director could have reasonably believed the action was in the company's interests.

The duty to exercise powers for proper purposes is assessed objectively. The court must identify the power, its proper purpose as delegated, the actual purpose for which it was exercised, and determine if that purpose was proper. The director's honest belief about acting in the company's interests is irrelevant to this assessment.

principles.principle_label: No conflict duty - objective and inflexible rule
principles.paraphrase_en: The duty to avoid conflicts of interest is assessed objectively: whether a reasonable person would see a real or substantial possibility of conflict. This duty is strictly enforced as an inflexible rule, regardless of conscious wrongdoing or whether the company actually suffered loss.

principles.principle_label: Articles void if contrary to companies legislation
principles.paraphrase_en: Any provision in a company's articles of association that conflicts with the relevant companies legislation is void. This principle is based on public policy and applies generally.

principles.principle_label: Court's reluctance to micromanage internal affairs
principles.paraphrase_en: The court will generally not intervene to micromanage issues of internal company management that fall within the remit of the board of directors or shareholders in general meeting, especially where shareholders could resolve the complaints before court determination.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as part of the line of authority establishing that articles conflicting with companies legislation are void.

relationships.target_case_name: Re Greater Beijing Region Expressways Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as part of the development of the principle that articles contrary to companies legislation are void.

relationships.target_case_name: Ghossoub v Team Y&R Holdings Hong Kong Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this unreported Court of Appeal case which references the GBRE case on the voidness of articles contrary to legislation.

candidates.case_name: NEW SPARKLE ROLL INTERNATIONAL GROUP LTD v. ZHENG HAO JIANG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Alexander Stock SC in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Subjective test for director's breach of duty
principles.paraphrase_en: To establish a breach of duty by directors, a company must prove the directors acted dishonestly and not in the company's bona fide interests. The test is subjective, focusing on whether the directors honestly believed their actions were in the company's interest, not whether the actions were objectively beneficial or merely negligent.

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof for dishonesty allegations
principles.paraphrase_en: Allegations of fraud or dishonesty are serious and require a commensurately high standard of proof. Inferences of dishonesty should not be drawn lightly.

principles.principle_label: Caution in summary determination of dishonesty
principles.paraphrase_en: While it is permissible in principle to resolve allegations of dishonesty summarily, the authorities indicate that courts should exercise caution in doing so.

candidates.case_name: LAW YIU WAI RAY AND ANOTHER v. CHEUNG YING MAN also known as CHRISTINA HADIWIBAWA
judges: Before: Mr Recorder Manzoni SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A court should not determine whether a document or arrangement is protected by legal professional privilege on a strike-out application, as this requires detailed factual investigation.

The scope and application of a statutory exemption, particularly when its meaning is difficult to ascertain, is not suitable for determination on a strike-out application and should be decided at trial after all facts are known.

principles.principle_label: Strike-out suitability for waiver of privilege
principles.paraphrase_en: The question of whether a party has waived confidentiality or privilege through their conduct is fact-intensive and cannot be properly determined on a strike-out application, requiring a full trial.

principles.principle_label: Pleading requirement for common law duty of confidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim based on a common law duty of confidence must be pleaded fully and clearly, with sufficient particularity.
If a party discloses information they should not have disclosed, or mischaracterizes an arrangement, the affected party is entitled to seek remedies through properly pleaded causes of action.

candidates.case_name: CHINA MEDICAL TECHNOLOGIES, INC (IN LIQUIDATION) AND OTHERS v. SAMSON TSANG TAK YUNG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Au and Chow JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Stable-door point as part of risk assessment
principles.paraphrase_en: The 'stable-door' point concerning whether assets have already been dissipated is not a separate legal issue but is instead integrated into the overall assessment of the risk of asset dissipation when considering an injunction.

principles.principle_label: Equity will not act in vain
principles.paraphrase_en: The principle that equity will not act in vain, which holds that an injunction should not be granted if the defendant's assets have already been dissipated, is reflected in the consideration of the 'stable-door' point within the risk assessment.

relationships.description: The judgment references this case as having cited the principle that equity will not act in vain in the context of injunctions and asset dissipation.

relationships.target_case_name: Ras al Khaimah Investment Authority v Bestfort Development LLP
relationships.description: The judgment notes this English Court of Appeal case criticized and rejected the approach taken in Re Chau Cham Wong Patrick regarding the 'stable-door' point.

candidates.case_name: RICH ENGINEERING LTD v. FIRE SERVICE INSTALLATION CONTRACTORS DISCIPLINARY BOARD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Gary CC Lam in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A disciplinary board must provide reasons for its sentencing decisions, including its choice of penalty, the duration of any removal order, decisions on concurrency or consecutiveness of sentences, and the application of the totality principle.

An appeal under Order 55 rule 3(1) is conducted as a rehearing, but not one that completely disregards the original proceedings or decision. The appellate court does not typically rehear evidence or permit re-cross-examination, but must give proper weight to the tribunal's decision.
For an appeal to succeed, the appellant must demonstrate that the tribunal erred in law, departed from an applicable principle, or misapprehended the facts or evidence.

principles.principle_label: Burden on applicant alleging procedural handicap
principles.paraphrase_en: An applicant alleging that a disciplinary board considered itself procedurally handicapped in its sentencing deliberations bears a heavy burden of proof to establish that fact.

candidates.case_name: ZOU GEBING v. HONG KONG XINXIN INFORMATION LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge MK Liu in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A proprietary injunction must be tied to a specific asset under the defendant's control or its traceable proceeds, over which the plaintiff asserts a proprietary claim.
For knowing receipt liability, the defendant must have actually received the property for which they can be held accountable as constructive trustee; mere abstract benefit is insufficient.
The threshold for granting a proprietary injunction is lower than for a Mareva injunction; the applicant need only show a serious question to be tried on the merits.

principles.principle_label: No automatic balance of convenience for trust property
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no general principle that the balance of convenience automatically favors preserving trust property once a serious issue is shown; each case depends on its own facts.

In considering an interlocutory injunction, the court assesses the real risk that either party may be unable to obtain adequate compensation if the injunction is later found to have been wrongly granted or refused.

relationships.target_case_name: Pacific Rainbow International Inc v Shenzhen Wolverine Tech Ltd & Ors
relationships.description: Cited by the plaintiff to support the lower threshold for proprietary injunctions compared to Mareva injunctions.

relationships.target_case_name: 李明實, 方壘 and史洪源 (suing on behalf of themselves and the other employees employed by or by the Group of Companies under和利時科技集團有限公司) v Ace Lead Profits Limited and Another
relationships.description: The Court of Appeal decision is applied to reject a general principle that balance of convenience automatically favors preserving trust property.

relationships.target_case_name: Heitkamp & Thumann KG v Living Profit Trading Development Ltd & Ors
relationships.description: Distinguished as involving email fraud and not establishing a general principle for proprietary injunctions.
Distinguished as concerning discovery in aid of Mareva injunction for trust property, not a general principle for proprietary injunctions.

candidates.case_name: RE ZPMC-RED BOX ENERGY SERVICES LTD
judges: Before: Hon Cheng J in Court

principles.principle_label: Onus for establishing cross-claim in winding-up
principles.paraphrase_en: A company opposing a winding-up petition based on a cross-claim bears the burden of proving that the cross-claim is genuine, serious, and substantial, supported by relevant details demonstrating substantial grounds.

The standard for assessing whether a cross-claim is sufficient to resist a winding-up petition is the same as the test for a disputed debt petition: it must be disputed in good faith and on substantial grounds.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the test established in Re Sinom for determining whether a debt or cross-claim is disputed on substantial grounds.

candidates.case_name: LAU TUNG HOI KENT v. LAU TUNG KUEN
judges: Before: Mr. Recorder Jin Pao, SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: In administration proceedings involving hostile litigation between a beneficiary and an executor, the general starting point is that the unsuccessful party should pay the costs of the successful party.

The court may classify litigation in estate administration into categories, with the third Re Buckton category covering adverse claims in hostile litigation brought by a beneficiary against an executor.

principles.principle_label: Dishonesty not required for adverse costs
principles.paraphrase_en: The absence of a finding of dishonesty against an unsuccessful party is not sufficient to displace the general principle that the unsuccessful party should pay costs in hostile beneficiary-executor litigation.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the third category from Re Buckton to classify the litigation as a hostile claim by a beneficiary against an executor.

candidates.case_name: CHINACHEM CHARITABLE FOUNDATION LTD v. CHAN WAI TONG CHRISTOPHER AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Cheung JA and Yuen JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A claim alleging fraud or dishonest conduct may be struck out if it is made without any proper factual basis to support the allegation.

A statement of claim may be struck out on grounds including that it discloses no reasonable cause of action, is scandalous, frivolous or vexatious, may prejudice or delay a fair trial, or is an abuse of court process.

principles.principle_label: Court's approach to striking out applications
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering a striking out application, the court must determine whether the matters relied on in the claim raise genuine issues that ought to proceed to trial, even when making assumptions in favour of the claimant.

candidates.case_name: CLARK QUANTUM KENT (formerly known as John Mohammad Clark and John Julius Tasca) v. HAI TIN LTD (formerly known as Allied Finance Asia LTd) AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Barma JA and Au JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Test for leave to serve out of jurisdiction
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's approach to deciding whether to grant leave to serve process outside the jurisdiction is analogous to the test for summary judgment under Order 14 of the Rules of the High Court.

Summary judgment under Order 14 is not available for claims involving allegations of fraud, and the court should not investigate the merits of such allegations when determining if a good arguable case exists for service out of jurisdiction.

principles.principle_label: Good arguable case standard for service out
principles.paraphrase_en: The court must be satisfied that the plaintiff has a good arguable case on the merits of the claim when considering an application for leave to serve process outside the jurisdiction.
A plaintiff seeking leave to serve process outside the jurisdiction must demonstrate that the claim falls within one or more of the specified gateways under Order 11 of the Rules of the High Court.

A claim may qualify for service outside the jurisdiction under the contractual gateway of Order 11 if it is made in respect of a contract that was made within the jurisdiction, governed by Hong Kong law, or contains a term to that effect.

candidates.case_name: TSE MAN YIU AND OTHERS v. TSE MAN WAI AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The primary purpose of allowing amendments to pleadings is to ensure the court can decide the actual substantive dispute between the parties or to correct procedural errors.
Unless there is genuine prejudice, a court should generally allow a late amendment to enable the real claims and defences to be adjudicated, prioritizing substance over procedural technicalities.
A party relying on the defence of illegality must specifically plead it in their statement of case.
The defence of illegality can apply in civil wrongs concerning private interests, such as tort (excluding those requiring dishonesty), breach of contract, and statutory wrongs.

An agreement where an eligible purchaser under a public housing scheme does not intend to own or reside in the property, but instead uses their eligibility to benefit ineligible third parties, constitutes a misuse of the scheme and is not a legitimate pooling of family resources.

relationships.target_case_name: Sino Favour Development Ltd & Anor v Kan Wai Chung & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment cites Fung J's ruling on the requirement to plead illegality.
The judgment cites this case for the principle on when illegality applies to private wrongs.
The judgment cites this case indirectly via Monat Investment for the scope of the illegality defence.

candidates.case_name: THE LAW SOCIETY OF HONG KONG v. JIM YIU MING AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Au JA and Chow JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Suspension is generally appropriate for a solicitor who has not acted dishonestly but whose conduct has fallen below the required standards of integrity, probity, and trustworthiness. The test for applying this principle is conjunctive, requiring all three elements to be engaged.

principles.principle_label: Use of sentencing precedents in disciplinary cases
principles.paraphrase_en: It is unhelpful to cite previous sentencing cases to argue for a lighter or heavier sentence by comparing seriousness. Each case must be determined on its own facts, and sentences in other cases are not designed as binding precedents, as cases involving lapses of professional standards vary in gravity.
Principals of a law firm are responsible for the acts or omissions of the firm, other partners, and staff under professional conduct rules.

principles.principle_label: Duty to respond to regulatory inquiries
principles.paraphrase_en: Solicitors have a professional duty to reply fully and promptly to inquiries from the Law Society.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Bolton v Law Society for the principle that suspension is generally appropriate when a solicitor's conduct falls below standards of integrity, probity, and trustworthiness.

candidates.case_name: ORIENTAL TEXTILE PRODUCTS LTD v. ASIA TELEVISION HOLDINGS LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Gary CC Lam in Chambers (open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may still make a costs order against a party as a matter of principle, even when the opposing party indicates it does not seek costs, and it is for the beneficiary of the order to decide whether to enforce it.

candidates.case_name: JSP INTERNATIONAL S.R.O. v. ALACRITY LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Cheng J in Chambers (by Paper Disposal)

principles.paraphrase_en: An allegation of fraud or dishonesty must be pleaded with sufficient particularity. It is insufficient to merely state facts that could be consistent with honest conduct; the specific primary facts from which dishonesty is to be inferred must be clearly set out so the defendant knows the case to meet.

A court may grant a declaration that a plaintiff has a proprietary interest in funds held by a defendant, but may refuse to grant a further declaration that the defendant holds those funds on constructive trust for the plaintiff. These are distinct forms of relief.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites Edison Norge AS as an example where declarations of proprietary interest were granted but further declarations of constructive trust were refused.

candidates.case_name: EVERCARE HEALTH LTD v. SO LAI KAN also known as GOBBY SO AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge B. Mak in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A proprietary injunction must be connected to a specific asset, or its traceable proceeds, that is held by or under the control of the defendant and is the subject of a proprietary claim by the plaintiff.

To obtain a proprietary injunction, the plaintiff must generally provide reasonable evidence showing the existence of the specific asset (or its traceable proceeds) and that it is held by or under the defendant's control.

principles.principle_label: Limitation on proprietary injunction for dissipated assets
principles.paraphrase_en: A proprietary injunction will not normally be granted if the asset in question has been dissipated and is no longer traceable.

candidates.case_name: KWOK LAI SHEUNG v. MORE INSURANCE BROKER COMPANY LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge B. Mak in Chambers (Open to Public)

candidates.case_name: CHINA MEDICAL TECHNOLOGIES INC (In Liquidation) AND OTHERS v. THE BANK OF EAST ASIA, LTD
judges: Before: Hon Ng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: Allegations of fraud or dishonesty must be pleaded with sufficient particularity; stating facts that could be consistent with honest conduct is insufficient. The defendant must know both the allegation of dishonesty and the primary facts from which that inference is to be drawn.

principles.principle_label: Bank's receipt of funds - ministerial vs beneficial capacity
principles.paraphrase_en: It is not a settled principle that a bank always receives funds in a purely ministerial capacity when a trustee deposits trust money into a personal account. The law in this area is difficult and unsettled, making it unsuitable for determination on a striking-out application.

principles.principle_label: Fraud not essential for knowing receipt/unauthorised act claims
principles.paraphrase_en: Fraud is not an essential element of claims based on want of authority or knowing receipt. Therefore, statutory provisions relating to the limitation period for actions based upon fraud do not apply to such claims.

The practice of deciding preliminary points of law should generally be confined to cases where the facts are complicated and the legal issue is short and easily decided. Cases outside this guiding principle should be exceptional.

principles.principle_label: Fraudulent trading vs dishonest assistance distinction
principles.paraphrase_en: Liability for fraudulent trading under statute requires that the defendant was carrying on the business of the company. This is distinct from, and should not be equated with, liability for dishonestly assisting breaches of duty by the company's directors.

relationships.target_case_name: Hing Yip Holdings (Hong Kong) Limited v Cellmark China Limited
relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case for its obiter analysis concluding that fraud is not an essential element of claims based on want of authority or knowing receipt.
The judgment references this case in the context of the principle that statutory limitation periods for fraud do not apply where fraud is not an essential ingredient of the cause of action.

relationships.target_case_name: George Chu v Tan Giong Seng Johnson
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that it is not in the interests of justice to relieve a party from an 'unless order' where they have consciously decided not to comply.

candidates.case_name: TSUI PING SUM AND ANOTHER v. CHAN KA LOK AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Le Pichon in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Strike out application sole issue determination
principles.paraphrase_en: When determining a strike out application, the court may focus on a single, central issue to decide the application, such as whether a party has properly pleaded a necessary element of a claim like knowledge in conspiracy or dishonest assistance.

If a claim for conspiratorial agreement fails, a plaintiff may alternatively pursue a claim for misrepresentation. Such a claim can be based on fraudulent misrepresentation if the defendant knew the representation was false, or on negligence if the defendant was careless. A statutory misrepresentation claim may shift the burden of proof.

A constructive trust may be imposed as a remedy for unjust enrichment where the defendant is enriched at the claimant's expense. However, the claimant must demonstrate the case falls within a recognised category where such restitution has been awarded, or justify it as a principled extension from such a category.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites the Court of Appeal's decision in the Dingway case regarding the refusal of leave to appeal on a strike out application concerning the pleading of knowledge in conspiracy and dishonest assistance.
The judgment cites Derry v Peek as the foundational type of case for a claim in fraudulent misrepresentation.
The judgment cites Lord Carnworth's holding in Ramsden v Dyson, indicating it is referencing established authority on a point of law.

candidates.case_name: GALLERIA (HONG KONG), LTD (IN COMPULSORY LIQUIDATION) AND ANOTHER v. DBS BANK LTD., HONG KONG BRANCH
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Cheung JA and Au JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Suspicions that do not meet the threshold for establishing blind-eye knowledge are not irrelevant to the assessment of dishonesty in dishonest assistance claims. The court must first ascertain all relevant facts, including the defendant's knowledge and beliefs.

The test for dishonesty involves two distinct stages: first, ascertaining the facts known to the defendant, and second, objectively assessing whether their conduct was dishonest based on those facts. The court must not conflate these stages.

A finding that a defendant turned a blind eye requires evidence that they knew or suspected a fraud and deliberately chose not to investigate. The inherent improbability of a bank acting dishonestly is not, by itself, a decisive factor.
The standard of unconscionability for knowing receipt is distinct from and lower than the standard of dishonesty required for dishonest assistance. The two should not be treated as the same.

candidates.case_name: CHINA MEDICAL TECHNOLOGIES, INC. (IN LIQUIDATION) AND OTHERS v. SAMSON TSANG TAK YUNG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Au and Chow JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Inference of dissipation risk from dishonesty
principles.paraphrase_en: Where the alleged dishonesty is central to the claim against a defendant, the court may infer a real risk of asset dissipation from establishing a good arguable case of that dishonesty.

Delay in applying for a Mareva injunction may be justified if the claimant could not properly formulate their claim earlier, for instance due to impediments in investigation like missing records or lack of cooperation from relevant parties.

An ex parte without notice application for an injunction requires justification, such as urgency or a need for confidentiality. The absence of such justification constitutes a procedural irregularity warranting the injunction's discharge.

Establishing a real risk of asset dissipation depends on the specific facts of the case. Factors considered are often not direct evidence of dissipation but relate to the nature of the defendant's assets and their behavior.

principles.principle_label: Good arguable case from unexplained receipts
principles.paraphrase_en: A good arguable case for a claim like dishonest assistance or knowing receipt can be established by evidence that the defendant received substantial funds belonging to the claimant without providing a convincing justification or documentation for the entitlement.

relationships.target_case_name: Lakatamia Shipping Co Ltd v Toshiko Morimoto
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Lakatamia that a good arguable case of central dishonesty can support an inference of a real risk of dissipation.
The judgment cites the textbook for its discussion on the requirement of 'solid evidence' for a risk of dissipation and relevant factors.

candidates.case_name: LI NGAN KWAN AND OTHERS v. GAO LI HUI AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Resulting trust arising from property purchase
principles.paraphrase_en: A resulting trust may arise when a property is purchased in the name of a limited company but the purchase price is partly funded by a shareholder. The company may hold the property on resulting trust for that shareholder as the beneficial owner.

A person of full age and understanding who signs a document with legal effect is bound by it, unless they can establish a recognized legal basis that vitiates their apparent consent, such as fraud, mistake, misrepresentation, non est factum, duress, undue influence, or lack of capacity.

The guiding principle for allowing amendments to pleadings is to determine the real questions in controversy between the parties or to correct defects in the proceedings. Absent real prejudice, courts should allow amendments to enable adjudication on the real issues, and technical rules should not prevent parties from raising their genuine claims or defenses.

A court may question whether a claim by a shareholder for the value of an asset held by a company constitutes reflective loss, which is a loss suffered by the company that is merely reflected in the diminution of the shareholder's share value.

relationships.target_case_name: Lo Man Yau v Chiu Sung Fai & anor
relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case as an example concerning a property held by a company where the purchase price was partly funded by a shareholder.
The present judgment applies the legal principles on resulting trusts as approved from this unreported case.

relationships.target_case_name: National Bank of Commerce v National Westminster Bank
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case, referenced in McGee, for the proposition that an action for a declaration does not become time-barred.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YEOH, POH LEAN
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal under section 113 of the Magistrates Ordinance proceeds as a rehearing based on the original evidence, with the appellate court having discretion to admit additional evidence.
An appellate court will not overturn a magistrate's factual findings unless they are plainly wrong, recognizing the magistrate's advantage in hearing evidence firsthand.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Chou Shih Bin v. HKSAR to support the principle that appellate courts should defer to magistrates' factual findings unless plainly wrong.

candidates.case_name: PARVEJ v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge Levy :

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN PUI YU
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Edmond Lee in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant's explanation for possessing lost property is rejected, the court may infer dishonest appropriation with intent to permanently deprive the owner if the undisputed evidence supports no other reasonable conclusion.

The test for dishonesty requires the court to determine (1) whether the defendant's conduct was dishonest by the standards of reasonable and honest people, and (2) whether the defendant realized their conduct was dishonest by those standards.

principles.principle_label: Burden on prosecution after defense rejection
principles.paraphrase_en: Rejecting the defendant's evidence does not automatically establish guilt; the prosecution must still prove its case based on the remaining evidence, which must support an irresistible inference of guilt.

relationships.description: The appellant seeks to rely on this case as involving similar facts regarding the finding of lost property.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAM HING KAI
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge M Chow in Court (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Elements of conspiracy to money launder
principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits the offense of conspiracy to money launder by agreeing with others to deal with property known or believed to represent the proceeds of an indictable offense.

principles.principle_label: Actus reus of money laundering conspiracy
principles.paraphrase_en: The actus reus of the conspiracy is established by the defendant's actions in surrendering control of his bank account to another person for the purpose of receiving funds.

principles.principle_label: Mens rea for money laundering conspiracy
principles.paraphrase_en: The mens rea requires that the defendant knew or had reasonable grounds to believe that the property handled represented the proceeds of an indictable offense.

candidates.case_name: DAWAN MUNSOR v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge Michael WONG :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement claims is limited to examining the decision for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality; it does not involve a fresh assessment of the evidence or factual findings.
Leave to apply for judicial review will only be granted if the applicant presents a reasonably arguable claim with realistic prospects of success.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the limited grounds for judicial review intervention in non-refoulement decisions.

relationships.target_case_name: Po Fun Chan v Winnie Cheung
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Court of Final Appeal's test for granting leave to apply for judicial review.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HO KWUN MAN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge M Chow in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish the offence of trafficking in dangerous drugs under section 4(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Ordinance, the prosecution must prove the defendant was involved in an act of trafficking, which includes selling, offering for sale, distributing, or possessing for the purpose of trafficking, a dangerous drug.

Possession of a dangerous drug, when coupled with evidence indicating an intent to traffic (such as messages discussing supply, price, and photographs of drugs on a scale), can support a conviction for trafficking, not mere possession for personal consumption.

candidates.case_name: LAW TING PONG SECONDARY SCHOOL v. CHEN WAI WAH
judges: Before: Hon Yuen, Chu and Barma JJA in Court

candidates.case_name: LANTAU INTERNATIONAL SCHOOL LTD v. SECRETARY FOR EDUCATION
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A public authority is permitted to establish and operate a general policy to guide its exercise of statutory discretion, but it must not apply that policy so rigidly that it becomes an unlawful fetter on its discretion to consider the individual circumstances of each case.

A policy or protocol is unlawful if it is so rigid that it prevents a decision-maker from exercising their statutory discretion properly in individual cases, effectively binding them to a predetermined outcome.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of policy lawfulness vs. application
principles.paraphrase_en: A challenge to the lawfulness of a policy itself is distinct from a challenge to its application; a policy may be lawful in principle but become unlawful if implemented in a manner that fails to give proper consideration to individual merits.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Collymore that a lawful general policy can become unlawful if implemented too rigidly, preventing proper individual consideration.
The judgment cites Adath Yisroel as an example where a rigid policy was found to be an unlawful fetter on a decision-maker's statutory discretion.

candidates.case_name: NG YAN KIT ALFRED AND ANOTHER v. EVER HONEST INDUSTRIES LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Lok J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A contractual clause will be held to be a penalty if the stipulated sum is extravagant and unconscionable in amount compared to the greatest conceivable loss from the breach.
A clause will be a penalty if the breach consists solely of not paying a sum of money, and the stipulated sum is greater than the sum that ought to have been paid.

The true test for a penalty is whether the provision imposes a detriment on the contract-breaker out of all proportion to any legitimate interest of the innocent party in enforcing the primary obligation. The innocent party's interest is in performance or an appropriate alternative, not in punishment.

principles.principle_label: No duty to mitigate for contractual payment in lieu
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a contract provides for a payment in lieu of notice and the employer lawfully elects to make such payment, the employee is contractually entitled to that payment and is under no duty to mitigate the loss.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of liquidated damages in employment
principles.paraphrase_en: When assessing whether a payment-in-lieu clause in an employment contract is an unenforceable penalty, the court may consider the employer's legitimate interests, such as avoiding disruption to operations and the difficulty of finding a replacement.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an example where a clause providing for a year's salary and benefits upon wrongful termination was held not to be a penalty.
The judgment applies the reasoning from this employment case, which found a payment-in-lieu provision was not a penalty due to the employer's legitimate interest in performance.

candidates.case_name: P Y J v. N K S
judges: Coram : District Judge S. Lo in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: There is no fixed list of factors a court must consider when making schooling decisions for a child; each case is decided on its own facts, with the weight of each factor varying accordingly.
Greater consideration and weight should be given to the genuine, well-researched view of the child's primary carer regarding schooling, provided it is not motivated by malice.

relationships.description: The judgment cites A v B as an example where a child's cultural background and language foundation were considered relevant factors in choosing a local school.

candidates.case_name: LN, by his next friend A, LM v. LK, The Executor to the Estate of LC, Deceased
judges: Coram : Deputy District Judge Jacqueline Lee in chambers (Not open to public)

principles.principle_label: Objective standard for reasonable financial provision
principles.paraphrase_en: The determination of what constitutes reasonable financial provision under the Ordinance is an objective assessment of what the claimant should reasonably receive, not an assessment of whether the deceased acted reasonably.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for special expenses
principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant seeking specific expenses as part of reasonable financial provision must provide supporting evidence, such as medical reports or fee quotations; the court will not award substantial sums based solely on a party's unsupported assertions.

principles.principle_label: Relevance of deceased's lifestyle and expectations
principles.paraphrase_en: When assessing reasonable maintenance, the court may consider the deceased's lifestyle and the reasonable expectations for the claimant's standard of living that existed at the time of the deceased's death.

principles.principle_label: Best interests of a minor claimant
principles.paraphrase_en: Proposals for the use of maintenance funds for a minor must be shown to be in the minor's best interests, and the court will rely on evidence from neutral witnesses where available.

principles.principle_label: Costs from estate for reasonable defense
principles.paraphrase_en: An executor who reasonably defends a claim for financial provision, particularly where the claimant is found not credible, is entitled to have their costs paid from the deceased's estate on a solicitor and client basis.

relationships.target_case_name: Ilott v The Blue Cross and others
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Ilott that reasonable financial provision is determined by an objective standard for the claimant.

candidates.case_name: H&M HENNES & MAURITZ GBC AB v. ZOETOP BUSINESS CO LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Lok J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish joint liability as a joint tortfeasor, three criteria must be satisfied, as established by the leading authority on the subject.

relationships.target_case_name: Sea Shepherd UK v Fish & Fish Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment treats this UK Supreme Court case as the leading authority on the legal test for joint tortfeasorship.

candidates.case_name: CHINNEY SHUN CHEONG BUILDING SERVICES ENGINEERING LTD (formerly known as CHINNEY E & M (MAINTENANCE) LTD) v. LO KWAI FAN
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A court retains jurisdiction to issue further directions or vary an existing order for the sale of a property if there has been a material change in circumstances, particularly when the original order contains a liberty to apply clause.

principles.principle_label: Change of circumstances as grounds for variation
principles.paraphrase_en: A significant drop in the market value of a property constitutes a valid change of circumstances that can justify a court varying the minimum sale price specified in an earlier order.

relationships.target_case_name: Lam Chok Lai v The Official Receiver and Trustee in Bankruptcy of Lam Chok Wai
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the authority in Lam Chok Lai to affirm the court's jurisdiction to give further directions for a sale due to changed circumstances under a liberty to apply clause.

candidates.case_name: LI CHIU WAH JOSEPH v. HONG KONG SOCIETY OF NOTARIES
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Tang NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: To determine if a contractual provision is a penalty, the court must assess whether it is a secondary obligation imposing a detriment on the contract-breaker that is grossly disproportionate to the innocent party's legitimate interest in enforcing the primary obligation.

The inquiry into whether a sum represents a genuine pre-estimate of loss is subsumed within the broader question of whether the sum is so extravagant and disproportionate to the legitimate purpose it serves.

principles.principle_label: Restriction on new arguments on appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: A party cannot normally advance a new and different case in the Court of Final Appeal from that argued in the court below, absent a proper basis for doing so.

relationships.description: The Court of Appeal applied the principles from Law Ting Pong to conclude the late charge was not a penalty.
The judgment references the traditional test for penalties established in Dunlop.
The judgment references the Cavendish approach to penalty clauses, which was adopted in the Law Ting Pong case.

candidates.case_name: BRIGHT SHIPPING LTD v. CHANGHONG GROUP (HK) LTD
judges: Before: Hon Anthony Chan J in Chambers

relationships.target_case_name: Fortenova Grupa v LLC Shushary Holding
relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes Fortenova Grupa on the basis that the redemption in that case was indisputably unlawful, unlike the present situation.

relationships.target_case_name: Celestial Aviation Services Ltd v Unicredit Bank AG
relationships.description: The judgment cites Celestial Aviation Services as a related authority on the topic of payments involving sanctioned entities.

candidates.case_name: LAM SHUI KING v. POLLUTION & PROTECTION SERVICES LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Coram: Master Louise Chan in Chambers (Paper Disposal)

principles.paraphrase_en: The burden of proving that a plaintiff had constructive knowledge of a material fact rests on the party asserting it, and the test for such knowledge is objective.

The knowledge required to start a limitation period is a state of mind with a sufficient degree of certainty to justify taking preliminary steps towards a claim, such as seeking advice. It can include a reasonably firm belief that warrants investigation, not absolute certainty.
When assessing whether a plaintiff had the requisite knowledge, the court must consider the plaintiff's intelligence in understanding the information available to them.
