A plaintiff is expected to make reasonable investigations based on information available and cannot expect a defendant to provide every detail to absolve them of this duty.

While a defendant is always prejudiced by losing a limitation defence, the court may consider the relative strength of the plaintiff's case when assessing fairness, but cannot make a final determination on the merits at the limitation stage. The judge should take an overall view of the prospects of success.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Nash v Eli Lilly & Co regarding the nature of knowledge required to start the limitation period.

candidates.case_name: NLC v. YMF
judges: Before: Hon Yuen JA and B Chu J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court will not make a joint custody order if the parents are so antagonistic that they would be unable to agree on most matters, as such an order would be unworkable.
The party seeking to vary an existing custody order bears the burden of demonstrating why such a variation is necessary.
The court must take into account the views of a child, particularly as they grow older, when making any important decisions concerning the child's welfare.

A child's relationship with a parent's new family is more relevant to access arrangements than to decisions concerning custody, such as education. The time spent with a parent is governed by the access order, not the custody order.
An appellate court will not normally interfere with the findings of fact made by a first instance judge who has seen and heard the witnesses give live evidence.

relationships.description: The judgment cites the authority of PD in support of the proposition that a sole custody order does not prevent the other parent from providing input or challenging decisions.

candidates.case_name: CKFG v. WHHK
judges: Coram : Her Honour Judge Grace Chan in Chambers (Not Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Primary consideration in child welfare cases
principles.paraphrase_en: The sole and overriding legal principle in cases concerning children is the determination of what is in their best interests.

principles.principle_label: Status of Payne factors in relocation
principles.paraphrase_en: The factors outlined in Payne v Payne are not part of the applicable legal test or principles for deciding relocation applications; they may serve as a useful guide in some cases but only as part of a broader multi-factorial balancing exercise focused on welfare.

A judge must give real and meaningful effect to the expressed wishes of a child, especially when the child is mature, thoughtful, and able to express those wishes rationally and with emotional understanding. Failure to do so constitutes an error.
Little or no weight should be placed on a child's expressed wishes if, on the balance of probabilities, those wishes do not constitute an informed decision.

principles.principle_label: Weight on Social Welfare Officer reports
principles.paraphrase_en: Great weight should be adhered to the opinion and recommendation of a Social Welfare Officer when that officer has conducted an all-round and objective investigation which yields a logical conclusion.

relationships.description: The judgment clarifies that the Payne factors are not part of the applicable legal test but may be used as a non-binding guide in some circumstances.

candidates.case_name: PYK v. CCLA
judges: Before Coram: Deputy Judge R. Chow (Paper Disposal)

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may draw an adverse inference against a party who fails to provide full and frank financial disclosure, which can affect findings about their financial capacity.

candidates.case_name: MAKHLOUFI ALI v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Before: Hon Au and G Lam JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Judicial review scope in non-refoulement cases
principles.paraphrase_en: The Court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement decisions is limited; it will only intervene if there are errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality in the Board's decision, and does not serve as an additional appellate avenue.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of factual findings by Board
principles.paraphrase_en: Findings of fact made by the Torture Claims Appeal Board, including assessments of credibility and the severity of alleged harm, are to be respected if they are open to the Board based on the evidence and reasons provided.

principles.principle_label: Burden to demonstrate public law error
principles.paraphrase_en: An applicant challenging a non-refoulement decision must demonstrate a reasonably arguable public law error in the Board's decision to succeed in judicial review.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the legal principles established in Nupur Mst regarding the Court's approach to appeals in non-refoulement cases.
The judgment applies the principles from Re Md Shohel Sheak concerning the proper role of the Court in non-refoulement judicial reviews.
The Court follows the approach set out in Re Limbu Birkhaman for assessing appeals in non-refoulement matters.

candidates.case_name: CHAN YAU LING v. HOUSING APPEAL TRIBUNAL AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A decision-maker entrusted with discretion may adopt a guiding policy but must not apply it so rigidly as to fetter that discretion. The policy must allow for exceptions and consideration of the specific circumstances of each case.
A tribunal must give proper consideration to the particular circumstances of a case and not allow a general policy to automatically determine the outcome without such consideration.
Where a tribunal holds a hearing to consider matters beyond undisputed breaches, it implies the tribunal considered it had discretion in the matter, as a hearing would otherwise be redundant.

candidates.case_name: CLC v. OIE
judges: Coram: Her Honour Judge Thelma Kwan in Chambers (not open to public)

candidates.case_name: DO THI NGA v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement claims is limited to examining the decision for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality; it does not involve reassessing the factual merits of the claim or substituting its own view of the evidence.

The Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board are the primary decision-makers responsible for assessing evidence, making factual findings, and determining key issues such as risk of harm, state protection, and internal relocation viability in non-refoulement claims.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The present judgment cites Nupur Mst to support the proposition that judicial review intervention is limited to errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.

candidates.case_name: NG HON LAM EDGAR v. THE HONG KONG HOUSING AUTHORITY
judges: Before: Hon Chow JA (sitting as an additional judge of the Court of First Instance) in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish unlawful discrimination, a complainant must first show they were treated differently from a person in a comparable situation based on a prohibited ground, such as sexual orientation. Only after this is shown does the court consider if the differential treatment is justified.
principles.paragraph_span: [] (from the paragraph beginning 'Accordingly, the initial step...')
cited_statutes: Articles 22(1) and 1(1) of the Hong Kong Bill of Rights

principles.principle_label: Court's role in analogous situation analysis
principles.paraphrase_en: It is a question of law for the court, not a public authority, to decide whether two persons or groups are in a relevantly similar or analogous situation for a discrimination claim, considering the context of the differential treatment.
principles.paragraph_span: [] (2) (from the paragraph beginning 'The suggestion that due...')

principles.principle_label: Unified test for constitutionality and discrimination
principles.paraphrase_en: The same test is used to determine if differential treatment is unlawful as is used to determine if incursions into constitutionally protected rights are lawful. In the discrimination context, this is called the 'justification' test.
The first step in justifying differential treatment is to ask whether it pursues a legitimate aim.

While states enjoy a wide margin of appreciation in general measures of economic or social strategy, this does not justify adopting laws or practices that violate the prohibition of discrimination or fail the proportionality requirement.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the reasoning from QT regarding the analysis of analogous situations and the unified test for constitutionality and discrimination.
The court declines to defer judgment pending the appeal in the Infinger case, distinguishing the procedural posture and timing.

candidates.case_name: 阮旻僖 v. 中華基督教會基智中學
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to Public)

candidates.case_name: TOP GEAR INVESTMENT LTD AND OTHERS v. PACM GROUP (HOLDINGS) LTD
judges: Before: Hon Cheng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The court is bound to follow the established legal principle regarding what constitutes a penalty clause in a contract.

principles.principle_label: No obligation prior to definitive agreement
principles.paraphrase_en: Where parties are negotiating towards a loan, there is no binding obligation on either party to enter into the loan agreement until definitive loan documents are executed.

principles.principle_label: Implied term for best endeavours not established
principles.paraphrase_en: An argument for implying a term requiring parties to use their best endeavours to complete a transaction, based on business efficacy, was raised but not substantively developed.

An argument hinting at the application of the prevention principle—that a party should not deliberately prevent the occurrence of a condition precedent—was referenced but not fully developed in submissions.

relationships.target_case_name: Kensland Realty Ltd v Whale View Investment Ltd & another
relationships.description: The judgment notes that counsel's arguments hint at an application of the prevention principle established in this case.

candidates.case_name: WASHING BO LAUNDRY LTD v. NG KWOK WAI AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge C. W. Ling in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A contractual sum payable upon breach will be considered liquidated damages rather than a penalty if the amount is not extravagant, unconscionable, or out of all proportion to the legitimate interest protected by the clause.

The difficulty in quantifying loss of goodwill must be assessed in the specific commercial context; the impact of a store closure on goodwill differs substantially between a high-end shopping mall and a standalone franchised outlet.

Where a party abandons a claim at trial, the normal rule is that they should pay the costs associated with that claim, but the reduction may be limited if the issues overlap substantially with those that were argued.

The court may consider the manner in which a defence was conducted, and may criticize it as profligate and disproportionate if numerous arguments are advanced but later abandoned, wasting time on irrelevant issues.

relationships.target_case_name: The One Property Ltd v The Swatch Group (Hong Kong) Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment distinguishes this case on the basis that the commercial context and impact on goodwill from a store closure in a high-end mall differs from that of a standalone franchised laundry outlet.

candidates.case_name: WMW v. CYCH
judges: Coram: Deputy District Judge J. Chow in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The phrase 'enters university overseas' in a maintenance agreement is interpreted to mean the child's enrolment in an overseas university, not their physical attendance outside the jurisdiction.
The court may find the parties' submissions on how they negotiated the specific financial figures in a maintenance agreement to be irrelevant to the interpretation of the agreement's terms.

candidates.case_name: RAJESH NARAIN PARMAR v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

candidates.case_name: POON OI YIN FLORENCE v. WONG KWAN AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge James Kwan in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish adverse possession of land without legal title, a claimant must demonstrate both factual possession and the intention to possess the property throughout the relevant statutory period.

principles.principle_label: Limitation Ordinance application to equitable interests
principles.paraphrase_en: The Limitation Ordinance applies to equitable interests in land, including interests in proceeds of sale held on trust, in the same manner as it applies to legal estates, for the purpose of determining when a right of action to recover land accrues.

When land is held on trust and the limitation period for trustees to recover the land has expired, the trustees' estate is not extinguished as long as any beneficiary's right of action has not accrued or been barred; it extinguishes only when all such rights are barred.

principles.principle_label: Trustee's right to sue on behalf of beneficiary
principles.paraphrase_en: Trustees may bring an action to recover land on behalf of a beneficiary with a beneficial interest in possession, even if the trustees' own right of action would otherwise be time-barred, provided the beneficiary's right is not barred.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Powell v McFarlane that adverse possession requires both factual possession and intention to possess.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Tak Yue v Kung Kwok Wai & Another (No 2)
relationships.description: The judgment cites this authority in support of the requirements for establishing adverse possession.
The judgment applies reasoning from Ho Hang Wan regarding how a claimant's actions and assertions can establish adverse possession despite their subjective state of mind.

candidates.case_name: THE JOINT AND SEVERAL TRUSTEES OF THE PROPERTY OF THE BANKRUPT v. LEUNG YUET MEI
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Kent Yee in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: When determining whether an order for sale is beneficial to all co-owners, the court must make an objective assessment. The dissent or subjective assertion of opposing co-owners that it is not beneficial is not determinative.

Under the Partition Ordinance, the court will generally make an order for sale upon application by a co-owner unless satisfied that refusal would better serve all co-owners or that the order would cause very great hardship to one co-owner.

relationships.description: The judgment notes this English authority suggests difficulties from evicting a bankrupt's family are ordinarily insufficient to override creditors' interests in a sale.
The judgment references this English case alongside Re Citro for a similar proposition favoring creditors' interests in bankruptcy sales.
The judgment notes that Ng J in this Hong Kong case declined to adopt the approach from the English authorities Re Citro and Re Bremner.

relationships.target_case_name: Shanghai Commercial Bank Limited v Lee Yau Tak & Chan Wan Ying
relationships.description: The judgment notes the Court of Appeal acknowledged two conflicting lines of first instance authorities but did not resolve the divergence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG CHUN YAU (黃竣佑)
judges: Appeal Committee: Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ and Mr Justice Lam PJ

principles.paraphrase_en: The proper approach for a judge to direct a jury on an alternative verdict is established in Chau Yui Ming, and the earlier approach in Ho Hoi Shing must now be understood and applied in light of that case.
The ordinary meaning of 'supply' connotes making goods available from resources other than those of the recipient, so the return of deposited goods to their owner does not constitute a supply.
A depositor who is in unlawful possession of drugs has no enforceable right to demand their return from a temporary custodian.
A temporary custodian of unlawfully possessed drugs has a duty to hand those drugs over to the authorities.

relationships.description: The present judgment states that the proper approach for directing on alternative verdicts was set out in this case.
The judgment cites this case as recently holding that Ho Hoi Shing must be understood in light of Chau Yui Ming.
The judgment states that the approach in this earlier case must now be understood and applied in light of the later decision in Chau Yui Ming.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YEUNG CHUN PONG ANDREW
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Bruce SC in Chambers

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. HUI KA LOK, SAM
judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Statements made by one participant in a common enterprise are generally admissible against co-participants only if they relate to directions, instructions, arrangements, or utterances accompanying acts, rather than mere narrative accounts of past events.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG CHUN YAU
judges: Before: Hon Chu and Zervos JJA and M Poon J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: If two individuals are in joint possession of drugs for their own consumption, a subsequent transfer of the drugs between them does not constitute the act of trafficking.
Evidence that a co-accused has pleaded guilty to a related trafficking offence can be admissible and relevant to a defendant's defence that the drugs did not belong to him.

principles.principle_label: Purpose of possession as essential issue
principles.paraphrase_en: Once the jury determines an accused was in possession of drugs, the purpose of that possession becomes an essential, live issue that must be proven beyond reasonable doubt.

An alternative verdict of possession for own consumption should not be left to the jury where there is no evidence the drugs were for the accused's self-consumption and no suggestion from counsel that it should be considered.

relationships.target_case_name: R v Wong Wing Yui and Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the principle that transfer between joint possessors for own consumption does not constitute trafficking.
The judgment references this case in discussing when the purpose of possession becomes an essential issue for the jury.
This case is cited alongside Chong Wing Hoi regarding the jury's consideration of the purpose of drug possession.
The judgment applies the reasoning from this case that an alternative verdict is not required when there is no evidence of possession for self-consumption.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. ZHENG YUBING AND OTHERS
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for the offence of going equipped for stealing, courts must consider the specific facts of each case, including: the circumstances of apprehension, the nature of the equipment carried, the defendant's character and record, whether other offences were committed concurrently, and the totality principle.
Separate and distinct offences committed on different dates and at different places should, in principle, receive consecutive sentences upon conviction.
When determining a final aggregate sentence for multiple offences, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is just and proportionate.
The court must avoid double-counting an aggravating factor, such as coming to Hong Kong with the purpose of committing offences, when sentencing for multiple related offences.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the Court of Appeal's guidance in Li Ho Yin that there are no fixed sentencing guidelines for going equipped for stealing and that courts must consider the specific facts of each case.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. EXAVIER, FABIAN CARLO
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Edmond Lee in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A trial judge must actively analyze and assess the credibility and reliability of prosecution witnesses, considering specific defense criticisms such as viewing distance and lighting conditions, before accepting their observation evidence.

Flight from police, especially when not a direct reaction to police aggression and when coordinated with another suspect from whom stolen property is recovered, can support an inference of guilt and consciousness of wrongdoing rather than innocent reaction.

principles.principle_label: Standard of proof for rejecting defense
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may reject a defendant's exculpatory allegation as lacking substance if, upon consideration of all circumstances, it finds the allegation did not occur.
On appeal, re-arguing issues of fact already considered at trial does not constitute a valid ground of appeal unless the appellant can demonstrate an error in the trial judge's assessment.
Guilt in a joint enterprise charge can be established by sufficient evidence proving the defendant's participation beyond reasonable doubt.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. UMER-FAROOQ
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN WAI HANG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

relationships.description: The judgment references the findings in Nguyen Thang Loi regarding the THC concentration in cannabis products.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. OBI COSMAS NONSO AND ANOTHER
judges: the Honourable Madam Justice D’Almada Remedios

principles.principle_label: Mitigation for personal consumption in drug trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: When an accused pleads guilty to drug trafficking but claims a significant portion of the drugs was for personal use, the court will consider factors such as drug quantity, value, circumstances, addiction status, post-arrest explanation, and criminal record to determine the issue, often without requiring a Newton hearing.
A defendant can be convicted of drug trafficking if they trafficked in only part of the drugs found, rather than all of them.

principles.principle_label: Admission of partial trafficking leads to inevitable guilty verdict
principles.paraphrase_en: If defendants admit to trafficking in part of the drugs, it is inevitable that a jury would find them guilty of the trafficking charge.

principles.principle_label: Newton hearing for disputed personal consumption claims
principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant pleads guilty to trafficking but claims a significant proportion was for personal consumption, and the prosecution disputes the quantity, a Newton hearing should be held to determine the disputed quantity.

principles.principle_label: Procedure following guilty plea with disputed facts
principles.paraphrase_en: After a guilty plea to trafficking where facts are disputed (such as quantity for personal use), the court should hold a Newton hearing to resolve those specific issues, with the prosecution calling necessary witnesses.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN TUNG SHING
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J in Court

principles.principle_label: Joint enterprise inference from circumstantial evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may infer participation in a joint enterprise from circumstantial evidence, such as a defendant's presence at the scene, attire matching co-participants, and actions consistent with a common purpose, provided it is the only reasonable inference and excludes innocent explanations like merely passing by.

Evidence that a defendant fled from police is not, by itself, conclusive proof of guilt, as flight may have an innocent explanation. It must be considered independently and not used in circular reasoning to support other inferences of guilt.

principles.principle_label: Inference of intent from possession of an article
principles.paraphrase_en: An inference that a person possessed an article (such as a tool) with the intent to cause injury to others requires a sufficient factual basis and cannot be drawn merely from possession alone, especially in the absence of evidence of actual or threatened violence.

Legal rulings on doctrines such as joint enterprise are based on the specific facts of each case and cannot be applied as a general principle without considering the unique evidence and circumstances of the present case.

relationships.description: The present judgment cites this case for the principle that flight alone is not conclusive evidence of guilt.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LEUNG HO-HIN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: DHCJ Eric Kwok, SC

principles.paraphrase_en: A guilty plea based on joint enterprise to commit a robbery necessarily implies both an agreement to rob and the intent to commit robbery.
When determining sentence severity, the court may consider alternative characterizations of the offense equally serious if they involve comparable harm and culpability.
A plea of guilty can constitute the sole mitigating factor warranting a one-third reduction in sentence.

candidates.case_name: KHAN ASAF NAWAZ v. SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE on behalf of COMMISSIONER OF POLICE
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Ko in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To succeed in an action for malicious prosecution, the claimant must prove four elements: that the defendant initiated a criminal prosecution against them; that the prosecution ended in the claimant's favour; that the prosecution was without reasonable and probable cause; and that it was motivated by malice. The burden of proof for all elements rests on the claimant.

For liability under joint criminal enterprise, it is not a legal requirement that the accused be physically present at the scene of the crime. Liability can arise from an arrangement or understanding between parties, and presence merely strengthens the case for such liability.

principles.principle_label: Honest belief does not preclude malice
principles.paraphrase_en: An honest belief by a prosecutor in the guilt of the accused does not automatically negate the existence of malice for the purpose of malicious prosecution. Malice can be established if the prosecution case is knowingly tainted by serious illegality, such as an intention to pervert the course of justice.

The police may still be considered as prosecuting an offence for tort liability purposes even after they transfer the prosecution to an independent prosecutor. This is because they retain a duty to inform the independent prosecutor of any relevant matters that subsequently come to light.

relationships.description: The judgment cites McHugh J's reasoning from Osland to support the principle that presence at the scene is not invariably essential for joint enterprise liability.
The judgment cites the High Court of Australia's decision to reinforce the point that parties to an arrangement can be liable for each other's acts.

The judgment applies the CFA's decision, which upheld a murder conviction where the appellant was not present during the attack, as an example supporting the principle that presence is not a legal necessity.

relationships.target_case_name: Rees v Commissioner of Police for the Metropolis
relationships.description: The judgment follows the principle established in Rees that an honest belief in guilt does not negate malice if the case is knowingly presented in a form tainted by serious wrongdoing.

relationships.target_case_name: Clifford v Chief Constable of Hertfordshire Constabulary
relationships.description: The judgment cites Clifford, as referenced in Clerk & Lindsell, for the principle regarding the police's continuing prosecutorial role and duties after transferring a case.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. TSANG FUK WING
judges: Before: Hon Macrae VP in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge is entitled to reject a defendant's explanation as not credible based on the totality of evidence, including CCTV footage and the implausibility of the narrative when viewed in context.

The court may draw adverse inferences against a defendant from their conduct and the surrounding circumstances, such as following a person with others, which contradicts an innocent explanation like merely passing a message.

The court may find a defendant's claim of ignoring or being unconcerned with incriminating messages on their phone to be not credible, especially when the content is directly related to the alleged offense.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YEUNG HO YIN
judges: Before: High Court Judge Albert Wong in Court

principles.principle_label: Presumption of mens rea in statutory interpretation
principles.paraphrase_en: When interpreting a statutory offence, there is a presumption that mens rea (a guilty mind) is required for each element of the offence, unless legislative intent clearly displaces this presumption.

principles.principle_label: Test for displacing mens rea presumption
principles.paraphrase_en: To determine if the presumption of mens rea is displaced, the court must consider factors including the nature and seriousness of the offence, the penalty, social stigma, public protection needs, practicalities of prosecution, and whether the defendant had sole access to relevant information.

principles.principle_label: Objective test for 'reasonable concern' in loitering
principles.paraphrase_en: For the offence of loitering causing concern for safety or well-being, the concern must be reasonable, which involves both subjective and objective elements; it is not enough that a person unwittingly causes concern.

principles.principle_label: Certainty and proportionality in offence definition
principles.paraphrase_en: Legal provisions must be sufficiently specific and certain to allow the public to regulate their conduct and must adhere to the principle of proportionality, not exceeding what is necessary to achieve a legitimate aim.

principles.principle_label: Prosecution basis and joint enterprise notice
principles.paraphrase_en: If the prosecution's case is based on joint criminal enterprise, and the defence counsel addresses this point in closing submissions, it cannot later be argued that the defence was deprived of a chance to adduce evidence due to lack of notice.

relationships.description: The judgment questions the magistrate's reliance on Ng Chun Yip for defining 'well-being' as equivalent to a happy and contented state, criticizing it as overly broad and derived from an English civil case.
Cited by the defence to support the principle of statutory interpretation that offences are presumed not to be of absolute liability, requiring proof of mens rea.
Cited alongside Hin Lin Yee to support the established principle that legislative intent is presumed not to create absolute liability offences.
Applied to affirm that the mere fact an offence is regulatory does not automatically mean the legislature intended to dispense with the need to prove mens rea.
Distinguished as a civil case whose definition of 'well-being' was inappropriately applied to a criminal context in Ng Chun Yip.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. PANDI GURMEET SINGH
judges: Before: HH Judge Casewell

principles.paraphrase_en: The usual sentencing starting point for burglary of non-domestic premises, absent aggravating or mitigating features and for a defendant of clear record convicted after trial, is 2.5 years (30 months) imprisonment.

The conventional sentencing starting point for burglary can be lowered in cases of opportunistic offending, akin to a sneak thief who takes advantage of an open entry point without significant premeditation or planning.
The normal starting point for burglary already incorporates consideration of a degree of premeditation and planning; a lack of such planning is a mitigating factor that can reduce the sentence.
A previous conviction that is minor and unrelated to the current offence may be disregarded for sentencing purposes.
An offence may possess elements of different categories of crime (e.g., pickpocketing and burglary), and its hybrid nature is a relevant consideration in sentencing.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the proposition that the sentencing starting point can be lowered for opportunistic burglars.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG WAI FUNG, TIMOTHY AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Campbell-Moffat J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A robbery executed by a coordinated gang, even if composed of distinct groups, is a serious offense that warrants severe punishment, particularly when targeting vulnerable premises for high reward and involving callous disregard for victims.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for serious robbery
principles.paraphrase_en: A starting point of 10 years' imprisonment after trial is appropriate for a serious, pre-planned robbery involving violence against a victim.
A defendant is entitled to a one-third reduction in sentence for a timely plea of guilty.
A defendant's claim of being a mere bystander or low-level participant can be rejected based on evidence from co-defendants showing deeper involvement in the planning and execution of the crime.
Previous good character carries limited mitigating weight in sentencing for a very serious offense, especially where no explanation for the offending is provided.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WONG HO LEONG
judges: Before: HH Judge K Lo

principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits burglary when they enter a building as a trespasser with intent to steal, or having entered as a trespasser, they steal or attempt to steal.
Entering a building without permission when it is closed to the public constitutes trespassory entry for the purpose of burglary.
A defendant's admission made under caution, when properly recorded, can serve as evidence supporting a burglary conviction.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YU YIHAN ALSO KNOWN AS YU YEEHON AND OTHERS
judges: Before: HH Judge Kathie Cheung

principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple offenses, the court must consider the overall criminality and ensure the total sentence is just and proportionate, not excessive.
A court may order sentences for separate offenses to run concurrently if justified by the totality principle and the specific circumstances of the defendant.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. DELA ROSA ARNULFO SIBLANG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Casewell

principles.paraphrase_en: The court must follow a three-step sentencing process: first, identify any applicable sentencing guideline; second, consider enhancement for aggravating factors to establish a starting point; third, apply reduction for mitigating factors.
A defendant who pleads guilty and admits the offense is entitled to a full one-third discount from the sentencing starting point determined by the court.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. BUDI YANTO AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: H.H. Judge G. Lam

principles.paraphrase_en: When multiple individuals participate in a burglary with a common purpose, such as one entering the premises and another acting as a lookout, all participants can be held liable for the offense as joint principals.

Video recorded interviews containing admissions by defendants, including those conducted with sign language interpreters for non-verbal defendants, are admissible as evidence of their involvement in the alleged offense.
Physical evidence, such as fingerprints found at the crime scene, can corroborate admissions made by a defendant and support the prosecution's case.

candidates.case_name: MASON GROUP HOLDINGS LTD formerly known as MASON FINANCIAL HOLDINGS LTD AND ANOTHER v. TAM JOSEPH WING ON AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge KC Chan in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The established economic tort of lawful means conspiracy is carefully defined to avoid imposing liability on legitimate business activities. Liability requires the deliberate infliction of injury on the plaintiff in the absence of a just cause or legitimate interest, as distinct from lawfully advancing one's own interests where damage to another is merely a foreseeable consequence.

For a conspiracy, it is not necessary that every conspirator performs every overt act, that all join at the same time, or that the origins of the agreement are precisely known. The essential requirements are that the overt acts are done pursuant to the conspiracy and that the parties, at the time of the acts complained of, were sufficiently aware of the circumstances and shared the same object, thereby acting in concert.

principles.principle_label: Inference of overt acts in furtherance
principles.paraphrase_en: Whether pleaded overt acts were done in furtherance of a conspiratorial agreement can be a matter of inference drawn from the pleaded facts. A court may refuse to strike out a claim if it is not impossible to draw such an inference from those facts.

In assessing the viability of a pleaded conspiracy, the court may be asked to consider the timing and connections between various events and transactions, take a holistic view, and draw threads together to see if an inference of a conspiratorial agreement is possible.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that economic torts like lawful means conspiracy are narrowly defined to avoid penalizing legitimate business activities.

relationships.target_case_name: Metro Holdings Ltd v Periwin Development Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that whether overt acts were in furtherance of a conspiracy can be a matter of inference from pleaded facts.

candidates.case_name: WONG KEAVEN KA HAY t/a HOI SHING SEAFOOD CO. v. CENTRAL INTERNATIONAL (HK) LTD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Eugene Yim in Chambers (Open to Public)

candidates.case_name: CHAN WAI KEUNG LAWRENCE v. AU CHI MAN ALBERT AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The established economic torts are narrowly defined to avoid interfering with legitimate business activities or imposing liability beyond what is justified in principle.

For lawful means conspiracy, a distinction exists between deliberately injuring the plaintiff without just cause or legitimate interest, and lawfully advancing one's own interests where damage to another is merely a foreseeable consequence.

Conspiracy agreements are typically concealed; it is not necessary to prove when or where the initial agreement was made, that every conspirator performed every overt act, or that all conspirators joined at the same time.
For parties to be acting in concert in a conspiracy, they must be sufficiently aware of the surrounding circumstances and share the same object at the time of the acts complained of.

principles.principle_label: No requirement for price to cover mortgage
principles.paraphrase_en: There is no legal principle or conveyancing practice requiring the purchase price of a property to be sufficient to discharge an existing mortgage; a vendor may sell at a loss and cover any shortfall personally.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that economic torts are narrowly defined to avoid trespassing on legitimate business activities.

candidates.case_name: TAIHEI DENGYO KAISHA, LTD v. BOK JOENG HONG KONG CO LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Master Keith Lam in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A default judgment creates an estoppel only for issues that were necessarily and with complete precision decided by that judgment. The scope of what is conclusively determined must be scrutinized with extreme particularity.

A default judgment establishing liability does not automatically determine the quantum of damages or equitable compensation. The assessment of loss remains a separate exercise where the defendant can contest the extent of their liability.

principles.principle_label: Liability of non-direct recipients in fraud
principles.paraphrase_en: A non-direct recipient (not the first-layer recipient) of fraudulently obtained funds may, in principle, be held liable for the entire defrauded sum, not merely the amount they personally received.

principles.principle_label: Distinguishing liability based on recipient role
principles.paraphrase_en: The inference that a recipient was a party to a fraud is more readily drawn against a direct recipient of the majority of the defrauded sum, compared to a more remote recipient.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Ozer Properties that a default judgment for damages to be assessed does not necessarily determine specific underlying issues like tenancy status.

relationships.target_case_name: Natures Mark HK Ltd v Hongkong Honxintai Trading Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as an example where a non-direct recipient was held liable for the entire defrauded sum in an email fraud case.

relationships.target_case_name: 800 Columbia Project Company LLC v Xiang Yang Logistics Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as precedent allowing for damages against a defendant for the entire loss in a fraud, not limited to the sum they received.
The judgment indicates it follows the principles of law on dishonest assistance as set out by Mance J in Grupo Torras.

candidates.case_name: KOO MING KOWN v. THE BAPTIST CONVENTION OF HONG KONG AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge MK Liu in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: An appeal from a Master's decision is conducted as a fresh hearing, where the judge considers the applications anew as if they were brought for the first time.

principles.principle_label: Test for joinder of new party
principles.paraphrase_en: The test for joining a new party under Order 15 rule 6 is whether there exists a genuine claim and a proper question to be tried between the plaintiff and the intended defendant, which is necessary or just and convenient to resolve. While the applicant need not show the merits of their case, joinder will be refused if the opposing party demonstrates it is plain and obvious the plaintiff has no case against them.

Factual findings from a judgment in earlier proceedings are inadmissible as evidence in subsequent proceedings, unless the party against whom they are sought to be used is bound by them through issue estoppel or res judicata. The rationale is that each court must form its own opinion based on the evidence before it.

The Hollington principle does not render inadmissible the underlying evidence referred to in an earlier judgment, such as the contents of documents or witness testimony. Statements of the factual evidence presented to the previous court are not excluded.

For a claim in misrepresentation, the plaintiff must have acted on the representation in a manner consistent with the inducement intended. A plea that the plaintiff acted on a representation is inherently contradictory if the alleged action was to investigate and challenge the truth of that same representation.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the established Hollington principle that findings from prior judgments are inadmissible in later proceedings unless binding through estoppel.

principles.principle_label: Inference from failure to call witness
principles.paraphrase_en: An irresistible inference can be drawn against a party who, being in the best position to do so, fails to call a witness who could contradict the opposing party's evidence, leading to a finding that the opposing party's version of events is true.

principles.principle_label: Representation of belief or intention as fact
principles.paraphrase_en: A representation concerning a person's present belief or intention can constitute a statement of fact. If the representor did not actually hold that belief or intention at the time, the statement is false.
The elements of the tort of conspiracy are narrowly defined to avoid penalizing legitimate business activities and to prevent imposing liability beyond what is justified in principle.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority that a representation of belief or intention can be a statement of fact.

principles.paraphrase_en: For a serious allegation like unlawful means conspiracy, the court must find the inference of concerted action to be compelling and plainly established based on the primary facts. It requires sufficient awareness of surrounding circumstances and a shared objective to injure.

A party may, for practical reasons, elect not to pursue a specific head of relief at trial even if it is, in principle, entitled to it. This abandonment limits the scope of the final judgment and the remedies ordered.

principles.paraphrate_en: A claim is not reasonably brought if it is unmeritorious and devoid of sufficient evidential basis from the outset, particularly where the primary factual case is deficient and cannot meet the required legal standard of proof.

principles.paraphrase_en: It is an abuse of the court's process for a plaintiff to attempt to re-litigate a claim that has already been dismissed in a previous action.

principles.principle_label: Striking out frivolous or vexatious claims
principles.paraphrase_en: A statement of claim may be struck out if it discloses no reasonable cause of action, or if it is scandalous, frivolous, or vexatious.
Claims arising from the termination of employment should properly be dealt with by the Labour Tribunal, not the Court of First Instance.

candidates.case_name: DINGWAY INVESTMENT LTD (Provisional Liquidators Appointed) v. CHINA CITY CONSTRUCTION & DEVELOPMENT CO.,(HONG KONG) LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP and Barma JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Striking out standard for defective pleadings
principles.paraphrase_en: A pleading will not be struck out merely for deficiencies if those deficiencies can be cured by requesting further and better particulars, especially when trial is not imminent. The court may allow the pleading to stand and require particulars rather than striking it out at an early stage.

While allegations of serious wrongdoing like conspiracy or dishonest assistance require proper pleading, the court has discretion to determine whether the level of precision is sufficient. Deficiencies do not automatically warrant striking out if the core allegation is discernible and curable.

Liability for dishonest assistance requires a dishonest state of mind, which can consist of either knowledge that one cannot honestly participate in the transaction (such as misappropriation) or suspicion combined with a conscious decision not to make inquiries.
The court may reject a defense it finds to be artificial or unrealistic, particularly when it requires drawing implausible inferences from incomplete information.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for justification defence
principles.paraphrase_en: In a defamation case, the defendant bears the legal burden of proving that the defamatory statement's core meaning (the 'sting') is substantially true.
A defence of justification must be properly raised in the pleadings; arguments about whether a finding constitutes a variation of pleaded facts or a new, separate issue may be relevant.
To establish a conspiracy to injure, the plaintiff must prove there was an agreement or understanding between the defendants with the predominant purpose of injuring the plaintiff.
A reporter engaging in investigative journalism to expose alleged malpractices, without an agreement to injure, does not constitute a conspiracy.
Where a claim fails on liability, it is not necessary for the judge to adjudicate on the quantum of damages.

principles.paraphrase_en: A company director has a personal obligation to both ascertain precisely what actions the company must take to comply with a court order and to ensure those actions are properly carried out within the specified timeframe.

principles.principle_label: Knowledge inference from dispensation of service
principles.paraphrase_en: When the court uses its discretion to dispense with service of an order, and the director provides no evidence of being unaware of it, the court may infer that the director knew the order's terms before the compliance deadline and understood the plaintiff required them to use their directorial powers to secure the company's compliance.
A lower sentence of imprisonment is appropriate for contempt committed solely in the capacity of a company director, compared to contempt committed both personally and as a director.

principles.paraphrase_en: Valid service of a statutory demand is a mandatory requirement before bankruptcy proceedings can be commenced under section 6A of the Bankruptcy Ordinance. This ensures the debtor has actual notice of the demand and a fair opportunity to respond or object.

principles.principle_label: Standard for bringing demand to debtor's attention
principles.paraphrase_en: A creditor must take all reasonable steps to bring a statutory demand to the debtor's attention. Personal service is required when practicable. Alternative methods are only permissible after personal service has failed and reasonable enquiries have been exhausted.
Whether a creditor's steps to serve a statutory demand are reasonable is judged objectively, based on the information actually available or constructively known to the creditor at the relevant time.

relationships.description: The judgment adopts and applies the principle established in Re Pang Mei Lan May regarding the creditor's duty to take all reasonable steps to bring a statutory demand to the debtor's attention.

principles.principle_label: Late appeal filing procedure under amended Immigration Ordinance
principles.paraphrase_en: Following the amendment to the Immigration Ordinance, the Torture Claims Appeal Board is no longer required to consider the substantive merits of a case when determining a late appeal filing. The Board's consideration is limited to the reasons stated in the late filing application and any accompanying documentary evidence.

principles.principle_label: Scope of judicial review for Board decisions on late appeals
principles.paraphrase_en: A challenge to the Board's decision on a late appeal filing requires demonstrating an error of law or procedural unfairness. The absence of such error or unfairness means there is no reasonably arguable basis for judicial review.

principles.principle_label: Board's discretionary analysis of merits post-amendment
principles.paraphrase_en: Even when the Board is not statutorily required to analyze the substantive merits of a claim under the amended Immigration Ordinance, it may still do so. If the Board conducts such an analysis and finds no evidence sufficient to overturn the underlying decision, this reinforces the conclusion that there is no arguable challenge.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing starting point for drug trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: The initial sentencing benchmark for trafficking in dangerous drugs is determined primarily by the quantity of drugs involved in the offense.

principles.principle_label: Upper tariff limit for large-scale trafficking
principles.paraphrase_en: For trafficking in large quantities of dangerous drugs, the sentencing guidelines established by the Court of Appeal set a maximum starting point of 20 years' imprisonment.
A defendant's attempt to assist law enforcement authorities may be considered as a potential mitigating factor in sentencing, even if the information provided is ultimately assessed as not useful.

principles.paraphrase_en: The existence of a dispute necessary to attract 'without prejudice' privilege is determined objectively, based on whether a reasonable observer would conclude there was a reasonably concrete and definable issue between the parties. The subjective intentions of the parties are irrelevant.

The 'without prejudice' privilege applies to communications that are genuine attempts to compromise a dispute over the existence or extent of a liability. It does not apply to discussions merely about how an admitted liability is to be paid, as there is no dispute in such a case.

Marking a communication 'without prejudice' is not conclusive. The court must examine the substance of the communication to determine if it is a genuine negotiation document or merely an assertion of rights.

principles.principle_label: Privilege and absence of extant dispute
principles.paraphrase_en: Communications marked 'without prejudice' may fall outside the privilege if, viewed objectively, there was no extant dispute between the parties at the time of the communication, even if a dispute later emerged.
Whether a dispute exists for the purpose of 'without prejudice' privilege is an exercise that requires taking into account all relevant circumstances surrounding the communication.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the proposition that both parties must have realised or should have realised they were seeking to compromise a dispute.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that not all discussions aimed at reaching an accommodation are privileged.
The judgment applies the distinction drawn in this case between resolving a dispute over liability and discussing payment of an admitted liability.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that earlier 'without prejudice' communications may lack privilege if there was objectively no dispute at the time.

relationships.target_case_name: Poben Consultants Ltd v Clearwater Bay Golf & Country Club

principles.paraphrase_en: The legality of prolonged immigration detention pending removal must be assessed according to established principles, including whether detention remains necessary and removal is realistically achievable within a reasonable timeframe.

relationships.target_case_name: Harjang Singh v Secretary for Security

To successfully oppose a bankruptcy petition, a debtor must demonstrate a bona fide dispute to the debt on substantial grounds.

A single previous case, decided on its own specific facts, does not establish a binding principle or tariff for sentencing; the proper approach is to assess each case individually based on its circumstances.

principles.paraphrase_en: The without prejudice privilege only applies to communications made during genuine attempts to settle an existing dispute, where the parties intend that the communications remain confidential if negotiations fail. principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple drug offenses, the court should apply a combined approach using three tests: the absurdity test, the conversion test, and the ratio test.

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement claims is not to reassess the claim anew but to scrutinize the Board's decision for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality, as the primary fact-finding responsibility lies with the Director and the Board.

The court may determine an application on paper without an oral hearing after considering the nature of the application, the issues raised, the decision under review, the papers, and all the circumstances of the case.

Where the Board is conscious of potential interpretation risks arising from not sharing the applicant's first language and takes care to avoid mistakes, and where concerns are clearly raised and responded to during the hearing, the court may find no procedural unfairness absent evidence that anything went amiss.

principles.paraphrase_en: An administrative board's decision must be comprehensive and supported by adequate reasoning to satisfy legal requirements.

principles.principle_label: Relevance of evidence to pleaded case
principles.paraphrase_en: Evidence sought must be directly relevant to the issues in the action as pleaded against the specific defendant. Evidence relating to matters outside the scope of the defendant's responsibility is irrelevant and inadmissible.

A court will not permit a party to conduct tests or investigations aimed at discovering a case or cause of action not already pleaded. Using a test to explore potential sources of liability outside the existing claim constitutes an impermissible fishing expedition.

principles.principle_label: Scope of action against incorporated owners
principles.paraphrase_en: An action against the incorporated owners of a building is limited to matters concerning the common parts for which they are responsible. Claims or evidence regarding individual flats or units for which the incorporated owners bear no responsibility fall outside the scope of such an action.

judges: Before: HH Judge Casewell

candidates.case_name: WALLACE SHAUN MARK v. DEPARTMENT OF JUSTICE

principles.paraphrase_en: The lawfulness of immigration detention depends on whether its duration remains reasonable when assessed against all relevant circumstances, not merely on the length of time alone.
A court must conduct its own independent assessment of the facts and circumstances to determine whether continued immigration detention has become unreasonable and therefore unlawful.

relationships.description: The present judgment agrees with and follows the reasoning of Coleman J that the applicant's detention up to a specific date was reasonable and lawful.

candidates.case_name: 莊裕安 v. 特首李家超 AND ANOTHER

principles.principle_label: Time limit for leave to appeal to CFA
principles.paraphrase_en: A notice of motion for leave to appeal to the Court of Final Appeal must be filed within 28 days from the date of the judgment being appealed, as stipulated by statute.

principles.principle_label: Requirement to identify appeal question for CFA leave
principles.paraphrase_en: An applicant for leave to appeal to the Court of Final Appeal must identify a question involved in the intended appeal. This is a fundamental statutory requirement for granting leave.

principles.principle_label: Nature of question for CFA leave
principles.paraphrase_en: For leave to appeal to the Court of Final Appeal to be granted, the question identified must be one of great general or public importance.

principles.principle_label: Failure to explain delay in application
principles.paraphrase_en: A failure to provide a proper explanation for a delay in filing an application can be a ground for refusing the application. Lack of legal aid is not necessarily a relevant or acceptable excuse, particularly for an experienced litigant in person.

candidates.case_name: MICHAEL YAP AND ANOTHER v. LAI YIP DYEING FACTORY LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Kenneth Wong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When a solicitor cannot disclose privileged communications with their client due to lack of waiver, the court must make full allowance for this disadvantage in any application against the solicitor, giving them the benefit of any doubt.

principles.principle_label: Test for leave in derivative actions
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's function when considering leave to commence a derivative action under the Companies Ordinance is to determine whether there is a serious question to be tried, not to make findings of fact or conclude the claim has no prospect of success without a full trial.

principles.principle_label: Legal representative liability for doomed claims
principles.paraphrase_en: A legal representative does not act improperly, unreasonably, or negligently merely by acting for a party whose claim or defence is ultimately doomed to fail, provided they have not knowingly assisted in an abuse of process.

No adverse inference should be drawn against a solicitor from the content of legal advice given to their client when that advice remains protected by legal professional privilege and has not been waived.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Ridehalgh that a legal representative is not liable simply for acting in a case that fails.
The judgment cites Lord Pearce's observation from Rondel v. Worsley in support of the principle that lawyers are not liable merely for representing clients with weak cases.

candidates.case_name: PARAMJIT SINGH v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Before: Hon Au and Chow JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A non-refoulement claim cannot be based on a fear that the applicant has subjectively created or imagined without objective, reasonable grounds, even if the applicant genuinely feels afraid.
To establish a valid non-refoulement claim, an applicant must demonstrate a genuine and objectively reasonable belief that they would face a substantial risk to their life if returned.

The principle of non-refoulement, which prohibits returning a person to a place where they would face persecution, is derived from Article 33 of the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees.

candidates.case_name: ABOGADO EDNA BARRIENTOS v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge Bruno Chan :

principles.paraphrase_en: A party challenging an administrative decision must clearly and properly identify specific errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality in that decision to establish a reasonably arguable basis for challenge.

principles.principle_label: Standard of review for credibility findings
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate body's adverse credibility finding, made after a thorough analysis of oral evidence and with the benefit of hearing the witness, is difficult to challenge absent a clear error of law or procedural unfairness.
The court must subject decisions on torture and non-refoulement claims to rigorous examination and anxious scrutiny.
Bare assertions made in legal proceedings, such as claims of health issues affecting testimony, require corroborating evidence to be accepted, especially when not raised at the original hearing.

candidates.case_name: KHAN MOHIB ALI v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Before: Hon Chow JA and H. Au-Yeung J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court must apply the principle of irrationality when reviewing the decisions of administrative bodies such as the Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board.

principles.principle_label: Procedural fairness duty to give reasons
principles.paraphrase_en: The principle of procedural fairness requires administrative bodies like the Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board to provide reasons justifying their decisions.

The assessment of risk of persecution in non-refoulement claims must be conducted with reference to the non-refoulement principle established in Article 33 of the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees.

candidates.case_name: ALI KHAN SAJID alias SAJID ALI KHAN alias RASHID ALI v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE

principles.principle_label: Time limit for judicial review leave applications
principles.paraphrase_en: An application for leave to apply for judicial review must be made within three months from the date of the decision being challenged, as stipulated by the High Court Ordinance and Rules.
cited_statutes: Rules of the High Court O.53 r.4(1)

principles.principle_label: Court's role in non-refoulement judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: In judicial review of non-refoulement claims, the court does not reassess the claim or evidence afresh; its role is limited to scrutinizing the Board's decision for errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.

The Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board are the primary decision-makers responsible for assessing evidence, making factual findings, and determining risk of harm, state protection, and internal relocation in non-refoulement claims.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the principle that judicial review intervention is limited to errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality in non-refoulement decisions.

candidates.case_name: REYES MILAGROS LAGUA v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.principle_label: Judicial review scope for non-refoulement claims
principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement decisions is limited to examining the decision for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality; it does not involve reassessing the factual merits of the claim or substituting its own view of the evidence.

The primary responsibility for assessing non-refoulement claims, including evaluating evidence, making factual findings on risk, state protection, and internal relocation, lies with the Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board, not the court.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the proposition that judicial review intervention is limited to errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.

candidates.case_name: MALIK MUHAMMAD SARFRAZ v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement decisions is not to reassess the claim anew. The court will only intervene for errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality, as the primary assessment of evidence and facts rests with the Director and the Board.

A decision-maker must not rely on speculative arguments or their own theories about how a claimant or others should have acted. The assessment must focus on what is objectively or reasonably believable based on reliable sources.

While the claimant bears the burden of proving a well-founded fear, if no adverse credibility finding is made, the Board must assess the claim on all applicable grounds assuming the case is plausible. However, this guideline is not a rigid rule of law and depends on the circumstances.

When considering whether a claimant will face a flagrant denial of justice, the decision-maker must assess whether systemic failures in the destination country's criminal justice system are so severe as to nullify the right to a fair trial.

When assessing documentary evidence from a country where fraudulent documents are readily available, a decision-maker may place less weight on documents that exhibit internal inconsistencies or features incongruous with their purported origin.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the principle that judicial review of non-refoulement decisions is limited to errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.
The Board applied the reasoning from this Court of Appeal decision when assessing whether delays in Pakistan's criminal justice system amount to a flagrant denial of a fair trial.
The judgment follows the Court of Appeal's approach in Hidayah regarding the assessment of a claimant's case as plausible if no adverse credibility finding is made.

candidates.case_name: PANAGODA LIYANAGE SURANGA DULIP PERERA v. DIRECTOR OF IMMIGRATION
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.paraphrase_en: For a subsequent non-refoulement claim to be accepted, the applicant must prove both that there has been a significant change in circumstances since the previous claim was finally determined, and that this change, when considered alongside previously submitted material, gives the new claim a realistic prospect of success.
The fact that an applicant has established a family in Hong Kong is not a relevant factor for consideration in determining the merits of a non-refoulement claim.
Matters that have been finally determined as unsubstantiated in a previous non-refoulement claim cannot be re-litigated in a subsequent claim.

principles.principle_label: Standard of review for subsequent claims
principles.paraphrase_en: The court, in reviewing a decision on a subsequent non-refoulement claim, must rigorously examine the decision with anxious scrutiny to ensure the law was correctly stated, issues correctly identified, evidence properly analysed, and a high standard of fairness observed.

candidates.case_name: IQBAL HASSAN v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge (Non-refoulement Claims) Teresa Wu :

principles.paraphrase_en: A decision-making body must demonstrate that it has given substantive consideration to the core elements of an applicant's case in its reasoning.

principles.principle_label: Standard for well-founded fear of persecution
principles.paraphrase_en: For a claim of persecution risk to succeed, the applicant must establish a well-founded fear of persecution for a reason protected under the law, assessed as a real chance or real risk of suffering harm amounting to persecution.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of credibility and documentary evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A tribunal may find a claim not substantiated if it concludes the claim is fabricated, based on an assessment that provided documents are wholly fabricated, materially altered, or do not support the applicant's account.

candidates.case_name: FERNANDEZ MARICEL BOLOAVIO v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE AND ANOTHER
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement decisions is not to reassess the claim de novo or make fresh factual findings, but to scrutinize the decision for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.

The Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board are the primary decision-makers responsible for assessing evidence, making factual findings, and determining risks, state protection, and internal relocation viability in non-refoulement claims.
The applicant bears the burden of proving their claims for non-refoulement protection, and the Board must assess whether this burden has been discharged based on the applicable legal principles.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the limited grounds on which a court will intervene in a non-refoulement decision during judicial review.

principles.principle_label: Extension of time factors for judicial review
principles.paraphrase_en: When considering an application to extend time for filing a judicial review application, the court must evaluate four factors: the length of the delay, the reasons for the delay, the merits of the intended application, and any prejudice to the respondent if the extension is granted.

The judgment references the Court of Appeal's decision in Re: Kartini as relevant authority.

candidates.case_name: MUZAFFAR BILAL v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge Michael WONG :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement decisions is limited to examining legal errors, procedural fairness, or irrationality; it does not involve reassessing the factual merits of the claim or substituting its own view of the evidence.

The obligation to consider internal relocation as an alternative only arises if the applicant has first established refugee status under the Refugee Convention by demonstrating a real risk of persecution in their home area.
Leave to apply for judicial review should only be granted if the claim is reasonably arguable and has realistic prospects of success.

relationships.target_case_name: Nupur Mst v Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the limited grounds for judicial review intervention in non-refoulement decisions.
The judgment cites this English case to establish that internal relocation is only considered after refugee status is established.
The judgment cites this case to reinforce that internal relocation analysis requires a prior finding of real risk of persecution in the home area.

candidates.case_name: MIA SHAHIN v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD
judges: Order by Hon Fung J :

principles.paraphrase_en: An applicant for judicial review must clearly and properly identify specific grounds for relief, such as errors of law, irrationality, or procedural unfairness, in their application documents; failure to do so may result in dismissal of the application.

principles.principle_label: Standard of review for torture claims
principles.paraphrase_en: Courts must examine decisions of immigration authorities on torture and non-refoulement claims with rigorous examination and anxious scrutiny to ensure proper application of legal standards.

The Director of Immigration must assess torture claims under multiple legal frameworks including risk of torture under immigration law, risks to absolute rights under the Bill of Rights (particularly right to life and freedom from torture), and persecution risks engaging non-refoulement principles.

candidates.case_name: USMAN JAVED v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement claims is not to reassess the merits of the claim or make fresh factual findings. The court examines the decision of the primary decision-makers for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality, but does not substitute its own assessment of evidence or risk.

The Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board are the primary bodies responsible for assessing non-refoulement claims. Their exclusive functions include evaluating evidence, making factual findings on risk of harm, state protection, and internal relocation viability.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the limited grounds on which a court may intervene in a non-refoulement decision during judicial review.

candidates.case_name: PARAMJEET KAUR AND ANOTHER v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's function in judicial review of non-refoulement claims is not to conduct a fresh assessment of the claim but to scrutinize the decision of the primary decision-makers (the Director and the Board/Petition Office) for errors of law or procedural unfairness.

The Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board/Non-Refoulement Claims Petition Office are the primary bodies responsible for assessing evidence, making factual findings, and determining risks related to non-refoulement claims, including risk of harm, state protection, and internal relocation viability.

Decisions of the Board in non-refoulement matters must satisfy the enhanced Wednesbury test of reasonableness, meaning they must not be so unreasonable that no reasonable decision-maker could have reached them, with particular scrutiny given to the context of fundamental rights.

principles.principle_label: Leave to apply for judicial review standard
principles.paraphrase_en: Leave to apply for judicial review will be refused if the applicant fails to demonstrate a reasonably arguable case with a realistic prospect of success, showing either an error of law or procedural unfairness in the Board's decision.

candidates.case_name: NMW v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung :

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof in asylum claims
principles.paraphrase_en: In non-refoulement claims, the burden of proof rests on the applicant to provide evidence supporting their case. The decision-making authority assesses this evidence according to high standards of fairness but is not required to produce evidence to challenge the applicant's factual allegations.

A decision-maker must base credibility assessments on objective evidence and avoid substituting personal assumptions, conjecture, speculation, or subjective views for such evidence, while still being entitled to use common sense in evaluating the facts.
The Court in judicial review proceedings does not assume an inquisitorial duty; it is the applicant's responsibility to raise the specific grounds for review before the court.
An adjudicator who has heard the evidence and considered submissions is entitled to reach conclusions on the facts, including interpreting the meaning of an applicant's statements.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of evidence in the round
principles.paraphrase_en: A decision-maker must consider all relevant evidence collectively when assessing a claim, including the timing and context in which certain allegations are raised.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle from Soni Pratik that the court has no inquisitorial duty in judicial review and the applicant must raise the grounds.
The judgment cites Re: Kartini as the source for the principle regarding the court's lack of inquisitorial duty.

relationships.target_case_name: TK v Michael C Jenkins Esq and Director of Immigration
relationships.description: The judgment cites TK v Jenkins for principles concerning the assessment of evidence and burden of proof in asylum cases.

relationships.target_case_name: KM & Ors v Torture Claims Appeal Board

principles.paraphrase_en: An applicant for judicial review must clearly articulate proper grounds for challenging a decision, such as errors of law, irrationality, or procedural unfairness; merely repeating the original claim without identifying such grounds is insufficient.

principles.principle_label: Standard of review for non-refoulement claims
principles.paraphrase_en: The court must examine decisions on non-refoulement claims (including torture risk and Bill of Rights risks) with rigorous examination and anxious scrutiny.

A non-refoulement claim can be rejected on multiple applicable grounds including risk of torture, risk to absolute rights under the Hong Kong Bill of Rights (such as right to life), and risk of persecution under the non-refoulement principle.

principles.paraphrase_en: A very substantial delay in applying for judicial review requires a satisfactory explanation from the applicant for the entire period of delay.

principles.paraphrase_en: In judicial review of non-refoulement claims, the court does not reassess the claim afresh or make primary factual findings; its role is limited to scrutinizing the decision for legal error, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.
