relationships.target_case_name: Flywin Co Ltd v Strong & Associates Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Flywin doctrine as a discretionary principle restricting new points on appeal based on fairness.
The judgment cites this case to support that the Flywin principle is of general application beyond civil trials.

relationships.target_case_name: Nice & Well Ltd v Fu Mee Yuk Shirley
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority that points not pleaded below are generally not open to an appellant on appeal.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheng Loon Yin v Secretary for Justice & anor
relationships.description: The judgment references this case in discussing the limitations on raising new factual arguments on appeal.
The judgment references the Ladd v Marshall requirements as the relevant test for admitting fresh evidence on appeal.

candidates.case_name: 莊如芬 v. 黃志輝 AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Aidan Tam in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Test for permanent injunction in libel
principles.paraphrase_en: The court will grant a permanent injunction in a defamation case if there is a reasonable basis to believe the defendant will publish the defamatory material again.

relationships.description: The judgment cites the 12th edition of this authoritative text as the source for the legal test governing permanent injunctions in libel cases.

candidates.case_name: CHAN KWOK KUEN v. LAI YAT HO AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Gary CC Lam in Chambers (Open to public)

principles.paraphrase_en: There is no automatic rule that costs must be awarded on an indemnity basis when an ex parte order is discharged due to material non-disclosure. An indemnity basis is typically appropriate only if the non-disclosure was deliberate or amounted to serious culpability.

A party making a factual assertion, such as a claim about their own state of knowledge, must provide supporting evidence from a person with direct knowledge of that fact. An affirmation from a solicitor who lacks that direct knowledge is insufficient.

relationships.description: The judgment cites the commentary in Hong Kong Civil Procedure 2026 regarding the principles for awarding costs on an indemnity basis following the discharge of an ex parte order.

candidates.case_name: COOMBER, PETER JOHN v. MR. PANG CHUNG PING
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Jonathan Wong in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The court has the power to strike out a statement of claim and dismiss an action where the claim is frivolous, vexatious, or discloses no reasonable cause of action, particularly when it is part of a pattern of unmeritorious litigation.

principles.principle_label: Misfeasance in public office as basis for action
principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff may bring an action in tort for misfeasance in public office against members or staff of the judiciary whom they allege to have committed wrongful acts in the course of their official duties.
The District Court has jurisdiction to hear claims against judicial officers, such as Temporary Deputy Registrars and judicial clerks, for alleged tortious conduct in the performance of their duties.

relationships.target_case_name: Coomber, Peter John v. Mr. Wong King Wah
relationships.description: The present judgment references the prior related action DCCJ 1956/2022, which involved similar claims against a different judicial officer.

relationships.target_case_name: Coomber, Peter John v. Ms. Lau Yin Ha Eva & Ms. Cheung Man Ying Sandy
relationships.description: The judgment cites the related action DCCJ 3416/2022, noting that a decision was already handed down striking out those claims.

candidates.case_name: CONVOY COLLATERAL LTD v. CHO KWAI CHEE (also known as CHO KWAI CHEE ROY) AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Coleman J in Chambers (Open to Public)

candidates.case_name: HONG KONG FAR INFRARED RAYS ASSOCIATION LTD v. LAM HONG NAM AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon B Chu J in Court

candidates.case_name: SHENZHEN HONEYCOMB SYSTEM CO LTD v. HCT TECHNOLOGIES (HONG KONG) CO LTD
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Indemnity costs may be ordered against a party who pursues an appeal that lacks reasonable prospects of success, particularly where the appeal appears to be a delaying tactic against the enforcement of an arbitral award.

A court may refuse to stay the enforcement of an arbitral award pending an appeal if it finds the appeal has no reasonable prospect of success and if enforcement would be prejudiced by delay, such as where the award creditor is based outside the jurisdiction.

relationships.description: The judgment references the prior decisions of Mimmie Chan J and Anthony Chan J in the same proceedings regarding the enforcement and stay of the arbitral award.

candidates.case_name: GERMAN POOL GROUP CO LTD AND OTHERS v. DYSON TECHNOLOGY LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Kent Yee in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A court order requiring disclosure must precisely identify the specific documents or categories of documents to be disclosed. Failure to do so may expose the person making the disclosure list to liability for swearing a false affidavit, even if they acted honestly.

The assessment of compensation payable under a cross-undertaking in damages is determined by the proper construction of the terms of that specific undertaking. The claim for damages arises from and is limited by the undertaking itself.

The fundamental purpose of a cross-undertaking in damages is to compensate the defendant for loss suffered due to wrongly granted interlocutory relief, not to punish the plaintiff. It ensures that unjustified harm can be remedied, thereby facilitating the grant of interim relief.
Exemplary damages at common law are confined to specific categories. One established category is for oppressive, arbitrary, or unconstitutional actions by government servants.
Exemplary damages are not available for a claim made under a cross-undertaking in damages, as the undertaking's purpose is compensatory, not punitive.
principles.paragraph_hrase_en: Exemplary damages are not available for a claim made under a cross-undertaking in damages, as the undertaking's purpose is compensatory, not punitive.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the requirement for precision in disclosure orders to avoid liability for false affidavits.

relationships.target_case_name: Al-Rawas v Pegasus Energy Ltd and Ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that compensation under a cross-undertaking depends on the proper construction of its terms.
The judgment follows the House of Lords' categorization which confines exemplary damages to specific categories, including oppressive government action.
The judgment distinguishes the alleged misuse of an injunction order from the conduct of solicitors executing an Anton Piller order in that case.

candidates.case_name: NGAI WAI YEUNG v. LAU YUN FUNG BERTHA
judges: Before: Master Matthew Ho in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Test for striking out defamation claims
principles.paraphrase_en: A defamation claim should only be struck out if it is plain and obvious that the words complained of are incapable of bearing a defamatory meaning. The application is decided on the pleadings alone, assuming the pleaded facts are true, and the claim should not be struck out unless its legal basis is unarguable or almost incontestably bad.

A statement is defamatory if it tends to lower the claimant in the estimation of others, causes them to be shunned or avoided, exposes them to hatred, contempt or ridicule, or imputes unfitness or lack of ability in their office or profession.

principles.principle_label: Defamatory meaning of imputing professional inefficiency
principles.paraphrase_en: Statements that arguably impugn a person's efficiency or professional ability prima facie establish a defamatory meaning. An imputation of inefficiency or incompetence in a person's profession or calling is usually defamatory.

For malicious falsehood, the plaintiff must prove that the complained words are false and were made with malice. Falsity is determined by what the words would convey to an ordinary reader. Saying something known to be untrue is malicious ipso facto.

principles.principle_label: Striking out where factual disputes exist
principles.paraphrase_en: Where the legal validity of a cause of action is sensitive to the facts, or where there are contentious issues of fact, an order to strike out should not be made. The claim should not be struck out if it is not plain and obvious that it must fail.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Wing Ho v Chong Lai Wah
relationships.description: The judgment applies the test from this case that a defamation claim should only be struck out if it is plain and obvious the words are incapable of bearing a defamatory meaning.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that a claim should not be struck out unless its legal basis is unarguable or almost incontestably bad.

relationships.target_case_name: Suen Wah Fai v Cis Global Opportunities Fund SPC
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle that disputed facts should be taken in favour of the party sought to be struck out and an order should not be made where legal validity is fact-sensitive.

relationships.target_case_name: Fan Chak Wah Edmond v Lam Wai Keung
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that the falsity of a statement in malicious falsehood is determined by what the words would convey to an ordinary reader.

relationships.target_case_name: Golden Field Glass Works Co Ltd v Yeung Chun Keung
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle that statements arguably impugning professional efficiency prima facie establish a defamatory meaning.

relationships.target_case_name: Sim Hok Gwan v Tin Tin Yat Po Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the principle that imputations of professional inefficiency are usually defamatory.

candidates.case_name: STEPHEN KRUGER v. SECRETARY FOR JUSTICE
judges: Before: Hon S T Poon J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: Police officers may lawfully act without an arrest or search warrant if they believe they are authorized by a specific statutory provision, such as section 17C of the Immigration Ordinance.

candidates.case_name: CHAN SHUNG FAI v. CHAN KAM WAH
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Kenneth K. H. Lee in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. WOO KA WAH WILCOX
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Casewell

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant's admission to the factual elements of an offence, including causation and resulting injury, can be accepted by the court when consistent with evidence.
The defence may concede careless driving while contesting that the driving fell far below the standard of a competent and careful driver, which is a distinct legal threshold.
A court may accept a defendant's account of an incident where it is consistent with objective evidence such as dash cam footage.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. IP KWOK KEUNG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Edward Wong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The primary sentencing principle for traffic offenses is general deterrence, with severe sentences serving to educate the public about the dangers of unsafe driving and to reinforce drivers' social responsibility to drive safely.
A defendant's clean driving record, absent any previous convictions, constitutes a mitigating factor in sentencing for traffic offenses.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Liu Kwok Chun
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from this case that a good driving record is a mitigating factor.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. BROWN JAMES MCCREADIE
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Charles J Chan

principles.principle_label: Partial consecutive sentencing for separate offences
principles.paraphrase_en: When sentencing for multiple separate offences arising from the same background, a court may order sentences to run partially consecutively, rather than fully concurrently or consecutively.

When imposing consecutive sentences for multiple offences, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the final aggregate sentence is not excessive and is proportionate to the offender's overall criminality.

cross_references.query: grievous bodily harm murder intent HKSAR

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. YU JIE
judges: Before: Hon Macrae Acting CJHC, Zervos and M Poon JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: There is a general presumption that a court should award costs to a defendant who has been acquitted, unless specific positive reasons exist to justify not making such an award.

The prosecution may be justified in proceeding to trial on a more serious charge, even if a defendant offered to plead guilty to a lesser offence, particularly where new material evidence emerges only during the defence case that could not have been reasonably anticipated.
A defendant who succeeds on appeal against conviction is generally entitled to be awarded the costs of that appeal, absent any specific reasons to the contrary.

cross_references.query: grievous bodily harm murder intent HKSAR

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LIN YU QUN
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Charles Chan

principles.principle_label: Starting point for GBH by dangerous driving
principles.paraphrase_en: The normal sentencing starting point for causing grievous bodily harm by dangerous driving is an immediate custodial sentence.
The length of the immediate custodial sentence is determined by the judge's assessment of the culpability of the driving and the seriousness of the injuries caused.

principles.principle_label: Maximum penalty for GBH by dangerous driving
principles.paraphrase_en: The maximum penalty for causing grievous bodily harm by dangerous driving upon indictment is a level 5 fine and 7 years' imprisonment.
Sentencing options other than immediate imprisonment, such as suspended sentences or community service orders, are only available in exceptional cases for this offence.

principles.principle_label: Severity of injury as aggravating factor
principles.paraphrase_en: The severity, extent, and nature of the injuries caused to the victim constitute an aggravating factor in sentencing for this offence.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. NG SHEUNG YEE
judges: Before: HH Judge Dufton

principles.paraphrase_en: Disregarding traffic lights when approaching a pedestrian crossing constitutes a serious aggravating feature in dangerous driving cases, with conscious disregard likely placing the offense in the most serious category.
Each sentencing decision must be based on the specific facts and circumstances of the individual case, and comparisons with other cases may provide limited assistance.

principles.principle_label: Sentencing range for pedestrian crossing incidents
principles.paraphrase_en: For cases involving pedestrians being knocked down on pedestrian crossings, the Court of Appeal has held that immediate imprisonment ranging from 6 to 20 months is generally appropriate.

principles.principle_label: Mandatory disqualification for GBH by dangerous driving
principles.paraphrase_en: There is a mandatory disqualification of not less than 2 years for the offense of causing grievous bodily harm by dangerous driving, unless the court finds special reasons not to impose it.

A court lacks the power to impose a lifetime driving disqualification for causing grievous bodily harm by dangerous driving; such power exists only for causing death by dangerous driving or driving under the influence of specified illicit drugs.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Lam Siu Tong
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that disregarding traffic lights at pedestrian crossings is a serious aggravating feature in dangerous driving offenses.
The judgment cites this case as an example where a lower starting point was justified due to very unusual circumstances involving conscious disregard for traffic lights.
The judgment references this case as one where a higher sentence was imposed compared to HKSAR v Lee Yau Wing.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Chu Wing Ying Christine
relationships.description: The judgment references this case as one where a higher sentence was imposed compared to HKSAR v Lee Yau Wing.
The judgment distinguishes this case as unhelpful because it predates the enactment of the offense of causing grievous bodily harm by dangerous driving.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LAI TSZ CHUNG
judges: Before: Hon Anna Lai J

principles.paraphrase_en: For offences that are viewed with abhorrence by society, the sentencing principles of denunciation and deterrence must be given primary emphasis, resulting in severe sentences, while rehabilitation plays a much less significant role.

relationships.description: The present judgment references the authority of Mr Justice of Appeal Woo from this case.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN KWAN CHUEN
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish an offence under section 36A of the Road Traffic Ordinance, the prosecution must prove that the defendant caused grievous bodily harm to a person by driving a motor vehicle on a road in a manner that is dangerous.

Dangerous driving is established when the defendant's driving falls far below the standard expected of a competent and careful driver, and it would be obvious to such a driver that driving in that way would be dangerous.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KO YUN SANG
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. SHEK TSZ CHUNG
judges: Before: Hon Barnes J

principles.paraphrase_en: When imposing consecutive sentences for multiple offences, the court must apply the totality principle to ensure the overall sentence is not excessive and remains proportionate to the offender's overall criminality.

Where separate offences arise from distinct criminal acts with different legal character, the court may order sentences to run consecutively, particularly when one offence involves an attempt to evade justice for another.

relationships.description: The judgment references the Court of Appeal's listing of aggravating factors in dangerous driving cases from Chu Wing Yin.

candidates.case_name: MA CHUN MAN v. COMMISSIONER OF CORRECTIONAL SERVICES
judges: Before: Hon Alex Lee J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Prisoners do not have a legal right or entitlement to remission; the Commissioner of Correctional Services possesses the discretion to grant remission based on a prisoner's industry and good conduct.

principles.principle_label: Nature of National Security Test assessment
principles.paraphrase_en: The Commissioner's assessment of whether a prisoner passes the National Security Test is a holistic, predictive, and evaluative exercise based on all relevant factors concerning the risk to national security upon early release. It does not involve a legal burden of proof or a presumption against referral for early release.

A distinction exists between a measure that constitutes a penalty and a measure concerning the execution or enforcement of a penalty. Changes to early release provisions by the legislature generally relate to enforcement, not the imposition of a new penalty.

The concept of a 'penalty' under the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights and the Hong Kong Bill of Rights is autonomous, requiring courts to assess the substantive nature of a measure beyond its formal classification.

The preventive act of recalling a prisoner to prison under a supervision order scheme is an adjunct to rehabilitation and a tool to render the scheme effective, not an act of, or akin to, sentencing for a new offence.

relationships.description: The judgment follows the holding that powers to impose supervision orders and recall prisoners do not constitute a heavier penalty and are not in breach of constitutional provisions.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong Tak Wai v Commissioner of Correctional Services
relationships.description: The judgment cites the case for its description of the Correctional Services Department's past practice regarding the printing of expected and latest discharge dates on prisoner identity tags.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v Leung Hiu Yeung & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that remission is a factor to be taken into account in applications for review of sentence and sentence appeals.
The judgment applies the principle from the European Court of Human Rights regarding the autonomous concept of a 'penalty' and the distinction between a penalty and the enforcement of a penalty.
The judgment cites this case alongside Del Río Prada for the principle distinguishing a substantive penalty from measures concerning the execution of a penalty.

candidates.case_name: YUEN TSZ CHUN (as the administrator of the estate of CHAN CHOI LIN (陳彩蓮), deceased) v. LO CHI KEUNG AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon B Chu J in Court

principles.principle_label: Interpretation of spousal accounting under s.8A(2)
principles.paraphrase_en: Where the value of beneficial interests acquired by a surviving spouse under foreign intestacy law exceeds the HKD 1,000,000 net sum specified in section 4(4) of the Intestates' Estates Ordinance, the reference to that 'net sum' in section 4(4) is to be omitted, and the distribution of the residuary estate proceeds according to the remaining provisions of section 4(4)(a) and (b).
The interpretation of statutory provisions, such as the spousal accounting rules in section 8A(2), must be consistent with the discernible legislative intent behind those provisions.

principles.principle_label: Plain meaning as primary interpretive tool
principles.paraphrase_en: The primary method of statutory interpretation is to give effect to the plain and natural meaning of the statutory wording.
The legislative intent behind the relevant provisions was for the spousal accounting rules to operate uniformly, regardless of whether the assets were governed by foreign or local law.

relationships.description: The judgment cites In re Collens for the principle that succession to immovable property is governed by the lex situs (law of the place where the property is situated).

candidates.case_name: SUCCESS LANE DEVELOPMENT LTD v. IFC DEVELOPMENT (SUITE) LTD AS AGENT FOR IFC DEVELOPMENT LTD
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Alan Kwong in Chambers (Open to the Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: A corporation may have standing to bring a harassment claim in its own capacity, not because it can experience emotional distress, but because it may incur expenses to address harassment affecting its employees.
Courts will not decide academic questions that serve no useful purpose and do not affect the parties' substantive interests.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the Court of Appeal's reasoning that corporations may bring harassment claims based on economic harm rather than emotional distress.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that courts should not adjudicate purely academic controversies.

candidates.case_name: CHAN YEE TAK RACHEL AND ANOTHER v. DSM INTERIOR DESIGN LTD
judges: Coram: Master Dick Ho in Court

principles.principle_label: Breach of reasonable skill and care duty
principles.paraphrase_en: A contractor can be held responsible for damages caused by their failure to exercise reasonable skill and care, including failing to replace defective materials delivered to the site or allowing problems to develop while in control of the premises during works.
Losses are recoverable in a breach of contract claim if they were foreseeable and flowed directly from the breach.
Damages for loss of rental income can be awarded based on honest evidence of market rental value, with the period of loss reasonably determined by the court.

candidates.case_name: TSE LAI SING v. TUNG WAH GROUP OF HOSPITALS
judges: Coram: His Honour Judge H. Au-Yeung in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An expert witness owes a duty to the court to conduct necessary tests, review all relevant records, and provide an impartial opinion on the real condition of a party, rather than simply accepting the party's account without verification.

When a claimant's complaints involve pain or psychiatric illness which are difficult to verify objectively, the court must carefully examine all evidence to determine as a matter of fact whether the claimant has exaggerated the alleged problems.

principles.principle_label: Expert reliance on subjective vs objective evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: An expert's opinion should not rely solely on a patient's subjective complaints while ignoring objective observations, as it is possible for a patient to deliberately answer screening tests incorrectly to exaggerate problems.

A psychiatric expert must provide an adequate factual basis for concluding that diagnostic criteria are met, including specific inquiry into the frequency and nature of symptoms during relevant periods, rather than relying on general statements.

principles.principle_label: Court's de novo hearing on appeal
principles.paraphrase_en: When an appeal is lodged under the relevant ordinance, the court must conduct a fresh hearing and is not bound by the assessment of the lower tribunal, allowing parties to argue for outcomes different from that assessment even if no cross-appeal was filed.

candidates.case_name: CHOW WING MAN v. AU TZE PUI AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Kent Yee in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A party's failure to comply with an undertaking given to the court as part of an injunction order constitutes a flagrant breach of that order, particularly when the undertaking was offered voluntarily in supporting affirmations.
A director who withdraws company funds under the pretext of safeguarding them, but subsequently dissipates those funds, commits a breach of trust against the company.
An allegation that a party holds shares on a beneficial trust for another requires evidence that the registered shareholder was aware of such a claim, especially at the time of an ex parte hearing.

principles.principle_label: Assistance in breach of exclusive distribution
principles.paraphrase_en: Assisting a company to deal with products that are subject to an exclusive distribution agreement with another company can be a relevant factor in establishing wrongful conduct, regardless of whether the assisting party holds an official position in that company.

candidates.case_name: NAIDA BADUGE NISHANTHA PRADEEP DE SILVA alias BOVI THANTRIGE LASANTHA v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement decisions is not to reassess the claim or evidence anew, but to examine the decision for legal errors, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.

The Director of Immigration and the Torture Claims Appeal Board are the primary decision-makers responsible for assessing evidence, making factual findings, and determining risk of harm, state protection, and internal relocation viability in non-refoulement claims.

Non-refoulement protection is assessed against the principle prohibiting return to persecution under Article 33 and the refugee definition in Article 1A(2) of the 1951 Refugee Convention and its 1967 Protocol.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. FUNG HING NAM, MICKEY
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge M Chow

principles.paraphrase_en: A person commits attempted robbery when they, with intent to steal, attempt to use force or the threat of force on another person to obtain property immediately before or at the time of the attempt.
A defendant's admission made under police caution, including their stated intent and motive for the criminal act, constitutes admissible evidence against them.

candidates.case_name: RE GUY KWOK-HUNG LAM
judges: Before: Hon Barma, G Lam and Chow JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The power to award indemnity costs is discretionary, and courts must avoid establishing rigid categories that would compromise this discretionary character.
Creating fixed categories of cases where indemnity costs would routinely be ordered should be exceptional and requires compelling justification.
A finding that particular conduct does not justify indemnity costs is a decision on the specific facts of the case and does not establish a general legal principle.

candidates.case_name: LAM SHUN YUEN AND ANOTHER v. WONG SUET KIU AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Master Raymond Chu (Paper Disposal)

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant must give prior warning to a plaintiff before applying to the court to vary the costs scale, when the plaintiff is considering whether to accept a sanctioned payment. This prevents the defendant from ambushing the plaintiff with an unexpected costs application.

The rules governing sanctioned payments are designed to promote amicable settlement by providing certainty about costs consequences, allowing a plaintiff to properly assess their exposure to or entitlement to costs before deciding to accept the payment.

candidates.case_name: MANSOOR HUSSAIN SYED IBRAHIM v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge K.W. Lung :

principles.paraphrase_en: The assessment of a claim for non-refoulement protection under the Refugee Convention requires evaluating the risk of persecution upon return to the country of origin.
A claimant alleging persecution based on political opinion must provide evidence of holding a significant position or profile that would make them a target.
A material change in country conditions, such as a general election, can diminish or eliminate a previously claimed risk of harm upon return.

relationships.description: The present judgment references the Court of Appeal's decision in Re: Kartini regarding non-refoulement principles.

candidates.case_name: SHEIKH MAHASIN v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP and Cheung JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: To establish a claim for protection against refoulement, the decision-maker must be satisfied that there is a real risk the applicant would face serious harm if returned to their country of origin.

A single incident of past violence causing only minor injuries, coupled with verbal threats on other occasions, may be insufficient by itself to establish a real risk of future serious harm for non-refoulement purposes.
In assessing a non-refoulement claim, the decision-maker must consider whether effective state protection would be available to the applicant in the country of origin.
A non-refoulement claim may be rejected if it would not be unreasonable for the applicant to relocate internally within their country of origin to avoid the risk of harm.
The principle of non-refoulement in Hong Kong derives from Article 33 of the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees and its 1967 Protocol.

candidates.case_name: MEHMOOD TALAT v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge (Non-refoulement Claims) Teresa Wu :

principles.principle_label: Leave to apply for judicial review - arguability threshold
principles.paraphrase_en: Leave to apply for judicial review will only be granted if the intended application is reasonably arguable and has realistic prospects of success.

relationships.target_case_name: Peter Po Fun Chan v Winnie Cheung & Another
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the established test from this case that leave for judicial review requires the application to be reasonably arguable with realistic prospects of success.

candidates.case_name: X-SPOT GLOBAL LTD v. HUOBI GLOBAL LTD
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers (Open to Public)

candidates.case_name: WONG HOI HUNG v. DUNG KAM KIT
judges: Before: Master Kot in Court

principles.principle_label: Assessment period for total loss of earnings
principles.paraphrase_en: The period for calculating total loss of pre-trial earnings runs from the date of the accident until the date the plaintiff can reasonably resume work, plus an additional period to allow for job searching.
Pre-trial loss of earnings should end upon the expiry of the medically justified sick leave period when the plaintiff is deemed fit to resume their pre-accident employment.

principles.principle_label: Future loss assessment based on work capacity
principles.paraphrase_en: If a plaintiff is medically assessed as capable of resuming their pre-accident job, no award should be made for post-trial future loss of earnings.

principles.principle_label: Loss of earning capacity assessment criteria
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a plaintiff can return to their former job, compensation for loss of earning capacity depends on evaluating the real and substantial risk of future job loss and determining a fair and reasonable award for that risk.

candidates.case_name: KHAN MURAD v. CHAN YUK MING
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Zabrina Lau in Court (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: Where a plaintiff's testimony is inconsistent with contemporaneous documents, implausible, or internally contradictory, the court may find the plaintiff not credible and treat their evidence with great caution.
A court may find a plaintiff's evidence on the extent and severity of pain and disability to be exaggerated when it is contradicted by expert medical opinion and objective circumstances.

The court must exercise particular caution when assessing claims of chronic pain that persist beyond normal recovery time, especially where there is no objective medical evidence to support the existence of a continuous injury.

principles.principle_label: Mitigation of loss - failure to seek work
principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff's failure to obtain necessary qualifications or to make any attempt to find alternative employment, despite claiming an ability and desire to work, may undermine their credibility and their claim for loss of earnings.

In assessing damages, particularly where the plaintiff's credibility is in doubt, the court will rely primarily on contemporaneous documents and expert joint reports rather than the plaintiff's subjective assertions.

relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Li Cheuk Lam that the court must take particular care when assessing claims of chronic pain lacking objective evidence.

candidates.case_name: WONG LAM WEI HOLLY v. HOSPITAL AUTHORITY
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Joseph Vaughan in Court

principles.principle_label: Employer duty of care on return from sick leave
principles.paraphrase_en: A ward manager or supervisor has a duty to inquire about an employee's medical condition upon their return from sick leave to assess their fitness for work and assign appropriate duties.

principles.principle_label: Witness evidence relevance to central issue
principles.paraphrase_en: The evidence of a party's witnesses may not be determinative of the central legal issue in the case, and its primary relevance may be confined to matters of credibility.

principles.principle_label: Credit for prior payments in compensation
principles.paraphrase_en: Any periodic payments already received by a claimant for the same injury must be deducted from the final compensation award.

relationships.target_case_name: Pak Sai Ming v. JV Fitness Limited
relationships.description: The present judgment references this case for a legal principle concerning compensation assessment.

candidates.case_name: FAISAL v. OCEAN PARK CORPORATION
judges: Coram: His Honour Judge Harold Leong in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An expert opinion may be rejected if it is based on unreliable subjective evidence from a witness and demonstrates selective use of objective evidence.
A court may find a plaintiff's evidence to be fabricated and indicative of dishonesty if it appears to be invented during the course of proceedings.

A plaintiff's failure to work may be attributable to factors other than the alleged accident, such as pre-existing conditions or a failure to mitigate loss, and any disadvantage in the labour market must be proven to be significant and causally linked to the accident.

candidates.case_name: CHOI CHUN TAI v. LAM SZE LING
judges: Coram: Master Vivian Lee

principles.paraphrase_en: When interlocutory judgment on liability is entered against a defendant due to their failure to file a defence, the issue of liability is conclusively determined against them, and the proceeding moves solely to the assessment of damages.
A court must be satisfied that proper service of the notice of hearing and relevant documents has been effected on the defendant before proceeding with an assessment of damages hearing.

principles.principle_label: Procedure for evidence at assessment hearing
principles.paraphrase_en: At a damages assessment hearing, a plaintiff may adopt their filed witness statement as their evidence-in-chief, and they may be the sole witness testifying.

principles.principle_label: Consequence of no answer to damages claim
principles.paraphrase_en: When a defendant files no answer to the detailed heads of damages claimed by the plaintiff, the court proceeds to assess those claims based on the evidence presented by the plaintiff.

candidates.case_name: GURUNG KUL RAJ v. MING TAI CONSTRUCTION ENGINEERING COMPANY LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Coram: Deputy District Judge Gary C C Lam in Court

principles.principle_label: Sufficiency of warning for safe work practice
principles.paraphrase_en: A general warning like 'be careful' is insufficient to constitute adequate notification of a specific hazardous action, such as the release of a heavy object, in a workplace context. Proper communication requires clear advance notice of the specific action to be taken.

principles.principle_label: Employer's vicarious liability for employee's negligence
principles.paraphrase_en: An employer is vicariously liable for the negligence of its employee where the employee's actions, performed in the course of employment, foreseeably cause injury. This liability is based on the employer-employee relationship, not on control of the worksite.

A plaintiff is not contributorily negligent if, on the facts, they had no knowledge or reasonable means of anticipating the specific negligent act that caused their injury, even if they were observing the actor.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of plaintiff's credibility in injury claims
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may find a plaintiff has exaggerated their injuries where their oral testimony is materially inconsistent with contemporaneous medical records, and they provide implausible explanations for those inconsistencies.

principles.principle_label: Liability of principal contractor for subcontractor's employee
principles.paraphrase_en: A principal contractor (or site owner) is not liable for the negligence of a subcontractor's employee where the principal contractor exercises no control over that employee's work methods. Liability rests with the direct employer.

relationships.target_case_name: Lui Cho Yin v Lui Kin Bong & others
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case to find that a principal contractor is not liable for the negligence of a subcontractor's employee over whom it has no control.

relationships.target_case_name: Shabar Rehman v Sunny Success Holdings Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment finds this case bears more resemblance to the present facts than others cited, using it as a comparator for assessing damages for a minor finger injury.

relationships.target_case_name: Rai Tej Kumar v Fulcrum Engineering & Construction Ltd & another
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case, which was presented by counsel, but ultimately finds another authority more analogous to the present circumstances.

candidates.case_name: YU CHUN SHING v. HUSSAIN AHMED AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge N. Hui in Court

principles.principle_label: Treatment of subjective vs objective medical evidence
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may treat a patient's subjective reports to medical experts as hearsay and is free to make different factual findings at trial, whereas objective medical evidence from examinations and investigations must be relied upon as it falls within expert expertise.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of multiple injuries for PSLA
principles.paraphrase_en: When assessing damages for pain, suffering and loss of amenities involving multiple injuries, the court should not simply combine awards for each separate injury but must assess the overall effect of all injuries on the injured person as a whole.

principles.principle_label: Court's role vs expert's role in evaluating malingering
principles.paraphrase_en: The duty of an expert to conduct a forensic evaluation is distinct from the court's function to make findings of fact; the court may reject an expert's opinion that a plaintiff is malingering if it finds the plaintiff to be genuine based on the evidence.

principles.principle_label: Burden for loss of future earnings claim
principles.paraphrase_en: An equivocal medical expert opinion stating that an injured person is unable to return to pre-accident employment is insufficient to discharge the burden of proof required for an award of damages under that head of loss.

principles.principle_label: Basis for loss of earning capacity award
principles.paraphrase_en: Loss of earning capacity is a recognized head of damages covering the future risk that a claimant will lose employment and suffer financial loss due to disadvantage in the labour market, appropriate where the plaintiff clearly suffers from a labour market handicap.

relationships.target_case_name: Zahid Anwar v Graceful Sound Ltd and Ors
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case regarding the role of medical experts in the assessment of damages.

relationships.target_case_name: Li Cheuk Lam v Cheung Sun Tai & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case distinguishing between subjective and objective medical evidence.

relationships.target_case_name: Yun Pun Yuen v Ng Kwok Man trading as East Mountain Engineering Company & Ors
relationships.description: The judgment applies the approach from this case for assessing damages for multiple injuries by considering the overall effect on the injured person.

relationships.target_case_name: Rai Surya Prakash v Pacific Crown Security Services Ltd & Anor
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case regarding how a court may assess a plaintiff's lack of expression of discomfort during court proceedings.

relationships.target_case_name: Tamang Tikaram v Tong Kee Company Limited and Others
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case distinguishing the expert's forensic evaluation role from the court's fact-finding function regarding malingering.

relationships.target_case_name: Yuk Kok Wing v Lee Tim Loi
relationships.description: The judgment follows the well-settled principle from this case regarding the basis for awarding damages for loss of earning capacity.

candidates.case_name: CHAM CHI MING JOIE v. SCENIC RESTAURANT operated by 9288 LTD
judges: Before: Master Matthew Leung in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: An accident can be found to have caused or triggered a previously asymptomatic, pre-existing degenerative condition, making the defendant liable for the resulting symptoms.

When assessing damages for an injury, the court must apportion the plaintiff's condition between the effects caused by the accident and those attributable to a pre-existing condition, based on medical evidence.

candidates.case_name: IMELDA QUINTO ABONG v. CORONER
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Court

principles.principle_label: Threshold for ordering an inquest under s.20
principles.paraphrase_en: Under section 20(1)(a) of the Coroners Ordinance, the Court may order an inquest if satisfied that a coroner has failed to hold an inquest that ought to have been held. The Court's role is to consider de novo whether an inquest 'ought to be held', not to review the merits of or find fault in the coroner's original decision.

principles.principle_label: Scope of inquests for sudden deaths
principles.paraphrase_en: A sudden death, where the deceased had no serious prior disease, falls within the situations where an inquest may be held under section 14(1) of the Coroners Ordinance. This applies irrespective of whether the death occurred under suspicious circumstances, by accident, or by violence.

principles.principle_label: Purpose of an inquest under s.27
principles.paraphrase_en: In deciding whether an inquest should be held, it is relevant to consider the statutory purposes of inquests as set out in section 27 of the Coroners Ordinance, which includes preventing the recurrence of similar fatalities.

Where an applicant argues an inquest ought to be held because useful recommendations might be made, the applicant must identify at least broadly the areas for such recommendations and point to some evidential basis suggesting the coroner or jury would be likely to make them.

principles.principle_label: Final determination for ordering an inquest
principles.paraphrase_en: The court must be satisfied that an inquest ought to be held to inquire into the cause of and circumstances connected with the death, and to ascertain the circumstances in which the deceased came by their death.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v William Ng Esq, a Coroner
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the reasoning in William Ng that the court's role under s.20(1)(a) is a de novo consideration of whether an inquest ought to be held, not a review of the coroner's decision.

relationships.target_case_name: Secretary for Justice v William Ng, Esq
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from William Ng regarding the applicant's burden to identify areas for potential recommendations when arguing an inquest should be held for that purpose.

candidates.case_name: CHAN YUK WAI v. SWIRE PROPERTIES MANAGEMENT LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Isabella Chu in Court

principles.principle_label: Employer's duty to provide safe workplace
principles.paraphrase_en: An employer's duty to provide a safe place of work is not negated by an employee's improper use of equipment if the workplace itself was unsafe due to inadequate lighting.

principles.principle_label: Causation inference from breach of duty
principles.paraphrase_en: Where the precise mechanism of an accident cannot be ascertained, if on a balance of probabilities the accident could not have happened without a breach of duty, it can be held to be caused by that breach.

principles.principle_label: Evidential value of sick leave certificates
principles.paraphrase_en: Sick leave certificates are merely one piece of evidence that must be evaluated in light of all available evidence, including medical evidence, and are not determinative on their own.
An employee's failure to use available safety measures (like a handrail) or to use work equipment properly can amount to contributory negligence, reducing the employer's liability.

principles.principle_label: Causation for loss of employment benefits
principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff cannot claim for loss of employment benefits (like pension accrual) if they left employment out of personal choice rather than as a result of their injuries.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Stone v Taffe regarding inferring causation from a breach of duty where the exact accident mechanism is unclear.

relationships.target_case_name: Tam Fu Yip Fip v Sincere Engineering & Trading Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle that sick leave certificates are just one piece of evidence to be evaluated in context.
The judgment cites Huang Xinsheng as a comparative case for assessing the severity of the plaintiff's injuries.
The judgment cites Liu W as a comparative case where the injuries were more serious than the present plaintiff's.

candidates.case_name: LIN CHUI LING CHELLY v. KINGS BEAUTY HOUSE LTD
judges: Before: His Honour Judge KC Chan in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may proceed with a trial in the absence of a defendant if satisfied that proper notice of the trial has been given and the defendant has chosen not to participate despite having been previously involved in the proceedings.
Where a plaintiff's evidence is uncontested and nothing suggests it is not credible or reliable, and the plaintiff is found to be a frank and fair witness, the court may accept that evidence in full.

A defendant is vicariously liable for the negligence of an individual if that individual is found to be the defendant's employee or agent, and the negligence occurred in the course of that relationship.

relationships.description: The present judgment finds the facts of this case to an extent similar and apposite for application here, particularly regarding liability for injury sustained while under professional care.

candidates.case_name: UNWAR NAVIN KUMAR v. GURKHAS CONSRTUCTION LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Fung J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court may find a plaintiff has exaggerated their reported pain where the medical expert's assessment of subjective pain scales (like NPRS) is inadequately explained or conducted, and where this finding aligns with other credibility concerns.

A medical expert's opinion may be found unhelpful and given little weight if it fails to consider or explain relevant medical records, such as a sudden change in a patient's reported pain levels without reference to prior hospital documentation.
Whole person impairment assessments can provide an objective frame of reference for awarding damages for pain, suffering, and loss of amenities (PSLA).

A court may find a plaintiff not credible based on inconsistencies between their assertions of severe pain and contemporaneous medical records indicating no tenderness, coupled with findings of exaggeration in subjective pain tests.
A finding that a plaintiff suffers no handicap in the labour market due to residual symptoms or pain leads to the conclusion that there is no loss of earning capacity to be compensated.

relationships.target_case_name: Chan Yuet Keung v Harmony (International) Knitting Factory Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment cites Bharwaney J's holding that whole person impairment assessment provides an objective frame of reference for PSLA awards.

candidates.case_name: HUANG LIDONG v. KWONG WING CHUNG
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Frances Lok in Chambers (Paper Disposal)

principles.paraphrase_en: A party's negligence or lack of diligence in monitoring the progress of their own litigation does not constitute a good or sufficient reason to justify granting an extension of time for appealing.

principles.principle_label: Functus officio and jurisdiction to vary
principles.paraphrase_en: Once a judgment or order is perfected, the judge or master who made it becomes functus officio and cannot reopen or vary it. Such variation can only be made by a court with appellate jurisdiction over that decision.

principles.principle_label: Summary judgment based on pleaded case
principles.paraphrase_en: A court can only grant summary judgment based on the case as pleaded in the statement of claim. Defects or omissions in the statement of claim cannot be remedied by evidence presented in affidavits supporting the summary judgment application.

Once the statutory time limit for appealing has expired, the successful party in the lower court is entitled to treat the judgment in their favor as final. Granting leave to appeal out of time inherently causes at least some prejudice to that party.

relationships.target_case_name: Ampittia Inc v B‑Tech (Holdings) Limited & Ors
relationships.description: The present judgment clarifies that Ampittia does not support the broad proposition that a judge can reopen their own perfected order, but rather dealt with a refusal to order further fortification of an undertaking.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. CHAN TSZ WAH AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Toh, D’Almada Remedios and Alex Lee JJ in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: For conspiracy to commit a National Security Law offence, the sentence must comply with the mandatory sentencing bands prescribed for the substantive NSL offence, subject to the application of NSL section 33.

The National Security Law is to be construed using the common law approach. Where convictions result, local sentencing laws and principles are applied in tandem with the NSL's sentencing provisions to give effect to the principle of convergence, compatibility and complementarity.

The seriousness of a National Security Law offence, such as conspiracy to commit subversion, depends on a holistic assessment of factors including degree of planning, methods used, scale, number of people involved, potential harms, and actual outcomes.

principles.principle_label: Advanced age as mercy in sentencing
principles.paraphrase_en: An offender's advanced age may be considered in sentencing not as a matter of principle but as an act of mercy. This factor must be balanced against the gravity of the offence and the public interest in adequate punishment for serious crimes.

When sentencing an offender already serving a prison term for another offence, the court must apply the totality principle by considering all terms of imprisonment the offender will become subject to, approaching the matter as if the cases were heard together.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle that conspiracy to commit an NSL offence must comply with the mandatory sentencing bands for the substantive offence.
The judgment follows the Court of Final Appeal's holding on using the common law approach to construe the NSL and applying local sentencing laws alongside NSL provisions.

relationships.target_case_name: Director of Immigration v Chong Fung Yuen
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as establishing the common law approach to statutory construction, which applies to the NSL.

relationships.target_case_name: HKSAR v Ng Gordon Ching Hang and Others
relationships.description: The judgment applies the factors for assessing seriousness of NSL offences as outlined in this case concerning conspiracy to commit subversion.
The judgment applies the principle that advanced age may be considered in sentencing as an act of mercy rather than as a matter of principle.
The judgment applies the totality principle approach that sentences for separate cases should be considered as if heard together.

candidates.case_name: CHUNG CHUN WAH v. J.V. FITNESS LTD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge David Chan (Open to public)

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for causation of injury
principles.paraphrase_en: The claimant bears the legal burden of proving, on the balance of probabilities, that a specific injury was caused by the accident in question.

principles.principle_label: Standard for rebutting presumption of incapacity
principles.paraphrase_en: An employer can rebut the statutory presumption that a period of sick leave granted by a treating doctor constitutes a period of incapacity, but the court must give due weight to the decision of the treating doctors who assessed the claimant at the material time.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of loss of earning capacity
principles.paraphrase_en: The assessment of loss of earning capacity should be based on the court's factual findings regarding the nature and extent of the injuries actually caused by the accident.

Where medical experts provide conflicting opinions on the causation of an injury without citing supporting medical literature or providing key information (such as the force required to cause an injury), the court may find it impossible to resolve the conflict in favour of the claimant.

candidates.case_name: WONG WAI MAN v. CONCORD EXPRESS (HONG KONG) LTD
judges: Before: His Honour Judge H. Au-Yeung in Court

principles.principle_label: Assessment of compensation after liability established
principles.paraphrase_en: When interlocutory judgment on liability has been entered in an employee's compensation claim, the subsequent trial hearing is for the sole purpose of assessing the quantum of compensation.
A trial may proceed in the absence of a respondent if the court is satisfied that the respondent has been properly served with notice of the trial and should therefore have knowledge of it.

principles.principle_label: Injury arising in course of employment
principles.paraphrase_en: An injury sustained by an employee while performing an instructed task, such as clearing customs during a cross-border delivery, arises out of and in the course of employment.

candidates.case_name: DR. CHU KIN WAH v. THE MEDICAL COUNCIL OF HONG KONG
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, G Lam JA and Mimmie Chan J in Court

principles.principle_label: Appellate deference to professional disciplinary bodies
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will be reluctant to interfere with a professional disciplinary tribunal's assessment of matters requiring professional judgment and expertise, as the tribunal comprises members with specialized knowledge not possessed by judges.

principles.principle_label: Scope of disciplinary charge and procedural fairness
principles.paraphrase_en: A disciplinary tribunal is not necessarily confined to the exact case as opened by the presenting officer, provided its findings arise from matters properly investigated during the inquiry and do not constitute a wholly new case resulting in procedural unfairness.

principles.principle_label: Application of Bolam test in disciplinary proceedings
principles.paraphrase_en: The Bolam principle, which states a doctor is not negligent if acting in accordance with a practice accepted as proper by a responsible body of medical opinion, is relevant not only to civil negligence but also to professional disciplinary proceedings.

The principle of empirical antibiotic therapy is to cover possible expected organisms, tailored to the expected risk, not merely to cover as many organisms as possible; a broader spectrum does not necessarily make the prescription more appropriate.

A failure to conduct adequate investigation to confirm or exclude a diagnosis before deciding on treatment can constitute a breach of professional standards, such as the principle of prescribing antibiotics.

relationships.target_case_name: Sin Chung Yin Ronald v Dental Council of Hong Kong
relationships.description: The present judgment follows the principle from this case that appellate courts should be diffident about interfering with a disciplinary body's assessment of matters calling for professional judgment.

relationships.target_case_name: Bolam v Friern Hospital Management Committee
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Bolam test, stating it is relevant to professional disciplinary proceedings for determining whether a doctor's conduct met the required standard.

relationships.target_case_name: Sidaway v Board of Governors of the Bethlem Royal Hospital and the Maudsley Hospital
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case to support the explanation of the Bolam principle regarding acting in accordance with a responsible body of medical opinion.

candidates.case_name: PANG LOK SZE v. DIRECTOR OF PUBLIC PROSECUTIONS
judges: Before: Hon Alex Lee J in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's power to review prosecutorial decisions is extremely limited and only available in rare circumstances where there is evidence of bad faith or unconstitutional conduct by the Department of Justice.
The Basic Law protects the independence of the Department of Justice in controlling criminal prosecutions, shielding it from judicial interference.
A prosecutorial decision may be subject to challenge only if there is evidence it was made under political instruction or in bad faith, rendering it unconstitutional.

relationships.description: The present judgment follows the Court of Appeal's approval of the principles established in RV v Director of Immigration regarding prosecutorial independence.
The judgment applies the principle from RV v Director of Immigration that Basic Law Article 63 protects prosecutorial independence from judicial encroachment.

candidates.case_name: SIR ELLY KADOORIE & SONS LTD (FOR AND ON BEHALF OF ITSELF, ITS CURRENT AND FORMER OFFICERS, EMPLOYEES AND AGENTS, INCLUDING ITS LEGAL REPRESENTATIVES, MESSRS SIMMONS & SIMMONS) v. SAMANTHA JANE BRADLEY
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge H. Au-Yeung in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The Order 14A procedure for determining questions of law or document construction is suitable for putting an early end to an entire case or any claim or issue within it, but is inappropriate where factual issues are interwoven with the legal issues to be determined.
The question of law or construction to be determined under Order 14A must be stated in clear, careful, and precise terms without difficulty, obscurity, or ambiguity.

The court has a very wide discretion under Order 14A, which can be invoked for questions that finally determine the entire cause or any claim or issue, but the process does not contemplate the submission of trivial matters for determination.

A challenge to a plaintiff's locus standi (standing) should not be raised by way of defence, but must be raised at the outset or when it comes to the attention of the court or the defence, and must be decided promptly.

principles.principle_label: Representative actions - conflicting vs divergent interests
principles.paraphrase_en: A distinction exists between cases where class members have conflicting interests and cases where they have merely divergent interests; representation by the same person is permissible if advancing the case of affected members does not prejudice others.

relationships.target_case_name: Allen v Gulf Oil Refining Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites Allen for the principle that an Order 14A question must be formulated clearly and precisely.

relationships.target_case_name: Shell Hong Kong Ltd v Yeung Wai Man Kiu Yip Co Ltd & Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites Shell Hong Kong for the principle that the Order 14A procedure does not contemplate determining trivial matters.

relationships.target_case_name: 佛山市宏達發展公司清算組 v East Legend Investment Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case regarding the appropriate timing for raising a challenge to a plaintiff's locus standi.

relationships.target_case_name: Korso Finance Establishment Anstalt v. John Wedge
relationships.description: The judgment cites this unreported case as an example where factors were relevant to the Court of Appeal's exercise of discretion under Order 14A.

candidates.case_name: L, T v. M, MD
judges: Coram: Her Honour Judge Sharon D. Melloy in Chambers (Not open to public). Paper disposal

principles.paraphrase_en: Leave to appeal requires an arguable case with a reasonable chance of success, and appellate courts will not interfere with a lower court's exercise of discretion unless it was clearly incorrect or based on an error of law.

The general rule is that costs should not be ordered against a party who is capable of paying their own costs, especially when such an order would represent a significant departure from standard practice.

principles.principle_label: Children's welfare not a costs factor
principles.paraphrase_en: The best interests of children cannot be used as a justification for an adverse costs order against another party when the real purpose appears to be financial gain rather than genuine welfare concerns.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. LO KIN LOK
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Tam

principles.paraphrase_en: When imposing consecutive sentences for multiple offences, the court must consider the overall total sentence to ensure it is not excessive and is just and proportionate.
Offences committed in separate incidents, even if close in time and location, generally warrant consecutive sentences, subject to the totality principle.

candidates.case_name: POWER SECURITIES CO LTD (formerly known as JUN YANG SECURITIES CO LTD) v. SIN KWOK LAM AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Chu VP, Yuen and Barma JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: The bar against reflective loss focuses on the type of loss suffered, specifically whether the shareholder's loss could be remedied if the company enforced its own rights against the wrongdoer. This applies even if the obligations to the company and shareholder are distinct and even if the company chooses not to sue.
The principle against reflective loss is not confined to claims by shareholders in their capacity as such; it can also apply to creditors or employees of a company who are not shareholders.

principles.principle_label: No avoidance by ceasing to be shareholder
principles.paraphrase_en: A shareholder whose claim is barred by the reflective loss principle cannot circumvent the bar simply by ceasing to be a shareholder.

principles.principle_label: Analysis of claim nature for reflective loss
principles.paraphrase_en: When determining if the reflective loss principle applies, it is necessary to examine the facts and nature of the claim, with the focus being on the nature or type of the loss being pursued.

The principle of res judicata in its wider sense (Henderson v Henderson) prevents a party from raising in subsequent proceedings claims or issues which they could and should have raised in earlier proceedings, as doing so amounts to an abuse of process.

relationships.target_case_name: Basab Inc v Superb Glory Holdings Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Basab Inc regarding the focus on the type of loss for the reflective loss bar.
The judgment cites the UK Court of Appeal's analysis in Marex, agreeing with its reasoning on the reflective loss principle.
The judgment cites the ratio from the Cayman Islands Court of Appeal in Primeo regarding the reflective loss bar not being avoided by ceasing to be a shareholder.
The judgment applies the principle of res judicata in the wider sense as established in Henderson v Henderson.

candidates.case_name: YUM SIU WAH v. WONG CHI SHING
judges: Before: Hon Barma JA, G Lam JA and Chow JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When assessing pre-accident earnings for loss of earnings calculations, the court should base its finding on the available evidence, such as tax returns, and apply the correct arithmetic average from that evidence rather than an unsupported round figure.

The court should not use a claimant's actual post-accident earnings from a specific role as the basis for assessing loss of earnings if it has found the claimant could and should have secured more remunerative employment.

principles.principle_label: Compensation for inability to return to exact pre-accident employment
principles.paraphrase_en: Damages for loss of future earnings can be awarded for the disadvantage suffered by being unable to return to one's exact, more remunerative pre-accident employment, even if capable of undertaking similar but less well-paid work.
A trial judge is entitled to place little or no weight on the evidence of a witness, including expert medical evidence in a discharge summary, for reasons which they provide.

candidates.case_name: CHAN KAM PUI AND YANG WEI, THE ADMINISTRATORS OF THE ESTATE OF YANG FENG, DECEASED v. DR LAM MAN HO AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Winnie Tsui in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: There is no single prescribed method for identifying or calculating loss of dependency; the court adopts a realistic and common-sense approach, requiring only that the damage be quantifiable in monetary terms.

principles.principle_label: Viable claim for lost managerial services
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim under the statutory provision is viable where the deceased's skills, effort, and energy in managing a business or assets produced economic benefits for the dependants, and those benefits are lost due to the death.

principles.principle_label: No double recovery for inherited assets
principles.paraphrase_en: A dependant who inherits the assets that generated the income stream cannot also claim damages for the loss of that income; they cannot receive both the inherited assets and compensation for lost dependency.

principles.principle_label: Replacement cost method for lost services
principles.paraphrase_en: One appropriate method for quantifying loss of dependency is to ascertain the monetary cost of replacing the services the deceased provided to the dependants.

principles.principle_label: Comparative valuation method for managed assets
principles.paraphrase_en: Another possible method for quantifying loss involves comparing the likely increase in income and capital value of assets under the deceased's management against the expected performance under the dependant's management.

relationships.description: The judgment applies principles from Cape Distribution regarding the quantification of loss of dependency and the viability of claims for lost managerial services.

relationships.target_case_name: Rix v Paramount Shopfitting Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle that whether a dependant suffered loss is to be determined at the time of death.

relationships.target_case_name: Welsh Ambulance Services NHS Trust v Williams
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle against double recovery where a dependant inherits income-generating assets.

relationships.target_case_name: Tsang Mei Ying v Lam Pak Chiu
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for using the replacement cost method to quantify loss of services.
The judgment cites Wood alongside other cases for the principle against certain types of claims.

candidates.case_name: FOOK LEE HOLDINGS LTD v. THE SECRETARY FOR TRANSPORT AND HOUSING
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP and Cheung JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Leave to appeal test for Lands Tribunal
principles.paraphrase_en: Leave to appeal from the Lands Tribunal shall not be granted unless the court is satisfied that either the appeal has a reasonable prospect of success, or there is some other reason in the interests of justice why the appeal should be heard.
A 'reasonable prospect of success' for an appeal means the prospects are more than fanciful but do not need to be shown to be probable.

Compensation under item 2(a) of the Schedule to the relevant Ordinance for the creation of a right of temporary occupation under section 15 is based on the amount of an open market rent for the claimant's interest in the land during the period of occupation.

The Pointe Gourde principle is a principle of statutory interpretation designed to explain and amplify the expression 'value' in statutory provisions for compensation for compulsory acquisition, ensuring the compensation level does not include certain elements.

relationships.target_case_name: Pointe Gourde Quarrying and Transport Co Ltd v Sub-Intendent of Crown Lands
relationships.description: The judgment cites Pointe Gourde to explain the nature and purpose of the principle as one of statutory interpretation for compensation valuation.

relationships.target_case_name: Transport for London v Spirerose Ltd (in administration)
relationships.description: The judgment cites Lord Collins's explanation from Spirerose to define the Pointe Gourde principle's purpose and application.

candidates.case_name: WANG HAIHONG AND OTHERS v. J.P. MORGAN SECURITIES (ASIA PACIFIC LTD) AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Paul Lam SC in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: A defendant may apply to strike out a claim as frivolous, vexatious, and an abuse of process on the ground that it is barred by the applicable limitation period.

principles.principle_label: Accrual of cause of action in tort
principles.paraphrase_en: A cause of action in tort accrues when the plaintiff first suffers actual damage, which is an essential element of the tort.

principles.principle_label: Damage must be actual not purely contingent
principles.paraphrase_en: For a cause of action to accrue, the damage suffered must be actual and measurable, as opposed to being purely contingent. A contingent liability may still constitute actual damage if it causes an immediate diminution in the value of an asset or creates an immediate obligation.

principles.principle_label: Measure of damages for fraudulent share inducement
principles.paraphrase_en: The measure of damages when a person is induced by fraud to purchase shares is the difference between the price paid and the real value of the shares at the time of allotment, not their market value.

A statutory cause of action requiring payment of compensation for incurred expense accrues when the obligation to pay arises, which is typically when the event enabling calculation of the amount payable occurs.

relationships.target_case_name: Kensland Realty Ltd v Tai, Tang & Chong
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from Kensland that a cause of action in tort accrues when the plaintiff first incurs actual damage.

relationships.target_case_name: Nykredit Mortgage Bank Plc v. Edward Erdman Group Ltd (No.2)
relationships.description: The judgment cites Nykredit for the proposition concerning when damage is incurred for the accrual of a cause of action.
The judgment distinguishes Maharaj, holding it turned on specific statutory wording and does not lay down a general principle applicable to the present case.
The judgment applies Forster as an example where actual damage occurred upon executing a mortgage deed, not when a contingent payment was later demanded.
The judgment applies the rule in Potts v Miller for determining the measure of damages in cases of fraudulent inducement to purchase shares.

relationships.target_case_name: HTW Valuers (Central QLD) Pty Ltd v Astonland Pty Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this Australian case as approving the principles established in Potts v Miller.

candidates.case_name: PEKING UNIVERSITY RESOURCES (HOLDINGS) COMPANY LTD v. PEKING UNIVERSITY RESOURCES GROUP CO., LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge Alan Kwong in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.paraphrase_en: The rule against reflective loss is based on the principle from Foss v Harbottle that only the company itself can seek relief for injuries done to it, not shareholders, and the avoidance of double recovery is not the primary justification for this rule.

The reflective loss principle applies when a shareholder's loss (such as diminution in share value or loss of dividends) merely reflects the company's loss for which the company has its own cause of action, preventing the shareholder from recovering such losses.

principles.principle_label: Reflective loss focus on loss type
principles.paraphrase_en: The critical question in applying the reflective loss principle is whether the shareholder's loss could be remedied if the company enforced its rights against the wrongdoer, regardless of whether the wrongdoer's obligations to company and shareholder are distinct or whether the company chooses not to sue.

A claimant cannot rely on gateway (c) for service out of jurisdiction when the anchor defendant has voluntarily submitted to the jurisdiction; the anchor defendant must be served within the jurisdiction or under another recognized gateway.

relationships.target_case_name: Waddington Ltd v Chan Chun Hoo
relationships.description: The judgment applies the reflective loss principle as affirmed by the Court of Final Appeal in this case.

relationships.target_case_name: Sevilleja Garcia v Marex Financial Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this English Supreme Court decision regarding the rationale behind the reflective loss principle.
The judgment references this case as the foundational rule that only the company can seek relief for injuries done to it.
The judgment follows the reasoning in this case regarding the requirements for gateway (c) and anchor defendants.

candidates.case_name: BONNY ACE LTD v. KR SPACE (HK) LTD
judges: Before: Master Benny Lo in Court

candidates.case_name: IP SIU CHI v. KWAN WING HANG trading as FAI HUNG CONSTRUCTION COMPANY AND OTHERS
judges: Before : Hon Chu VP, Cheung and Au JJA in Court

principles.principle_label: Assessment of damages with pre-existing conditions
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a plaintiff has a pre-existing condition likely to cause disability and loss independently of the injury sued upon, the court should account for this by: (1) adjusting general damages; (2) potentially reducing pre-trial loss of earnings if the risk is sufficiently high; and (3) making appropriate adjustments for post-trial loss of earnings.

When a plaintiff's post-accident condition is caused partly by a pre-existing condition and partly by the accident, a percentage reduction in damages is appropriate to reflect the contribution of the pre-existing condition.

principles.principle_label: Threshold for reducing pre-trial loss of earnings
principles.paraphrase_en: A pre-existing condition justifies a reduction in pre-trial loss of earnings only if the risk of that condition causing loss is more than a remote possibility; it must be a 'sufficiently high' risk.

principles.principle_label: Error in applying reduction to total incapacity period
principles.paraphrase_en: It is an error in principle to apply a percentage reduction (for a pre-existing condition) to a period of pre-trial loss of earnings where the plaintiff was totally incapacitated from work solely due to the accident-related injuries.

A plaintiff should not be penalized in costs for not transferring a claim to the District Court after a jurisdiction increase if the claim's value was genuinely disputed on complex factual grounds requiring a trial to resolve.

relationships.target_case_name: Chan Kam Hoi v Dragages et Travaux Publics
relationships.description: The present judgment follows and applies the principles from Chan Kam Hoi regarding the assessment of damages where a pre-existing condition exists.

relationships.target_case_name: Chan Kam Hoi v Dragages et Travaux Publics
relationships.description: The judgment applies the Chan Kam Hoi framework to reduce damages by 30% to account for the contribution of the plaintiff's pre-existing psychiatric condition.
The judgment applies the scenarios outlined in Liu Chun Kow Joe for handling pre-existing disabilities, finding the 30% reduction fits within the second scenario.

candidates.case_name: B K ANIL KUMAR v. J V FITNESS LTD (TRADING AS CALIFORNIA FITNESS)
judges: Before: Master Rita So in Court
