principles.paraphrase_en: In a damages assessment hearing following an admission of liability, the credibility of the plaintiff is a central issue, particularly when there are allegations of exaggeration of injuries or concealment of facts such as a return to work. The court's finding on credibility directly affects the consideration of all heads of damages claimed.

The plaintiff bears the burden of proving, on a balance of probabilities, that a specific injury (such as hearing loss) was caused by the accident in question. The mere possibility of a causal link, or the absence of pre-existing symptoms, is insufficient to discharge this burden without affirmative evidence establishing causation.

principles.principle_label: Proof of earnings for loss claims
principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff claiming loss of earnings is put to strict proof of their monthly earnings. The absence of formal records like MPF or tax returns does not relieve this burden; the plaintiff should provide other credible evidence such as payslips or confirmation of salary received.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of reasonable sick leave period
principles.paraphrase_en: The reasonable period of sick leave for the purpose of calculating damages is determined by the court based on medical evidence. This period caps the recoverable medical and travel expenses, as well as informs the calculation for pre-trial loss of earnings.

A claim for special damages (such as the cost of a hearing aid) is only allowable if the plaintiff first proves that the need for the item was caused by the defendant's tort. If causation for the underlying injury is not established, the associated special damages claim must fail.

candidates.case_name: PEKING UNIVERSITY FOUNDER GROUP COMPANY LIMITED (北大方正集團有限公司) v. NUOXI CAPITAL LIMITED (諾熙資本有限公司) (IN LIQUIDATION IN THE BRITISH VIRGIN ISLANDS)
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Allsop NPJ

principles.principle_label: Trustee's right to sue for beneficiary's loss
principles.paraphrase_en: A trustee who holds contractual rights on trust for beneficiaries has the right to sue for damages to recover losses suffered by those beneficiaries due to a breach of covenant owed to the trustee.

Where a contract-breaker has a choice of alternative methods of performing a contractual obligation, damages for breach are to be assessed based on the method of performance that would have been least onerous to the contract-breaker.

principles.principle_label: General rule on recovery of damages
principles.paraphrase_en: The general rule is that a claimant in a breach of contract action can only recover damages for loss which it has itself suffered.
An established exception to the general rule on recovery of personal loss is that a trustee can sue to recover losses suffered by the beneficiaries of the trust.

principles.principle_label: No recovery for no net loss
principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant cannot recover substantial damages for breach of contract where it has suffered no net loss as a result of the breach.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the principle that a trustee can sue to recover losses suffered by beneficiaries.
Cited as authority for the principle that damages are assessed based on the least onerous method of alternative performance available to the contract-breaker.
Cited alongside other authorities supporting the principle for assessing damages based on the least onerous alternative performance.

relationships.target_case_name: Withers v General Theatre Corp Ltd
relationships.description: Cited as further authority for the principle concerning assessment of damages based on the least burdensome mode of performance.

relationships.target_case_name: Linden Gardens Trust Ltd v Lenesta Sludge Disposal Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment references this case as an extension of the principle allowing a party to enforce contractual rights on behalf of third parties in certain circumstances.

candidates.case_name: CHAN SIU LUNG v. YIP KAM SHUI t/a YING TUNG ENGINEERING CO AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Kwan Ag CJHC, Yuen JA and G Lam JA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: When assessing pre-trial loss of earnings, the court must consider whether the plaintiff could realistically resume their pre-accident job at the end of a reasonable sick leave period, or whether they could only perform lighter work due to residual impairments.

principles.principle_label: Credibility and objective evidence in impairment assessment
principles.paraphrase_en: Subjective complaints of residual impairment (like headaches or dizziness) require objective evidence for support. In the absence of such evidence, the court may find the plaintiff unreliable and may attribute symptoms to malingering or exaggeration.

When calculating partial loss of earnings, the deduction from the pre-accident income should reflect the income the plaintiff could realistically earn in their impaired state, not necessarily the income from an alternative lighter job they actually took.

principles.principle_label: Interrelation of physical and psychiatric symptoms
principles.paraphrase_en: The severity of a plaintiff's psychiatric symptoms may be linked to their perception of their physical impairment. If the physical condition is found to be less severe than claimed, this may indicate the psychiatric symptoms are also milder.

Pre-trial loss of earnings generally ends when the plaintiff should reasonably have been able to resume their pre-accident employment, which may be at the expiry of sick leave plus a reasonable job-search period.

candidates.case_name: ROCO INVESTMENT LTD AND ANOTHER v. RICH GAINER INTERNAITONAL LTD
judges: Before: Master Ebony Ling in Open Court

candidates.case_name: TONG WAI YEE WINNIE v. SECRETARY FOR EDUCATION AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon Chu VP, Yuen and Chow JJA

principles.principle_label: Judicial review grounds for administrative decisions
principles.paraphrase_en: An administrative decision can be quashed if it is found to be irrational, meaning the reasons given for the decision do not align with the evidence subsequently presented, or if the decision-making process involved an overly rigid application of a policy.

principles.principle_label: Collateral contract in public sector recruitment
principles.paraphrase_en: A collateral contract can arise from a public sector recruitment process, whereby the public body implicitly promises to consider an applicant's application alongside others, and a breach of this promise can give rise to a claim for damages.

An appellate court will only interfere with a trial judge's assessment of damages if the judge made a serious error, acted on a wrong principle of law, or awarded an amount outside the generous range open to the judge based on the facts.

principles.principle_label: Irrelevance of post-judgment events to damages
principles.paraphrase_en: Events occurring after the judgment on liability are not relevant and cannot be taken into account when assessing damages; the assessment must be based solely on the applicable legal principles at the time of breach.

principles.principle_label: Causation for future loss of earnings
principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant must establish a sufficient causal link between the wrongful act and any claimed future loss of earnings; the claim fails where numerous other factors could affect an employer's future employment decisions.

relationships.target_case_name: Tong Siu Wai v Poon Wing Fu
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the standard from Tong Siu Wai for when an appellate court can interfere with a trial judge's assessment of damages.

candidates.case_name: BONNY ACE LTD v. THE ATHLETIC CO., LTD
judges: Before: Master Maurice Lam in Court (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Recovery of government rates upon wrongful repudiation
principles.paraphrase_en: A landlord can recover loss of government rates as damages when a tenant wrongfully repudiates the tenancy, provided the calculation is supported by official demands and fairly applies any government concessions.

principles.principle_label: Recovery of licence fees for unexpired term
principles.paraphrase_en: A licensor is entitled to recover licence fees for the unexpired period of a licence agreement following the licensee's breach, where the agreement grants termination rights only to the licensor and the clause is not unreasonable.
A witness's credibility is assessed based on the plausibility and consistency of their evidence with documentary records, and their demeanor while testifying.

principles.principle_label: Reasonableness of costs in assessment hearings
principles.paraphrase_en: In assessment of damages hearings, the time claimed for preparing documents must be justified and proportionate to the complexity of the case and the length of the hearing.

candidates.case_name: TAM YUK CHING AND OTHERS v. HOOSENALLY & NEO (a firm) AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Mr Recorder Eugene Fung SC in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: No reflective loss principle engagement threshold
principles.paraphrase_en: The no reflective loss principle is an exclusionary rule that denies a plaintiff a right to sue. It only becomes relevant for consideration if the plaintiff has first established a prima facie right to sue. If no such right exists, the principle is not engaged.

principles.principle_label: Focus of no reflective loss principle
principles.paraphrase_en: The central consideration of the no reflective loss principle is not whether a personal wrong was done to the plaintiff, but whether the loss the plaintiff seeks to recover would be compensated if the company (in which the plaintiff has an interest) successfully enforced its own rights against the wrongdoer.

principles.principle_label: Binding precedent in Hong Kong courts
principles.paraphrase_en: The Hong Kong Court of First Instance is bound by the ratio decidendi of decisions from the Hong Kong Court of Appeal and the Court of Final Appeal. Decisions from foreign jurisdictions, such as the UK Supreme Court, do not bind Hong Kong courts and do not automatically overrule established Hong Kong appellate authority.

principles.principle_label: Strike-out application test for reflective loss
principles.paraphrase_en: On a strike-out application concerning the no reflective loss principle, the court must base its decision solely on the pleaded facts. The court must determine whether, based on those pleadings, the loss appears to be one that would be made good if the company enforced its rights.

principles.principle_label: Standard for refusing strike-out on reflective loss
principles.paraphrase_en: At the strike-out stage, a claim should not be struck out based on the no reflective loss principle if it is not 'plain and obvious' that the principle bars the claim. Any reasonable doubt must be resolved in favour of the plaintiff, allowing the claim to proceed to trial.

relationships.target_case_name: Wah Nam Group Ltd v Roderick John Sutton
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the definition of the no reflective loss principle as an exclusionary rule.

relationships.target_case_name: Topping Chance Development Ltd v CCIF CPA Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case alongside Wah Nam for the same proposition regarding the nature of the no reflective loss principle.

relationships.target_case_name: Landune International Ltd v Cheung Chung Leung
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this Court of Appeal case regarding the focus of the reflective loss rule.
The judgment rejects the sweeping submission that this UK Supreme Court decision overrules binding Hong Kong authority on reflective loss.
The judgment applies the observations from this case regarding the court's approach on a strike-out application concerning reflective loss.

candidates.case_name: HKSAR v. KOFI FRIMPONG
judges: Before: Hon Zervos JA in Court

principles.principle_label: Right to appeal determination by full court
principles.paraphrase_en: If a single judge refuses an application to exercise specified powers (like granting leave to appeal), the appellant is entitled to have that application determined by the full Court of Appeal.
A convicted person has a right to appeal to the Court of Appeal, but generally requires leave unless the appeal is on a point of law alone or the trial judge grants a certificate.

principles.principle_label: Single judge's power on leave applications
principles.paraphrase_en: A single judge of the Court of Appeal or Court of First Instance may hear an application for leave to appeal and has the same power as the full Court of Appeal to grant such leave.

principles.principle_label: Purpose of loss of time orders
principles.paraphrase_en: The power to order loss of time serves to deter unmeritorious applications, which waste judicial resources and cause delays to meritorious cases, potentially causing injustice to parties (especially those in custody).

candidates.case_name: YEUNG PAK KWAN as administrator of the estate of LAM FAN, deceased v. CHEN DECANG
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge H. Au-Yeung in Court

principles.principle_label: Dependency claim for loss of services
principles.paraphrase_en: A claim for loss of dependency can include the value of gratuitous services provided by the deceased, and the fact that the dependants did not actually incur expenses to replace those services is not a bar to the claim.

For a claim under fatal accident legislation, it is not necessary that the deceased was actually earning or contributing support at the date of death, provided the dependant had a reasonable expectation of future pecuniary benefit from the deceased's continued life.

principles.principle_label: Loss of accumulation of wealth - legislative intent
principles.paraphrase_en: The legislative intent behind a statutory provision for loss of accumulation of wealth is to prevent a windfall to the deceased's estate, and the court must determine whether the deceased would have achieved an accumulation of wealth by the date of their natural death.

principles.principle_label: Quantifying loss of accumulation of wealth
principles.paraphrase_en: Where the court is satisfied that an accumulation of wealth would have occurred but there is no evidential basis to quantify it precisely, a global award is an appropriate method of assessment.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle from this case that the lack of actual expense to replace gratuitous services is not a bar to a dependency claim.

relationships.target_case_name: Cheng Shiu Ling & Another v. Hui Wai Hung & Another
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case as authority for the principle that prospective loss can be considered in assessing dependency damages.

relationships.target_case_name: Taff Vale Railway Company v. Jenkins
relationships.description: The judgment cites this House of Lords authority for the principle that a reasonable expectation of future pecuniary benefit suffices for a dependency claim.

relationships.target_case_name: Piggott v. Fancy Wood Products Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment cites this unreported case as an example where a future career and intention to assist parents supported a claim for loss of dependency.
The judgment follows the reasoning in this case regarding the legislative intent and test for awarding damages for loss of accumulation of wealth.

relationships.target_case_name: Wong King Tai, the Administratrix of Wong Tak Luk v Lau Ka Kui
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for its discussion on the difficulty of quantifying loss of accumulation of wealth and the appropriateness of a global award.

candidates.case_name: WONG SIU CHI v. TAM KUT YU
judges: Before: Master Eleanor Yeung in Court

candidates.case_name: CHOI CHING WAI v. HU AIJIAO
judges: Before: Master Y. H. Chan in Court

candidates.case_name: LEUNG CHI KIN v. HUNG KA FAI t/a SIGN SQUARE
judges: Before: Master Matthew Leung in Court

candidates.case_name: BONNY ACE LTD v. MING YUE CHINESE CUISINE LTD
judges: Before: Master Benny Lo in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Expenses incurred as a consequence of a breach of contract are recoverable if they were reasonably incurred for the purpose of mitigating loss or addressing the situation created by the breach.
Interest may be awarded on a successful claim where the court finds such an award to be reasonable.

candidates.case_name: 廈門新景地集團有限公司 formerly known as 廈門市鑫新景地房地產有限公司 v. ETON PROPERTIES LTD AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Mimmie Chan J in Chambers (Open to Public)

principles.principle_label: Governing law for enforcement of arbitration award
principles.paraphrase_en: The law governing the enforcement of an arbitration award, including a common law action for breach of the implied promise to honour that award, is the lex fori (the law of the enforcing court).

principles.principle_label: Nature of implied promise to honour award
principles.paraphrase_en: The implied promise to honour an arbitration award constitutes a separate and distinct contractual obligation from the obligations under the original underlying contract.
The objective of awarding damages for breach of contract is to place the injured party, as far as money can, in the same position they would have been in had the contract been performed.
The required level of precision when pleading a head of damage is determined by what is necessary to provide a fair and sufficient indication of the case to be met by the opposing party.

Once an arbitral tribunal has fully adjudicated a case and issued its final award, it becomes functus officio and has no jurisdiction to issue further rulings or directions on how the award should be performed.

relationships.description: The judgment applies the classic principle from this case regarding the objective of damages for breach of contract.

relationships.target_case_name: Lam Sik Ying v Lam Sik Shi
relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle governing the required precision in pleading a head of damage.

relationships.target_case_name: D’Almeida Araujo Lda v Sir Frederick Becker & Co Ltd
relationships.description: The judgment distinguishes or does not apply the conflict of laws principles discussed in this case, finding them inapplicable to the present enforcement context.

candidates.case_name: SHEK PING CHEUNG DANIEL AND OTHERS v. PRINCE WINNER LTD
judges: Before: Master Connie Lee in Court

candidates.case_name: DOUBLE DRAGON DEVELOPMENT LTD v. CHEUNG KAM LAI
judges: Before: Master B. Mak in Court

candidates.case_name: CHIU SUI CHING, the administrator of the estate of LEUNG HO TIN, deceased v. CHENG KWAI HUNG t/a HANG NAGI WORKS AND OTHERS
judges: Before: Hon Marlene Ng J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: Interest on bereavement damages should be calculated at 8% per annum from the date of death, but the calculation period ends at the date of the assessment hearing or is truncated by any interim payment made, not at the date of the eventual court award.

principles.principle_label: Treatment of interim payments for interest calculation
principles.paraphrase_en: An interim payment is treated first as compensation for loss and expense incurred up to the date of that payment. For special damages, interest is payable on the full amount until the interim payment reduces it, after which interest runs only on the outstanding balance.
The award of interest on damages is a discretionary matter, and courts are generally reluctant to provide broad guidance because the factual circumstances of each case can vary significantly.

relationships.description: The judgment cites Bristow for the proposition that interest calculation where interim payments have been made is discretionary and fact-specific.
The judgment applies the principle from Bristow that an interim payment first compensates for loss incurred up to the payment date for the purpose of calculating interest.

candidates.case_name: NG YUN TING v. CHAN MAN WAI RAYMOND
judges: Before: Master Matthew Leung in Court

principles.principle_label: Effect of criminal conviction in civil proceedings
principles.paraphrase_en: In a personal injury claim arising from a traffic accident, the plaintiff may rely on the defendant's prior criminal conviction for dangerous driving stemming from the same incident as evidence supporting the claim of negligence.

principles.principle_label: Proceeding with assessment in absence of defendant
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may proceed with an assessment of damages in the defendant's absence if satisfied that proper notice of the hearing was given to the defendant and the defendant's non-attendance is a voluntary choice.

principles.principle_label: Adoption of witness statement as evidence-in-chief
principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff in a civil damages assessment may adopt a previously filed witness statement as their evidence-in-chief during the hearing.

candidates.case_name: GURUNG MOTI KUMARI v. NEW GLORY (H.K.) LTD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Timon Shum in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A court should not automatically reject a claimant's complaint of pain based solely on ambiguous or potentially unreliable entries in medical reports, especially when the meaning of those entries is unclear.
The court may accept the views of one medical expert over another when those views are found to be more consistent, complete, and capable of explaining the symptoms complained of by the claimant.

When assessing a claimant's ability to return to pre-accident employment, the court must consider the full range of job duties, not just an isolated task. The ability to perform one specific duty does not necessarily mean the claimant can resume all required duties.

principles.principle_label: Guiding principle for loss of earning capacity
principles.paraphrase_en: The award for loss of earning capacity is guided by the principle established in the case of Moeliker v A Reyrolle & Co Ltd.

relationships.target_case_name: Moeliker v A Reyrolle & Co Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment states that the guiding principle for making an award for loss of earning capacity was established in this case.

candidates.case_name: WIDELAND INTERNATIONAL LTD v. Crafa Hotel Management Ltd AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Master Reuden Lai in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Damages for breach of contract aim to compensate the claimant for the actual loss suffered as a result of the breach, subject to the claimant's duty to mitigate and the remoteness rule.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of loss for vacant period
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a breach of a lease leaves premises vacant, the claimant is entitled to recover the contractual rent for the period of vacancy pending the finding of a new tenant, as a direct loss from the breach.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of loss for re-letting at lower rent
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a lease is breached and the premises are re-let at a lower market rent, the claimant is entitled to recover the difference between the original contractual rent and the new market rent for the remainder of the original lease term.

principles.principle_label: Recovery of ancillary charges during vacancy
principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant can recover ancillary contractual charges (like management fees) and statutory charges (like government rates) for the period the premises were vacant due to the breach, as these are losses flowing directly from the breach.

principles.principle_label: Limitation on summary assessment of costs
principles.paraphrase_en: Where a judgment has ordered costs to be taxed on an indemnity basis, a judge conducting a damages assessment cannot perform a summary assessment of those costs; the claimant must proceed to formal taxation.

relationships.description: The judgment cites the case for the foundational principle that contractual damages aim to place the injured party in the position they would have been in had the contract been performed.

candidates.case_name: LO WAI KEUNG v. HANNOVER RÜCK SE
judges: Before: Hon K Yeung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The jurisdiction of the Labour Tribunal is determined at the time the claim is filed, whereas the appropriate forum for trial is assessed later when issues become clear or circumstances change after filing.

candidates.case_name: CHIU YU WUT AND ANOTHER v. CHIU PO WAI DOROTHY
judges: Before: Master Y. H. Chan in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Where a party adduces evidence that is not challenged by the opposing party, and the opposing party neither presents contradictory evidence nor attends the hearing, the court may accept and rely on that evidence if it finds it straightforward, credible, and reliable.

principles.principle_label: Reliance on expert evidence for damages
principles.paraphrase_en: The court may accept and act upon expert evidence in assessing damages, particularly where the expert provides a logical and sensible opinion with sufficient breakdowns and details, making the assessed sum reasonable and recoverable.

candidates.case_name: MA KWAN HO v. TSENG KIN MAN
judges: Before: Master Maurice Lam in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff can recover a policy excess paid to a repairer as compensable loss, provided the payment is supported by documentary evidence such as a receipt.
Additional travel expenses incurred due to an incident are in principle recoverable as damages, but the claimant must prove an actual net loss, not merely incurred expenses offset by savings.

Costs arising purely from a pre-existing contractual obligation (like tenancy agreement fees) that would have been incurred regardless of the incident do not constitute a recoverable loss in tort, even if perceived as 'wasted'.

When assessing costs, the court may apply a broad-brush approach to reduce claimed amounts to a reasonable sum, considering factors like the actual hearing duration, case complexity, and nature of the work done.

candidates.case_name: AMPOFO MOSEN ANTI v. NG CHING MEI
judges: Before: His Honour Judge Andrew Li in Court

principles.principle_label: Loss of earning capacity for unlawful workers
principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff who was not in lawful employment at the time of the incident due to their immigration status (such as being an asylum seeker or refugee) cannot establish a claim for loss of future earning capacity, as there is no evidential basis for such an award.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for future earnings
principles.paraphrase_en: A claimant asserting a specific level of future earnings must provide documentary proof to support that assertion; the court cannot make an award based on unsupported assertions.

relationships.description: The present judgment adopts the same reasoning from Kumara Debanama regarding the inability to claim loss of earning capacity when the plaintiff was not lawfully employed.
The court applies the principle from Kumara Debanama that a person without lawful employment status cannot establish a claim for loss of future earning capacity.

candidates.case_name: TOP VICTORY (ASIA) LTD v. KONG SHUI SUN AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge S P Yip in Court

principles.principle_label: Knowledge of defect triggers duty to act
principles.paraphrase_en: Once a property owner receives a credible report identifying their property as the source of a nuisance (like water seepage) affecting a neighbor, they can no longer deny knowledge of the source and have a duty to take prompt action to investigate and repair.

principles.principle_label: Delay in remedying known defect constitutes breach of duty
principles.paraphrase_en: A property owner who, after becoming aware their property is the source of a problem affecting another, unreasonably delays in investigating and carrying out necessary repairs, is in breach of their legal duty.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof for failure to mitigate loss
principles.paraphrase_en: The legal burden of proving that a plaintiff failed to take reasonable steps to mitigate their loss rests on the defendant who raises that plea.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of expert evidence on quantum
principles.paraphrase_en: In assessing conflicting expert evidence on the quantum of damages (such as reinstatement costs), the court will prefer the evidence that is more meticulous, reasonable, and professional.
For damages to be recoverable, the proposed remedial or reinstatement works must be shown to be both reasonable and necessary in the circumstances.

relationships.target_case_name: Yan Wing Fai Rick & Another v Century One Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from that case that delay in acting upon knowledge of a defect constitutes a breach of duty.

candidates.case_name: YU YINING v. HMVOD LTD
judges: Before: Deputy High Court Judge MK Liu in Court

candidates.case_name: SIT KAN TAT v. WORLD-CLASS TALENT LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Hon G Lam JA and Leung J in Court

principles.principle_label: Breach of statutory and common law safety duties
principles.paraphrase_en: An employer breaches its statutory duties under occupational safety legislation and its common law duties if it fails to take reasonably practicable measures to ensure safe access and egress, including by failing to notice and remove hazards, failing to conduct adequate inspections during busy periods, and failing to set a proper example or enforce safe practices.

A defendant is not liable for losses caused by an independent, supervening, non-tortious event (such as a pandemic) that is unrelated to the defendant's negligence. However, the defendant retains liability if the loss was caused by the plaintiff's disability combined with other factors, and the defendant fails to prove the same loss would have occurred even without the accident.

principles.principle_label: Assessment of residual disability and employability
principles.paraphrase_en: A judge may properly find that a plaintiff has suffered a reduction in employability due to a permanent impairment, even if the judge rejects some of the plaintiff's specific evidence about the extent of the disability and acknowledges that the condition may improve over time without full recovery.

principles.principle_label: Appellate review of contributory negligence percentage
principles.paraphrase_en: An appellate court will not interfere with a trial judge's assessment of the percentage of contributory negligence unless it is outside the range reasonably open to the judge on the facts. Each case is fact-specific, and previous cases do not establish a legal principle that contributory negligence must exceed a specific threshold (such as 50%).

candidates.case_name: TACHING PETROLEUM CO, LTD v. MEYER ALUMINIUM LTD
judges: Before: Hon Au-Yeung J in Chambers

principles.principle_label: Forum for expert evidence on damages
principles.paraphrase_en: A party must apply for expert evidence concerning loss and damages in the correct forum. If a claim element (like a damages claim) has been transferred to a specialist tribunal, an application for related expert evidence must be made to that tribunal, not the court from which the matter was transferred.

The defendant bears the burden of providing specific details regarding the amount claimed as a set-off or the method used to calculate that amount. A mere assertion of a set-off without such particulars is insufficient.

principles.principle_label: Particulars for set-off in summary judgment
principles.paraphrase_en: In a summary judgment application, a bare defence of set-off that lacks particulars is not enough to establish a triable issue that would defeat the application for judgment.

relationships.target_case_name: 浙江省鐵投國際貿易有限公司 v HK Zexin Resources Co Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from this case that a defendant must particularize a set-off defence in a summary judgment context.

relationships.target_case_name: Legend Pacific Ltd v Ng Choi Chi
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from this case that a bare set-off defence is insufficient to create a triable issue for summary judgment.

candidates.case_name: LIN HO PIN AND OTHERS v. JAMES TING-YEH YANG
judges: Before: Hon Fung J in Chambers

principles.paraphrase_en: The court is not obliged to accept sworn evidence supporting a defense as probably accurate if it is inconsistent with contemporaneous documents, inherently implausible, or contradicted by other compelling evidence.

principles.principle_label: Accrual of cause of action for indemnity
principles.paraphrase_en: A party induced by misleading conduct to give an indemnity does not suffer damage (and thus a cause of action does not accrue) merely upon entering the indemnity agreement, but only when called upon to meet the indemnity.

principles.principle_label: Accrual of action for contingent liability
principles.paraphrase_en: A contingent liability, standing alone, does not constitute actionable damage until the contingency occurs; no cause of action accrues until an actual claim is made or loss is sustained.

principles.principle_label: Loss of chance as immediate damage
principles.paraphrase_en: Where negligent advice causes a party to lose the chance of obtaining a better deal, that loss of chance constitutes actual, measurable damage at the time the advice is acted upon, even if the extent of loss is unclear until later.

principles.principle_label: Measure and timing of loss in negligence
principles.paraphrase_en: The measure of loss in negligence is a comparison between the plaintiff's position had the duty been fulfilled and the actual position; damage accrues when measurable, relevant loss is sustained, which may be immediate or later depending on the facts.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that the court must critically assess sworn evidence supporting a defense if it is inconsistent with documents or implausible.

relationships.target_case_name: Wardley Australia Ltd v The State of Western Australia
relationships.description: The judgment applies the principle that damage for an indemnity arises not upon agreement but when the indemnity is called upon.
The judgment follows the holding that a cause of action for a contingent liability accrues only when a claim is actually made, not when the liability is merely exposed.
The judgment cites this case for the approach to determining when measurable damage occurs in a 'no transaction' scenario.
The judgment cites this case as an example where damage accrued immediately upon a solicitor's failure to register a caution, not later when the loss materialized.
The judgment applies the principle that the measure of loss must be identified and that loss may accrue at different stages depending on the facts.

candidates.case_name: YU CHUN KIT v. WONG WING YAU formerly t/a VIEWBOND CARGO SERVICE CO AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Kay Seto in Court

principles.principle_label: Non-discharge of fraudulent debts in bankruptcy
principles.paraphrase_en: A debt incurred through fraud or fraudulent breach of trust is not released by an order of discharge in bankruptcy.
The court has discretion to order costs against a defendant even when the plaintiff has settled with another party, but may consider limiting liability based on the circumstances.
A party may be deprived of full costs recovery if they pursue unsubstantiated claims or raise unsupported legal challenges that prolong proceedings.

relationships.description: The judgment references this case in discussing the historical basis for non-discharge of fraudulent debts in bankruptcy.
The judgment distinguishes this case as concerning a bankrupt's right of action as plaintiff rather than locus standi as a defendant.

relationships.target_case_name: Lee Ting Lam v Leung Kam Ming
relationships.description: Cited for the definition of the 'Serious Injury' category in personal injury assessments.

relationships.target_case_name: David John Slater v Commissioner of Police
relationships.description: Referenced for establishing starting points for PSLA awards in the Serious Injury category.

candidates.case_name: CHEUNG HON YU v. CHUN LEE ENGINEERING CO LTD AND ANOTHER
judges: Before: Master Roy Yu in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: Sick leave should be terminated when a medical condition becomes static or stable, and does not need to continue until full recovery. It is common for middle-aged workers to continue employment while experiencing some degree of back pain, especially when the pain lacks a compensable cause.
When assessing causation for damages, the effect of a relevant pre-existing condition must be considered under the 'crumbling skull' principle.

relationships.description: The present judgment reiterates principles stated in paragraph 71 of Yu Wai Kan regarding the crumbling skull principle.

candidates.case_name: YIP YING CHING v. LAPCO SERVICE LTD
judges: Before: Deputy District Judge Timon Shum in Court

principles.principle_label: Assessment of claimant credibility in injury claims
principles.paraphrase_en: A court may find a claimant's evidence unreliable if their subjective complaints are inconsistent with objective medical findings, if they give shifting or evasive testimony, and if they deny documented medical records without reasonable explanation.

An injury is not considered causally related to an accident if it is not documented in the initial medical reports from treatment sought immediately after the incident and only emerges significantly later without a credible explanation linking it to the accident.

Sick leave certificates granted based on a patient's subjective complaints alone are not a safe basis for determining the appropriate period of incapacity, especially where the claimant's credibility is in doubt.

principles.principle_label: Entitlement period for loss of earnings
principles.paraphrase_en: The period for which loss of earnings is compensable is determined by when the claimant's condition stabilizes, as supported by medical opinion, not necessarily by the total period of sick leave granted.

principles.principle_label: Award for loss of earning capacity
principles.paraphrase_en: An award for loss of earning capacity requires a finding of a substantial or real risk that the claimant will lose their job in the future due to their injury. No such award is made if the claimant chooses not to work despite being physically able, or if the employment was likely to end for reasons unrelated to the injury.

relationships.target_case_name: Moeliker v A Reyrolle & Co Ltd
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the guiding principle from Moeliker for making an award for loss of earning capacity.

candidates.case_name: PERUSAHAAN PERSEROAN (PERSERO) PT PERTAMINA v. TREVASKIS LIMITED; and all other persons claiming or being entitled to claim damages arising from a collision between “STAR CENTURION” and “ANTEA”, which occurred on or about 13 January 2019 off Horsburgh Light House, South China Sea
judges: Before: Chief Justice Cheung, Mr Justice Ribeiro PJ, Mr Justice Fok PJ, Mr Justice Lam PJ and Mr Justice Keane NPJ

principles.paraphrase_en: The maxim that general provisions do not override specific ones is not a rigid rule but reflects common sense and contextual interpretation. Where a general provision would negate a special provision on the same subject, the special provision operates as an exception to the general.

Legislative instruments must be interpreted as a coherent whole. The principle that specific provisions prevail over general ones is an aspect of this broader requirement to give effect to the instrument's overall coherence.

principles.principle_label: Rejection of English law preconceptions in Convention interpretation
principles.paraphrase_en: When interpreting the Convention, certain English law distinctions (such as the historical distinction between limitable tort claims and non-limitable debt claims under old UK shipping law) must be disregarded.

An interpretation of the Convention that would lead to an incoherent result—such as allowing a state to exclude limitation for wreck removal claims under one article while permitting limitation for the same expense under another—should be rejected.

principles.principle_label: Use of travaux préparatoires as supplementary means
principles.paraphrase_en: The preparatory works of a convention may be used as a supplementary means of interpretation, particularly in cases involving genuinely feasible alternative interpretations, to confirm the ordinary meaning derived from context, object, and purpose.

relationships.description: Cites Lord Steyn's view on using travaux préparatoires as a supplementary means of interpretation in cases of feasible alternative interpretations.
Cites the exposition that a special provision must be read as a proviso to a general provision where the general would otherwise neutralize it.

relationships.target_case_name: Kwok Cheuk Kin v Director of Lands (No 2)
relationships.description: Applies the principle from this case that the specific-over-general rule is an aspect of interpreting legislative instruments as a coherent whole.
Notes that the majority judges applied the principle of coherent interpretation rather than expressly invoking the Generalia Maxim.

relationships.target_case_name: A.R. Appelqvist A/B v The Cyprian Coast (Owners) (The Arabert)
relationships.description: Indicates that the answer given in this case regarding limitability of claims must be disregarded when interpreting the Convention.

relationships.target_case_name: Effort Shipping Co Ltd v Linden Management SA (The Giannis NK)
relationships.description: Cites Lord Steyn's acceptance that travaux préparatoires may be used as a supplementary means of interpretation in appropriate cases.

candidates.case_name: SO SIN YING v. J. V. FITNESS LTD
judges: Before: Hon Kwan VP, Au and G Lam JJA in Court

principles.paraphrase_en: A judge is entitled to prefer one expert's opinion over another when assessing witness credibility and medical evidence, particularly when evaluating whether a claimant's symptoms are genuine or exaggerated.

principles.principle_label: Basis for rejecting symptom exaggeration claims
principles.paraphrase_en: A finding that physical symptoms are not fabricated can be properly based on contemporaneous medical documentation from treating doctors shortly after the injury, supported by accepted psychiatric expert opinion linking those symptoms to psychiatric conditions caused by the injury.

principles.principle_label: Calculation method for loss of earnings
principles.paraphrase_en: Loss of earnings should be computed by comparing the difference between post-tax earnings in the hypothetical scenario where the injury did not occur and the claimant's actual post-tax earnings.

principles.principle_label: Consideration of career progression in earnings assessment
principles.paraphrase_en: When assessing notional earnings, it is reasonable for a judge to consider that an experienced professional with an established client base would not require a significant transition period to reach previous earning levels when starting new employment or freelance work.

principles.principle_label: Costs allocation for partially successful appeals
principles.paraphrase_en: When an appellant succeeds on only some grounds of appeal, the court may make a proportionate costs order that reflects the partial success, particularly where reasonable grounds existed to pursue the unsuccessful aspects of the appeal.

candidates.case_name: FUNG YAT MING v. DISH JOCKEY (HONG KONG) LTD
judges: Before: Master Matthew Leung in Court

principles.principle_label: Loss of earnings despite higher post-accident income
principles.paraphrase_en: A plaintiff who earns more in post-accident employment may still claim loss of earnings if they cannot earn the full salary a fit person could earn in that job due to residual disability from the accident.

relationships.target_case_name: Li Wan Choi v Choi Wan Hing & Another
relationships.description: The present judgment applies the principle from Li Wan Choi regarding claiming loss of earnings when a plaintiff earns more post-accident but suffers residual disability.

candidates.case_name: JASPAL SINGH v. TORTURE CLAIMS APPEAL BOARD / NON-REFOULEMENT CLAIMS PETITION OFFICE
judges: Order by Deputy High Court Judge To :

principles.paraphrase_en: The court's role in judicial review of non-refoulement decisions is not to reassess claims de novo but to scrutinize for errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality, as primary fact-finding rests with the Director and Board.

principles.principle_label: Legal assistance standard in non-refoulement procedures
principles.paraphrase_en: The high standard of fairness requires adequate legal assistance for non-refoulement claimants, but does not mandate legal representation throughout the entire process.

principles.principle_label: Fugitive from justice vs persecution victim
principles.paraphrase_en: A person fleeing punishment for an offence (fugitive from justice) is not entitled to non-refoulement protection, unlike a person fleeing persecution who is a victim of injustice.

principles.principle_label: Burden of proof in non-refoulement claims
principles.paraphrase_en: The claimant bears the burden to establish, even to a low standard, a threat of harm reaching the required severity, and past absence of such harm may undermine a future risk claim.

relationships.description: The judgment cites this case for the principle that judicial review intervention is limited to errors of law, procedural unfairness, or irrationality.
The judgment applies the principle from this case that legal assistance is not required throughout all non-refoulement procedures.
The judgment applies the principle from this case regarding the limited requirement for legal assistance in non-refoulement procedures.
The judgment applies the principle from this case concerning the scope of legal assistance in non-refoulement claims.
The judgment applies the principle from this case about legal representation not being mandatory throughout non-refoulement procedures.
The judgment applies the principle from this case regarding the high standard of fairness not requiring continuous legal assistance.
